In the next 12-24 months, I expect SEBI to expand the BRSR Core assurance perimeter beyond the top 250 companies, potentially to the top 500 or 1,000 listed entities, and to introduce reasonable assurance (not just limited assurance) requirements for the largest companies. Simultaneously, MCA's work on India's sustainability accounting standards - informed by ISSB - will create a third layer of data demand. Companies that have not invested in structured ESG data management platforms by FY2027 will face a compounding liability: assurance qualifications, investor engagement deficits, and supply chain disqualification from EU-linked value chains. The Indian ESG software market - currently fragmented between global platforms, mid-size Indian providers, and in-house Excel models - is ripe for the kind of institutional capital consolidation that the Novisto investment exemplifies. Indian PE and growth investors should take note: the next ESG infrastructure investment story will very likely be made in India.

title: Pentagreen's $800M GIP Close: What the FAST-P Blended Finance Architecture Means for India's Transition Infrastructure Capital Gap
summary: Pentagreen Capital - the HSBC and Temasek-backed debt financing platform - has reached an $800 million second close for its Green Investments Partnership (GIP), a blended finance vehicle operating under Singapore's MAS-anchored Financing Asia's Transition Partnership (FAST-P). Critically for Indian

sections.summary: Pentagreen Capital - the HSBC and Temasek-backed debt financing platform - has reached an $800 million second close for its Green Investments Partnership (GIP), a blended finance vehicle operating under Singapore's MAS-anchored Financing Asia's Transition Partnership (FAST-P). Critically for Indian boards and CFOs, GIP's mandate explicitly covers South Asia alongside Southeast Asia, deploying debt capital into renewable energy, EV infrastructure, sustainable transport, and water and waste management - sectors where India's transition project pipeline is enormous but commercial financing remains structurally underpenetrated. This signals that institutional-grade blended finance capital is moving toward India's transition infrastructure at scale, with direct implications for how Indian project developers, listed infrastructure companies, and their lenders should position themselves for access. Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/hsbc-temasek-backed-pentagreen-raises-800-million-for-asia-focused-green-transition-fund/) covers the second close announcement in full.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today, the GIP fund has grown from its September 2025 first close of $510 million to $800 million at second close, with new institutional lenders DBS Bank and Cathay United Bank entering senior tranche positions - a meaningful signal of expanding commercial bank appetite for blended finance structures in the Asian transition space. The fund's tiered capital architecture - separating junior (concessional/philanthropic) and senior (commercial) tranches - is specifically designed to de-risk the 'marginally bankable' category of climate infrastructure that traditional project finance cannot absorb at current risk-adjusted return thresholds. This is precisely where India's transition pipeline sits: sub-investment-grade renewable projects in Tier 2 and Tier 3 markets, early-stage EV charging networks, distributed water treatment, and industrial energy transition projects that lack the balance sheet strength or offtake certainty to attract pure commercial debt. The India-specific consequence is structural, not incidental. India faces a documented climate finance gap estimated at over $170 billion annually through 2030 to meet its NDC commitments under the Paris Agreement, including its 500 GW non-fossil capacity target and energy intensity reduction goals. Domestic DFIs - IREDA, REC Ltd, PFC - have expanded their books aggressively, but their capital is concentrated in large-scale, utility-grade renewable projects with sovereign or near-sovereign credit profiles. The 'missing middle' - mid-market transition projects, industrial decarbonisation, green mobility, climate-resilient water infrastructure - remains severely underfunded. GIP's mandate, architecture, and sectoral focus map directly onto this gap. Indian project developers, green infrastructure SPVs, and transition-focused companies that can meet GIP's ESG and technical eligibility criteria now have a credible new debt capital pathway, provided they can demonstrate bankable environmental integrity under frameworks that GIP's LP base - which includes development finance institutions and institutional asset managers - will require. For Indian listed companies, this matters at the strategic level. Access to blended finance pools like GIP increasingly requires alignment with internationally recognised disclosure standards. GIP's LP base, including development finance institutions linked to MAS's FAST-P architecture, will apply ESG due diligence screens consistent with IFC Performance Standards, TCFD-aligned climate risk disclosure, and emerging ISSB IFRS S1/S2 requirements. Indian companies seeking to tap this capital - or seeking co-financing from GIP counterparties - will face diligence requirements that go substantially beyond current BRSR Core thresholds. The fund's DBS participation is also notable: DBS has explicitly aligned its own sustainable finance taxonomy with MAS's taxonomy and Singapore Green Finance standards, meaning project eligibility for GIP debt will be assessed against criteria that Indian companies may need to prepare for in advance.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian renewable energy developers - utility scale and mid-market - seeking project debt for solar, wind, storage, and hybrid assets that fall below investment-grade thresholds without credit enhancement EV infrastructure companies and fleet electrification platforms building out charging networks and electric bus/truck fleets across Indian Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities Water and waste management infrastructure developers - including those operating under CPCB EPR frameworks for plastic and municipal solid waste - where blended finance can bridge the viability gap Listed Indian infrastructure companies (BSE 500, NSE-listed) with transition project pipelines that require long-tenor, competitively priced debt and are currently reliant on domestic DFI financing alone CFOs and treasury heads at Indian conglomerates with dedicated green infrastructure subsidiaries or SPV structures seeking to diversify international debt capital sources Chief Sustainability Officers at Indian companies preparing BRSR Core disclosures who need to align their ESG reporting architecture with international investor-grade standards to qualify for blended finance diligence Indian private infrastructure funds and green bond issuers who may seek co-investment, leverage, or anchor debt relationships with GIP-style vehicles ESG analysts and institutional investors at FPIs and domestic funds assessing Indian infrastructure companies' access to concessional and blended international capital as a valuation and growth driver
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core - mandatory for India's top 150 listed entities by market cap from FY2023-24, expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25: Companies seeking international blended finance must treat BRSR Core as a floor, not a ceiling - GIP-level diligence will require Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 GHG data, water intensity metrics, and transition plan disclosures that go beyond current BRSR minimums ISSB IFRS S1 and S2 - while not yet mandatory in India, SEBI's International Advisory Board and MCA have both signalled convergence intent: Indian companies approaching GIP or FAST-P-linked financiers should begin voluntary ISSB-aligned climate risk disclosure now, as DFI LP due diligence will apply these standards as a reference framework from 2025 onwards IFC Performance Standards (PS1-PS8) - any project receiving GIP debt financing must demonstrate Environmental and Social Management System (ESMS) compliance; Indian project developers must assess their current ESMS maturity against IFC PS requirements before approaching GIP-linked intermediaries India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) 2023 - BEE-notified obligated entities in energy-intensive sectors pursuing transition projects should map their CCTS compliance trajectory, as blended finance eligibility for industrial decarbonisation may increasingly require demonstrated carbon accounting credibility RBI's Draft Framework for Sustainable Finance (under consultation) - Indian banks acting as on-lenders or co-financiers in GIP-style structures will need to align their own green finance taxonomies with RBI guidance once finalised, expected to reference MAS and EU taxonomy principles Green transition project developers must prepare bankable project documentation consistent with international ESG standards - including climate risk assessments, biodiversity impact screening under TNFD-adjacent frameworks, and gender and community safeguard disclosures - before seeking GIP debt eligibility
sections.climate_angle: The GIP architecture is a direct institutional response to what climate finance practitioners call the 'transition finance gap' - distinct from the green finance gap. Green finance covers already-bankable, proven-technology renewable assets. Transition finance covers the harder, messier middle ground: coal plant retirements with just transition obligations, industrial process decarbonisation, early-stage green mobility at non-metropolitan scale, and climate-resilient water infrastructure in high-physical-risk geographies. India's climate transition pathway under its Updated NDC - targeting 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030, a 45% reduction in emissions intensity, and net zero by 2070 - is overwhelmingly a transition finance challenge, not a green finance challenge. The bankable large-scale solar and wind market is reasonably well-served. The transition finance market is not. GIP's blended structure directly addresses this by using junior/concessional capital to absorb first-loss risk and unlock senior commercial debt at scale - the same logic that underpins India's own IREDA viability gap funding mechanisms and the World Bank's partial risk guarantee instruments deployed in Indian renewable auctions. What GIP adds is scale, international LP diversity, and explicit alignment with FAST-P's multilateral anchoring under MAS, which gives the instrument a credibility and replicability that purely bilateral DFI instruments lack. For Indian companies, the climate transition angle is also a BRSR Core obligation angle. SEBI's BRSR Core requires top-listed companies to disclose transition risks under Principle 6 of the National Guidelines on Responsible Business Conduct (NGRBC). Companies that cannot demonstrate a credible transition plan - with quantified Scope 1 and 2 emissions, energy intensity reduction trajectories, and capex allocation toward low-carbon assets - will not only face investor scrutiny under BRSR but will also be functionally ineligible for GIP-style international transition finance. The ESG reporting architecture and the capital access architecture are now the same architecture. The ISSB S2 standard's concept of 'climate-related opportunities' - specifically transition opportunities arising from the shift to lower-carbon energy systems - is precisely what GIP is designed to fund. Indian companies that have begun ISSB S2-aligned scenario analysis and identified their transition opportunity pipeline are already better positioned to engage with GIP-linked capital intermediaries. Those that have not begun this work are creating a capital access risk, not merely a disclosure gap.
sections.what_to_do: CFO / Treasury (immediate - within 60 days): Map your company's transition project pipeline against GIP's stated investment mandate - renewable energy and storage, EV infrastructure, sustainable transport, water and waste management - and identify which projects may qualify for international blended finance debt. Engage your investment banking relationships with HSBC and DBS (both are GIP participants) to understand deal origination pathways and eligibility thresholds Chief Sustainability Officer (next 90 days): Conduct a gap assessment between your current BRSR Core disclosures and IFC Performance Standards PS1-PS8 requirements, including ESMS maturity, climate risk disclosure depth, and Scope 3 coverage. GIP-level ESG diligence will exceed BRSR minimum requirements - identify and close those gaps before approaching international DFI-linked capital Board Risk Committee (next board cycle): Commission a formal briefing on India's transition finance landscape - including FAST-P, GIP, and comparable instruments such as Climate Policy Initiative's blended finance vehicles and AIIB co-financing programs - and approve a capital diversification strategy that explicitly includes international blended finance as a funding source for climate transition capex Sustainability / IR team (within 6 months): Begin voluntary ISSB IFRS S2 disclosure preparation, including physical and transition climate risk assessment using scenario analysis (1.5°C and 2°C pathways). Frame this as both a regulatory readiness measure (SEBI convergence is coming) and an investor relations priority for FPI capital retention Legal / Compliance (ongoing): Monitor RBI's Sustainable Finance Framework consultation for guidance on how Indian bank co-lenders in blended finance structures will be regulated - this will determine the availability of domestic bank participation in GIP-style co-financing arrangements for Indian projects Project development teams at infrastructure companies: Initiate TNFD-aligned biodiversity and ecosystem impact screening for transition projects in ecologically sensitive areas - GIP's LP base includes development finance institutions that will require this as a condition precedent for debt disbursement
sections.our_take: The Pentagreen GIP second close is not a fund story - it is an architecture story. What MAS has built through FAST-P, and what GIP operationalises, is a replicable institutional blueprint for mobilising private capital into transition finance at the scale Asia needs. The fact that DBS - a fully commercial, regulator-supervised bank - has entered as a senior tranche lender is the single most important data point in this announcement. It signals that the risk-return recalibration that blended finance is designed to achieve is actually working at institutional scale, not just in pilot structures. When commercial banks begin treating transition infrastructure debt as a core asset class rather than a developmental accommodation, the capital mobilisation dynamic changes fundamentally. For India, the 12-36 month implication is this: SEBI, RBI, and MoEFCC are all moving - at different speeds - toward a regulatory architecture that will make ESG disclosure quality a direct determinant of capital access, not merely a compliance exercise. SEBI's BRSR Core is the first layer. RBI's sustainable finance taxonomy, when finalised, will be the second. India's participation in ISSB convergence will be the third. Companies that treat these frameworks as independent compliance obligations are misreading the trajectory. The international capital market - and specifically blended finance vehicles like GIP, with their explicit South Asia mandates - is already running ahead of domestic regulatory timelines. Indian companies that invest now in ISSB-aligned disclosure, IFC-standard ESMS, and bankable transition project documentation will have a measurable cost-of-capital advantage over peers that wait for regulatory compulsion. Boards that understand this are making a strategic capital allocation decision. Boards that do not are accumulating an unpriced risk. Watch for SEBI to reference blended finance eligibility criteria in its forthcoming ESG disclosure consultations - the regulator is closely tracking how international capital is pricing Indian ESG quality, and the feedback loop between disclosure standards and capital access is tightening faster than most Indian companies currently appreciate.

title: Novata's Risk Atlas: Why AI-Powered Supply Chain Risk Monitoring for Private Markets Has Direct Implications for Indian PE-Backed Companies and BRSR Value Chain Disclosures
summary: Novata's launch of Risk Atlas - an AI-powered, multi-category ESG risk monitoring tool for private markets portfolios and supply chains - signals a structural shift in how global GPs and LPs will assess, screen, and engage with portfolio companies on ESG risk, including those domiciled in India. For

sections.summary: Novata's launch of Risk Atlas - an AI-powered, multi-category ESG risk monitoring tool for private markets portfolios and supply chains - signals a structural shift in how global GPs and LPs will assess, screen, and engage with portfolio companies on ESG risk, including those domiciled in India. For Indian companies that are either PE-backed, export-oriented, or sit within the supply chains of listed multinationals, this is not a distant fintech story: it is a preview of the due diligence standard their international investors will apply within the next 12-24 months. Combined with SEBI's BRSR Core value chain disclosure requirement - which mandates India's top 250 listed companies to obtain ESG disclosures from their top suppliers and customers - Risk Atlas-class tools are precisely the infrastructure that global LPs will use to flag, score, and escalate Indian counterparty risk before capital deployment or during portfolio review.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/novata-launches-portfolio-supply-chain-risk-monitoring-solution/) covers the May 2026 launch of Risk Atlas by Novata - a New York-based sustainability data platform co-founded by S&P Global, the Ford Foundation, Hamilton Lane, and Omidyar Network. Risk Atlas uses AI to continuously surface, structure, and refresh risk signals across five categories: reputational, cyber, geopolitical, physical climate, and transition risk. The platform is designed for the full investment lifecycle - from pre-investment screening through ongoing portfolio monitoring - and allows GPs and LPs to compare risk exposure across companies, sectors, and geographies, set custom thresholds, and prioritise engagement where material exposure is highest. What this overturns is the older model of annual ESG questionnaires and static ESG scores. Risk Atlas represents a move to continuous, AI-refreshed, multi-dimensional risk surveillance - a category of tool that will increasingly define the minimum standard of ESG due diligence expected by sophisticated LPs in North America and Europe. For Indian context, this has three direct pressure points. First, Indian PE-backed companies - particularly in sectors like manufacturing, logistics, pharma, chemicals, and consumer goods - will increasingly face this level of scrutiny from their foreign PE sponsors and co-investors. Second, Indian companies that are sub-suppliers to global listed multinationals subject to EU CSRD or the German Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (LkSG) are already within scope of value chain ESG risk mapping; Risk Atlas accelerates the commercial implementation of that obligation. Third, and most critically for Indian listed companies, SEBI's BRSR Core framework (effective FY2023-24 for top 150 listed companies, extending to top 250 from FY2024-25) already requires value chain ESG disclosures - meaning Indian CFOs and CSOs are building the data infrastructure that tools like Risk Atlas will query and benchmark against global peers.
sections.who_is_affected: PE-backed Indian companies in manufacturing, pharma, chemicals, logistics, textiles, and consumer goods - particularly those with foreign GP or LP ownership via APAC-focused or global private equity funds BSE 500 and NSE-listed companies in the top 250 by market capitalisation, subject to SEBI BRSR Core value chain disclosure requirements from FY2024-25 onwards Indian exporters supplying to EU, UK, or US multinationals subject to CSRD, LkSG, or the forthcoming UK sustainability disclosure standards - these buyers will use tools like Risk Atlas to assess sub-supplier ESG risk continuously CFOs and CSOs at Indian conglomerates with complex multi-tier supply chains, who must now anticipate that their international investors are moving from point-in-time ESG scoring to continuous risk monitoring ESG and IR teams at Indian listed companies that are either investee companies or supply chain nodes for global asset managers running PE or infrastructure funds through platforms like Hamilton Lane Institutional investors - domestic MFs, insurance companies, and FPIs - that have exposure to Indian PE-backed unlisted companies and need equivalent risk surveillance tools for their own stewardship reporting under SEBI's stewardship code
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core - Value Chain Disclosure: Top 250 NSE/BSE-listed companies (by market cap) must disclose ESG performance of value chain partners (upstream and downstream) covering at least 75% of purchases/sales by value - effective FY2024-25 annual reports, filed by September 2025. Companies that have not yet operationalised supplier ESG data collection are already in a deficit position relative to what global investors using Risk Atlas-class tools will expect. ISSB IFRS S1/S2 - Supply Chain Risk Disclosure: ISSB standards require entities to disclose material sustainability risks across the value chain, including Scope 3 emissions, physical climate risk, and transition risk. Indian companies with international listings or cross-listed bonds, or those raising ECB/green bonds, face de facto adoption pressure as global investors align their due diligence to ISSB. EU CSRD / ESRS E1 and S2: Indian suppliers to EU-listed companies with turnover above €150M must provide ESG data to their EU customers under ESRS S2 (Own Workforce) and E1 (Climate) from FY2025 reporting cycles. This is a contractual, not just regulatory, obligation - and non-compliant Indian suppliers face delistment from preferred vendor programs. German Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (LkSG): Already in force for German companies with 1,000+ employees since January 2024. Indian sub-suppliers in the automotive, textile, and chemical sectors are within scope of LkSG risk assessments - which Risk Atlas-type tools are purpose-built to operationalise. SEBI Stewardship Code (2019, revised 2020): Institutional investors holding equity in listed Indian companies are expected to engage on material ESG risks, including supply chain risks. Continuous AI-powered risk monitoring creates both the data and the obligation trigger for escalated engagement.
sections.climate_angle: Risk Atlas's inclusion of physical climate risk and transition risk as two of its five monitoring categories is directly aligned with the ISSB IFRS S2 disclosure architecture, which Indian listed companies will increasingly be benchmarked against by global asset allocators. For India, physical climate risk is not abstract: the RBI's 2023 report on climate risk and the finance sector explicitly flagged that Indian banks and institutional investors carry significant unpriced physical climate exposure in their portfolios, particularly in coastal manufacturing, agriculture-linked supply chains, and infrastructure. A tool that continuously monitors physical climate risk signals at the portfolio and supply chain level will, within 24 months, become the standard by which foreign LPs assess whether their Indian GP partners are adequately managing climate-related financial risk under TCFD-aligned fiduciary standards. On transition risk, Indian companies in carbon-intensive sectors - steel, cement, aluminium, petrochemicals - are simultaneously subject to India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 and to the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which enters its full charging phase from January 2026. Risk Atlas's transition risk module will be used by European investors and buyers to flag Indian exporters with high CBAM exposure or inadequate decarbonisation roadmaps, directly affecting their access to European trade finance and supply chain contracts. Indian companies that have not yet mapped their transition risk under a Paris-aligned scenario - as required under BRSR Core's TCFD-aligned disclosures - will appear as elevated-risk nodes in any Risk Atlas-class assessment.
sections.what_to_do: CFO / Treasury (Immediate - within 60 days): Audit your current ESG data collection architecture against what Risk Atlas-class platforms will query - specifically: Do you have quantified, audit-ready data on Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 emissions? Do you have supplier-level ESG scorecards covering at least 75% of procurement value, as required under BRSR Core? Gaps here will directly surface as 'data unavailable' risk flags in LP-level monitoring tools. CSO / Sustainability Team (Within 90 days): Map your company's exposure across Risk Atlas's five risk categories - reputational, cyber, geopolitical, physical climate, and transition risk - and document where Indian-specific risk factors apply (e.g., CBAM exposure for exports, monsoon-related physical risk for operations, supply chain geopolitical risk from China+1 procurement shifts). Use this as the basis for your BRSR Core materiality assessment and your ISSB S1/S2 gap analysis. Procurement / Supply Chain Head (Within 6 months): Design and deploy a supplier ESG data collection programme that meets BRSR Core's value chain disclosure threshold. Prioritise Tier-1 suppliers in carbon-intensive or socially high-risk categories. Align questionnaire design with GRI 308/414 and ESRS S2 to ensure data collected is usable for both Indian regulatory reporting and EU customer due diligence. Board Risk Committee (Next board cycle): Commission a briefing on how global LPs and GPs invested in or co-investing alongside the company are evolving their ESG risk monitoring practices - specifically whether they have deployed or plan to deploy continuous AI-based monitoring tools. Ensure the board's risk register explicitly captures BRSR value chain disclosure risk, CBAM transition risk, and physical climate risk as named categories with owners and mitigation plans. IR / ESG Investor Relations (Within 90 days): Proactively communicate to foreign institutional investors and PE co-investors the status of your BRSR Core compliance, value chain ESG data programme, and any third-party assurance obtained on ESG disclosures. Investors using Risk Atlas-class tools will flag companies with opaque or unavailable ESG data as higher risk - a proactive IR position can influence how your company is scored.
sections.our_take: The launch of Risk Atlas is one data point in a much larger structural shift: the industrialisation of ESG risk intelligence for private markets. For too long, ESG due diligence in PE and private credit was episodic, questionnaire-driven, and manually intensive - which allowed Indian PE-backed and unlisted companies to operate with significant ESG data opacity relative to their listed peers. AI-powered continuous monitoring tools eliminate that opacity at scale. Within the next 18-36 months, we expect that any serious global GP with Indian portfolio exposure will either build or license a Risk Atlas-class capability, and LP advisory committees will begin requiring evidence of such monitoring as part of fund ESG governance standards - particularly as ILPA's ESG reporting guidance continues to harden and as EU AIFMD revisions tighten ESG disclosure obligations on EU-domiciled fund managers with Indian portfolio assets. For Indian regulators, this trajectory has a direct policy implication: SEBI's BRSR Core value chain disclosure framework was prescient, but its enforcement and assurance architecture needs to mature rapidly. The current requirement for 'reasonable assurance' on BRSR Core KPIs from FY2023-24 is a necessary foundation, but the assurance standards applied by Indian chartered accountants and sustainability assurance providers are not yet calibrated to the granularity that tools like Risk Atlas will expect. MoEFCC and SEBI should consider whether India's PAT scheme data, CPCB EPR compliance registry, and BEE energy performance data can be made machine-readable and API-accessible - which would allow Indian companies to demonstrate regulatory compliance as a positive risk signal within global ESG monitoring platforms, rather than having their risk profile defined entirely by the absence of data. Indian companies that invest now in structured, audit-ready, API-compatible ESG data infrastructure will have a material competitive advantage in capital markets and supply chain positioning within three years. Those that do not will find themselves flagged, downweighted, or excluded - not by a regulator, but by an algorithm.

title: Datamaran Core's CSRD/ISSB Enhancements: What AI-Driven Double Materiality and IRO Gap Analysis Mean for Indian Companies Navigating BRSR Core and Global Disclosure Convergence
summary: Datamaran's May 2026 enhancements to its AI-powered Datamaran Core platform - including a Double Materiality IRO gap analysis benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports, dynamic stakeholder intelligence, and AI-generated disclosure recommendations - represent a material shift in how technology is

sections.summary: Datamaran's May 2026 enhancements to its AI-powered Datamaran Core platform - including a Double Materiality IRO gap analysis benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports, dynamic stakeholder intelligence, and AI-generated disclosure recommendations - represent a material shift in how technology is industrialising sustainability compliance. For Indian listed companies, this matters immediately on two fronts: Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of EU-domiciled groups are already inside the CSRD value chain scope under ESRS 2 and sector-specific standards, and SEBI's BRSR Core framework - which mandates assurance for the top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 - is converging structurally with the ISSB IFRS S1/S2 logic that Datamaran Core now explicitly supports. The emergence of institutionalised, AI-benchmarked IRO gap analysis raises the bar for what 'adequate' sustainability disclosure looks like - and Indian boards unprepared for that standard face growing audit exposure and investor scrutiny.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/datamaran-launches-new-tools-to-support-csrd-issb-compliance-governance/) covers Datamaran's announcement on 20 May 2026 of a substantive upgrade to Datamaran Core, its AI-powered risk and governance platform. The enhancements are not cosmetic. The platform now delivers: (1) external signal monitoring tracking regulatory developments, peer disclosures, and value chain changes in near real-time; (2) a Dynamic Stakeholder Intelligence Matrix that maps evolving stakeholder priorities - a direct operational response to the ESRS 2 General Disclosures requirement for documented stakeholder engagement; (3) enhanced collaboration architecture with role-based workflows and stakeholder surveys that create an auditable trail across business functions; (4) an IRO gap analysis view benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports - this is the critical differentiator, as it uses actual assurance-level peer filings as the benchmark, not aspirational frameworks; and (5) AI-generated recommendations grounded in peer disclosures rather than generic guidance. What this platform update overturns is the assumption that sustainability disclosure is primarily a reporting exercise managed by a small ESG team working from a materiality assessment done once every two to three years. Datamaran Core is architecting continuous, governed, cross-functional disclosure management - the same operating model that financial reporting has used for decades. For Indian companies, the India-specific implication is structural: SEBI's BRSR Core - which requires the top 150 BSE/NSE-listed entities to provide reasonable assurance over 49 Key Performance Indicators from FY2023-24 onward, expanding to the top 250 from FY2024-25 - creates an assurance obligation that is functionally analogous to CSRD's limited assurance requirement under ESRS. Indian companies that treat BRSR Core as a compliance form-fill exercise rather than a governed, auditable process are creating exactly the kind of IRO gap that Datamaran's benchmarking tool is now designed to expose - and that institutional investors and Big Four auditors are increasingly equipped to identify.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian subsidiaries of EU-headquartered multinationals: directly inside CSRD value chain scope under ESRS G1, E1, and S1/S2 - parent-level CSRD filings now require supplier and subsidiary-level data that Indian entities must furnish Top 250 BSE/NSE-listed Indian companies subject to SEBI BRSR Core assurance obligations - particularly those in manufacturing, IT services, FMCG, chemicals, and infrastructure where IRO identification across Scope 1/2/3 and social dimensions is complex Indian exporters to the EU in textiles, pharma, auto components, and steel - where CBAM (for steel and aluminium), EUDR (for forest-linked commodities), and supply chain due diligence under CSDDD create compounding disclosure demands that feed into CSRD value chain chapters CFOs at listed Indian companies who own the BRSR sign-off and are increasingly accountable to audit committees for the quality and assurance-readiness of sustainability KPIs Chief Sustainability Officers and sustainability heads who must now defend materiality assessments against audited peer benchmarks - not just internal judgement ESG and climate-focused institutional investors - domestic (SBI MF, HDFC AMC, Mirae, Edelweiss Alternatives) and foreign (Norges Bank, APG, CDPQ) - who hold concentrated positions in BSE 500 companies and are using platform-level data to assess disclosure quality gaps Big Four audit firms and sustainability assurance providers in India whose BRSR Core assurance engagements will increasingly be benchmarked against CSRD-equivalent rigour
sections.key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core - Top 150 listed entities: reasonable assurance over 49 KPIs mandatory from FY2023-24 annual reports (filed by September 2024); top 250 entities from FY2024-25 - sustainability teams must ensure IRO identification is audit-ready, not retrospective EU CSRD - Large EU-listed companies (500+ employees): first CSRD-compliant reports covering FY2024 were due in 2025; mid-size listed EU companies covering FY2025 report in 2026 - Indian subsidiaries inside these groups must already be supplying structured Scope 3 and social data upstream ISSB IFRS S1/S2 - Jurisdictions adopting ISSB (Australia, UK, Singapore, Canada, Japan confirmed; India's ICAI and MCA have signalled convergence): Indian companies with cross-listed securities or overseas bond issuances face growing ISSB-aligned disclosure expectations from lenders and rating agencies from 2025-2026 onward ESRS value chain disclosure (ESRS 2, SBM-3): EU parent companies must disclose material impacts and risks in their upstream value chains - Indian contract manufacturers, IT vendors, and raw material suppliers must respond to data requests from EU clients or risk supply chain delisting BRSR Core Scope 3 KPI (KPI 22 - value chain GHG emissions): listed Indian companies must begin disclosing Scope 3 categories relevant to their business model - a requirement that demands the same IRO mapping logic that Datamaran Core now automates
sections.climate_angle: The climate transition relevance of Datamaran Core's upgrade goes beyond software features. The IRO gap analysis benchmarked against audited CSRD peer reports operationalises something that Indian boards have been slow to internalise: climate-related risks and opportunities are no longer self-assessed in isolation - they are now benchmarked against what peer companies have disclosed under external assurance. This creates a ratchet effect. Once CSRD-reporting peers in a sector establish a disclosure baseline - say, a European steel company disclosing transition plan milestones, carbon price sensitivities, and Scope 3 category-specific decarbonisation pathways under ESRS E1 - every Indian steel company supplying into that value chain, or competing for the same green bond investor pool, is implicitly being measured against that baseline. For Indian companies specifically, this connects to three live pressure points. First, the RBI's 2024 climate risk disclosure guidelines for banks and NBFCs, which require financial institutions to assess borrower-level transition and physical risk - a process that depends on the quality of ESG disclosures from listed borrowers. Second, SEBI's BRSR Core climate KPIs, which require Scope 1, 2, and relevant Scope 3 emissions with intensity metrics - the same data architecture that feeds ISSB IFRS S2 climate-related financial disclosures. Third, India's domestic carbon market under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified by MoEFCC in 2023 and operationalised through BEE's PAT scheme expansion - where credible emissions accounting, the foundation of any IRO-based climate risk analysis, determines both compliance cost and credit monetisation potential. Companies that build governed, continuously updated IRO registers now are building the data infrastructure that the CCTS, BRSR Core, and ISSB convergence will all eventually draw from.
sections.what_to_do: Sustainability and ESG teams (immediate, within 30 days): map your company's current materiality assessment against the ESRS Double Materiality structure - identify whether your IRO register distinguishes between impact materiality and financial materiality, as SEBI's BRSR Core and ISSB S1/S2 both implicitly require this granularity for assurance readiness CFOs and Finance teams (within 60 days): commission a gap analysis comparing your BRSR Core KPI disclosures against audited CSRD peer reports in your sector - this is exactly the benchmarking Datamaran Core now automates, but it can be done manually as a one-time diagnostic; the output should feed directly into your FY2025-26 BRSR Core assurance engagement scope Legal and Compliance teams (within 90 days): identify which EU-headquartered clients, parent companies, or joint venture partners have CSRD obligations for FY2025 reporting - map what supplier-level data they will formally request from your Indian entity under ESRS 2 SBM-3 and ESRS G1 supply chain provisions, and establish a data governance protocol now rather than responding reactively Board Risk and Audit Committees (next board cycle): include a standing agenda item on sustainability disclosure governance - specifically, whether the company's materiality process is documented, cross-functionally reviewed, and defensible under external assurance; reference SEBI's BRSR Core assurance obligation and the growing use of AI benchmarking tools by institutional investors to assess disclosure quality Procurement and Supply Chain heads (within 90 days): for companies that are suppliers to EU-listed groups, proactively build a structured ESG data response capability - scope, emissions intensity, water use, human rights due diligence - before your EU clients formalise their CSRD value chain data requests; being ahead of the request strengthens commercial relationships and reduces delisting risk Investor Relations teams (ongoing): brief your top 20 institutional investors on your BRSR Core assurance status, your IRO identification methodology, and your ISSB convergence roadmap - as platforms like Datamaran Core become standard tools for investor-side ESG analysis, companies that cannot articulate a structured IRO framework will face increasing ESG rating downgrades and exclusion from sustainability-themed funds
sections.our_take: Datamaran Core's upgrade is a signal, not just a product announcement. It marks the point at which sustainability disclosure governance is being industrialised the same way that financial reporting controls were industrialised after Sarbanes-Oxley - through technology platforms that create auditable, role-governed, continuously updated data trails benchmarked against external assurance-level comparators. The implication for Indian boards over the next 24-36 months is severe and underappreciated: the standard of 'adequate' ESG disclosure is about to be set not by regulatory minimums but by what AI-benchmarked peer analysis identifies as the norm among assurance-level CSRD reporters. Indian companies that are still treating BRSR Core as a form-filling compliance exercise - rather than as a governed, enterprise-wide disclosure process - will find themselves on the wrong side of that benchmark as institutional investors, Big Four auditors, and ESG rating agencies all adopt similar AI-driven gap analysis tools. Three specific regulatory and capital market shifts warrant board-level attention in the next 12-36 months. First, SEBI is likely to expand BRSR Core assurance obligations beyond the top 250 listed entities and progressively tighten the KPI scope - the ministry's own 2023 consultation papers and IOSCO's global push for ISSB adoption signal that India's trajectory is toward reasonable assurance over a full ISSB S1/S2-equivalent disclosure set by the end of the decade. Second, India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, once operationalised beyond the PAT sector, will create a direct financial consequence for companies with weak IRO-level emissions accounting - the quality of your climate risk identification today determines your CCTS compliance cost tomorrow. Third, the EU's CSDDD (Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive), now in force with transposition deadlines through 2026-2027, will extend legally binding supply chain due diligence obligations to large EU companies' Indian suppliers - meaning that Indian manufacturers who cannot produce structured IRO registers, human rights assessments, and environmental impact disclosures will face contractual and legal exposure, not just reputational risk. Boards that treat ESG disclosure governance as a cost centre are mispricing the liability they are accumulating.

title: ESG & Climate Compliance Digest - 22 May 2026: EPR Enforcement Intensifies Across E-Waste, Plastics & Batteries
summary: Today's digest captures a significant regulatory surge across India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) landscape - with urgent enforcement notices targeting e-waste recyclers and producers, a second amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, updated EPR guidelines for plastic packaging, two

sections.summary: Today's digest captures a significant regulatory surge across India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) landscape - with urgent enforcement notices targeting e-waste recyclers and producers, a second amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, updated EPR guidelines for plastic packaging, two rounds of e-waste rule amendments, and back-to-back Battery Waste Management Rules in 2024 and 2025. The cumulative signal is unmistakable: India's Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) are closing compliance gaps and dramatically raising the cost of non-conformance. Producers, importers, brand owners, and waste processors operating in electronics, FMCG, consumer goods, EV/energy storage, and packaging-intensive sectors must treat EPR obligations as board-level risk - not a back-office checkbox.
sections.what_changed: TODAY'S HEADLINE - EPR ENFORCEMENT NOTICES (E-WASTE): Two urgent notices issued by CPCB mark the sharpest escalation in e-waste enforcement to date. The first targets registered recyclers and refurbishers under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, flagging non-submission or under-reporting of processing data and threatening suspension or cancellation of EPR certificates. The second is directed at producers - including manufacturers and importers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) - for failure to meet annual EPR targets, file returns, or maintain valid EPR authorisations. Both notices signal a shift from advisory to punitive enforcement. PLASTIC WASTE - 2ND AMENDMENT & EPR GUIDELINES: The 2nd Amendment to the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022 tightens definitions around single-use plastics, revises the categorisation of plastic packaging for EPR purposes, and introduces stricter producer accountability for multilayer plastics. Simultaneously, CPCB has released revised Guidelines on EPR for Plastic Packaging, which update the EPR certificate framework, clarify the role of PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations), and set revised recycling/end-of-life targets by plastic category. Together, these updates close several loopholes that companies had used to reduce reported plastic footprints. E-WASTE RULE AMENDMENTS - 2023 & 2024: The E-Waste Second Amendment Rules (2023) introduced revised collection targets phased by producer turnover category and tightened the dismantling-to-recycling handoff documentation requirements. The Third Amendment Rules (2024) went further - introducing mandatory deposit-refund scheme pilots for select EEE categories, stricter penalties for non-compliant recyclers, and a new requirement for producers to publish annual EPR performance reports on their websites. Read together, these amendments represent a near-complete overhaul of the 2022 framework within just two years. BATTERY WASTE MANAGEMENT RULES - 2024 & 2025: The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2024 established India's first comprehensive EPR framework specifically for batteries - covering portable, automotive, industrial, and electric vehicle batteries. The 2025 update accelerates collection and recycling targets, introduces extended producer liability for battery-embedded products (including EVs and consumer electronics), and mandates traceability through a centralised Battery Waste Management Portal. This is particularly consequential for EV OEMs, energy storage system providers, and consumer electronics brands, all of whom now carry full lifecycle responsibility for battery waste.
sections.who_is_affected: Electronics & IT Hardware Manufacturers (laptops, phones, white goods, industrial EEE) - directly under e-waste producer EPR obligations Importers & Brand Owners of EEE - including global brands selling through Indian subsidiaries or e-commerce channels E-Waste Recyclers & Refurbishers - facing direct CPCB scrutiny on processing records and certificate validity FMCG, Retail & Consumer Goods companies - under plastic packaging EPR rules, especially those using multilayer or non-recyclable packaging EV Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) and Battery Manufacturers/Importers - battery waste EPR obligations now fully operational Energy Storage System (ESS) Providers - industrial battery EPR targets now applicable Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) - compliance and reporting obligations tightened across all three waste streams E-commerce platforms acting as marketplace operators for EEE or packaged goods - increasingly in regulatory scope as deemed producers SMEs and MSMEs in manufacturing with annual turnover above threshold limits - phased targets now apply
sections.key_obligations: IMMEDIATE (within 30 days of notice): E-waste producers and recyclers served with CPCB urgent notices must respond with compliance documentation, EPR certificate status, and corrective action plans - failure risks certificate suspension Q1 FY2026-27 (by 30 June 2026): Annual EPR return filing deadline for e-waste producers for FY2025-26 on the CPCB EPR Portal - non-filing triggers automatic penalty proceedings 30 June 2026: Plastic packaging EPR targets for FY2025-26 must be met and reported; revised guidelines require updated PRO agreements aligned to new recycling categories 31 July 2026: Battery waste EPR targets (2025 Rules) - annual collection and recycling obligations for FY2025-26 must be submitted via the Battery Waste Management Portal Ongoing - FY2026-27 Targets: E-waste Third Amendment (2024) requires producers to publish EPR performance reports on corporate websites by 30 September 2026 for the previous financial year Ongoing: Recyclers must maintain real-time digital records of inward and outward material flows; CPCB has indicated surprise audits will increase in H2 2026 Ongoing: Multilayer plastic producers must demonstrate transition roadmaps to mono-material or recyclable alternatives under the revised PWM Amendment - no hard deadline yet, but regulatory guidance expected by Q3 2026 EV OEMs: Battery traceability registration on the Battery Waste Management Portal is now mandatory for all new vehicle models; retrospective registration for existing models required by 31 December 2026
sections.climate_angle: India's escalating EPR enforcement is not occurring in isolation - it is structurally aligned with the global shift toward circular economy principles embedded in major international frameworks. The ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard requires companies to assess physical and transition risks across their full value chain, which now unambiguously includes end-of-life waste liabilities. Companies with unresolved EPR non-compliance are, in effect, carrying unquantified transition risk on their balance sheets. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its accompanying European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) require disclosure on circular economy practices, waste generation, and resource use - meaning Indian subsidiaries or supply chain partners of EU-headquartered companies will face double-barrelled pressure: Indian regulatory compliance AND international disclosure obligations. The battery waste framework, in particular, connects directly to physical climate risk. As India's EV penetration grows - driven partly by climate policy and partly by energy security - the volume of lithium-ion battery waste will grow exponentially. Poorly managed battery waste creates both toxic contamination risk (a physical risk to communities and supply chains) and a critical minerals recovery opportunity. Companies that build closed-loop battery recovery systems now will have a strategic advantage in securing recycled lithium, cobalt, and nickel as virgin material costs rise. Finally, the tightening of plastic waste rules signals regulatory alignment with India's commitments under the UN Global Plastics Treaty negotiations. Companies that treat EPR as a minimum compliance floor rather than a strategic input risk being caught flat-footed when treaty obligations translate into even stricter domestic law - likely within the next 24-36 months.
sections.what_to_do: AUDIT EPR REGISTRATION & CERTIFICATE STATUS TODAY: Cross-check all EPR certificates (e-waste, plastic, battery) for validity, target achievement status, and filing deadlines on the CPCB portal. Identify any gaps before CPCB does - proactive disclosure is treated more favourably than reactive compliance. ISSUE AN INTERNAL COMPLIANCE ALERT TO ALL BUSINESS UNITS: Given the multi-stream nature of today's updates (e-waste + plastics + batteries), sustainability and legal teams should immediately brief product, procurement, and operations heads on applicable obligations. Many EPR failures originate in product design and procurement decisions made without compliance input. REVIEW PRO AND RECYCLER CONTRACTS: Validate that your registered PROs and recycling partners have valid CPCB authorisations and are meeting their processing obligations on your behalf. An invalid or suspended recycler certificate directly exposes the producer - conduct due diligence now, not at year-end. MAP YOUR BATTERY AND PLASTIC FOOTPRINT TO THE NEW RULES: For companies in EV, consumer electronics, or FMCG sectors, conduct a rapid materiality assessment against the updated Battery Waste Rules (2025) and revised plastic EPR guidelines. Quantify your FY2026-27 EPR targets and identify whether current collection infrastructure is sufficient. PREPARE FOR DISCLOSURE - ALIGN EPR DATA WITH ISSB/BRSR REPORTING: Begin structuring EPR performance data (collection volumes, recycling rates, EPR certificate spend) in a format compatible with Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) Core disclosures and ISSB S2 value chain metrics. Investors and lenders are increasingly requesting this data in ESG due diligence.
sections.our_take: Today's cluster of regulatory updates tells a single, coherent story: India is done with the 'awareness and registration' phase of EPR and has entered the 'enforce and penalise' phase with intent. The CPCB urgent notices - issued simultaneously against both recyclers and producers - are a deliberate signal that the regulator is tightening the full value chain at once, preventing companies from offloading liability onto downstream processors. The back-to-back amendments to e-waste and battery rules, layered over a revised plastic EPR framework, reflect a regulatory architecture that is becoming more sophisticated and more difficult to navigate through partial compliance. What's coming next? Expect CPCB to operationalise the deposit-refund scheme for select EEE categories by late FY2027, mandatory third-party EPR audits for Tier 1 producers, and the integration of EPR performance data into the BRSR Core framework for listed companies. For sustainability officers, the message is clear: EPR is no longer a peripheral compliance function - it is a material ESG risk that requires the same rigour, resourcing, and board visibility as carbon reporting.

title: Sustainability as a Growth Driver: What EY's Global Read Means for Indian CFOs Navigating BRSR, Credit Markets, and Supply Chain Scrutiny
summary: EY's Global Climate Change and Sustainability Services Leader Alexis Gazzo, writing in ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/sustainability-as-a-driver-for-growth/), argues that sustainability has decisively crossed from compliance territory into commercial competitiveness and capital access - driven

sections.summary: EY's Global Climate Change and Sustainability Services Leader Alexis Gazzo, writing in ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/sustainability-as-a-driver-for-growth/), argues that sustainability has decisively crossed from compliance territory into commercial competitiveness and capital access - driven by market forces, not just regulation. For Indian CFOs, this is not an abstract global trend: India's top 1,000 listed companies are already inside a BRSR Core assurance regime where ESG data quality directly affects how foreign institutional investors and international lenders price and access their paper. The inflection point Gazzo describes in Europe is 12-24 months away from becoming structurally embedded in India's credit and procurement markets.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/sustainability-as-a-driver-for-growth/) captures EY's Alexis Gazzo articulating a structural shift in how sustainability is now assessed by capital markets globally: lenders are asking for climate transition plans as part of credit underwriting, not waiting for regulatory mandates; procurement RFPs in Europe now weight sustainability criteria at approximately 25% of supplier evaluation scores; and physical climate risks - water stress, heatwave-driven production shutdowns, insurability gaps - are actively reshaping investment prioritisation. What overturns prior thinking here is the sequencing: regulation used to precede market response; now market-led demands are outpacing regulatory timelines. For Indian listed companies, this is directly material. SEBI's BRSR Core framework, made mandatory for the top 150 BSE/NSE companies by market capitalisation from FY2023-24 (with reasonable assurance required from FY2024-25), already mirrors the data quality demands Gazzo describes European banks requiring. The RBI's Draft Disclosure Framework on Climate-Related Financial Risks (2024), applicable to Scheduled Commercial Banks and NBFCs, is pushing Indian lenders to scrutinise borrowers' climate transition assumptions and Scope 3 data - precisely the 'credit assessment' dynamic Gazzo identifies in Europe via the ECB and EBA. Indian companies assuming their sustainability narrative is domestically ring-fenced are misreading the capital flow direction: FII allocations, green bond issuance, sustainability-linked loan pricing, and export eligibility to EU markets are all now conditioned on the same data infrastructure Gazzo describes as non-negotiable globally.
sections.who_is_affected: Top 1,000 BRSR-reporting listed companies on BSE/NSE - particularly those in the top 150 subject to BRSR Core reasonable assurance from FY2024-25 Indian exporters to the EU - including textiles, chemicals, auto components, steel, and pharmaceutical supply chains - where EU CSRD-driven customer RFPs are beginning to embed Scope 3 and supplier ESG screening criteria analogous to the 25% RFP weighting Gazzo cites Water-intensive sectors facing physical climate risk: cement, sugar, thermal power, textiles (especially in water-stressed river basins like Cauvery, Sabarmati), and agri-processing companies dependent on monsoon-linked raw material availability CFOs and Treasury heads at companies with green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, or ECB (External Commercial Borrowing) facilities - where covenant compliance increasingly includes ESG KPI performance Chief Sustainability Officers and ESG/IR teams at BSE 500 companies preparing for ISSB IFRS S1/S2 alignment as SEBI's roadmap likely converges BRSR with ISSB by FY2026-27 Indian mid-cap suppliers to MNC anchor customers with CSRD obligations - their supplier ESG questionnaires are already landing; commercial de-listing risk is real Insurance and risk managers at asset-heavy companies in coastal, flood-prone, or high heat-stress geographies - insurability and property coverage pricing are beginning to move in India as reinsurers reprice climate exposure
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core Reasonable Assurance - Top 150 listed companies by market cap: mandatory from FY2024-25 annual reports (filing deadline typically September 2025 for FY25). Top 250 companies: limited assurance from FY2023-24. Top 1,000: BRSR reporting (unassured) from FY2022-23. Companies not yet audit-ready on BRSR Core KPIs face qualified assurance opinions - a material investor relations risk RBI Climate Risk Disclosure Framework - applicable to Scheduled Commercial Banks and select NBFCs: finalisation expected in 2025-26; lenders will cascade disclosure and transition plan requirements to large borrowers as part of credit risk management - CFOs of large corporates with bank credit facilities should anticipate climate transition plan requests from relationship banks within 12-18 months EU CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) - embedded obligation for Indian steel, aluminium, cement, fertiliser, and hydrogen exporters to the EU: full financial liability phase begins January 2026; Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions per tonne of product must be verified by an EU-accredited verifier. Indian exporters not yet measuring product-level embedded emissions face tariff exposure from Q1 2026 EU CSRD Supplier Due Diligence Cascade - Indian Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers to EU-listed companies: CSRD-obligated EU buyers must report on supply chain ESG data from FY2025 onwards (large EU companies) and FY2026 (mid-size EU companies); supplier ESG questionnaires, science-based targets evidence, and Scope 3 data sharing are becoming contractual requirements, not courtesy requests SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard - Indian companies with committed SBTi targets (over 140 Indian companies as of 2025) must submit validated near-term and long-term targets and align capital expenditure plans accordingly; SBTi's updated standards now require Scope 3 coverage for companies where Scope 3 exceeds 40% of total emissions - relevant to most Indian manufacturing conglomerates TNFD Voluntary Disclosure (building toward mandatory) - Indian companies in sectors with high nature dependencies (mining, agri, FMCG, pharma) should begin TNFD LEAP assessments now, as SEBI's likely next BRSR iteration will incorporate nature-related disclosure requirements modelled on TNFD, consistent with the ISSB-TNFD collaboration pathway
sections.climate_angle: Gazzo's observation that physical climate risks - water restrictions causing production shutdowns, heatwave disruptions to energy systems, and emerging insurability gaps - are now reshaping investment priorities maps with acute precision onto India's operational risk landscape. India is already among the most climate-vulnerable major economies: the 2024 monsoon's uneven distribution caused Kharif crop losses across Maharashtra and UP; Chennai's water crisis of 2019 is a template, not an outlier; and extreme heat events (47°C+ in Delhi and Rajasthan in May 2024) are measurably disrupting construction, outdoor manufacturing, and logistics. Indian CFOs who are not yet building physical climate risk into asset valuation models, supply chain resilience planning, and working capital assumptions are carrying an unquantified but material balance sheet risk. On transition finance, Gazzo's point that corporate investment in renewables through PPAs is now driven more by energy price volatility and security than by regulation directly mirrors India's situation. The surge in Indian corporate PPAs - led by companies like Tata Steel, JSW, Mahindra, and Hindustan Zinc - is a market-led response to merchant tariff volatility and RE cost competitiveness, accelerated by India's PAT (Perform Achieve and Trade) scheme obligations under BEE and the forthcoming Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under MoEFCC's 2023 notification. The CCTS, once operationalised, will price residual carbon emissions in energy-intensive sectors - creating a direct financial incentive structure that aligns exactly with the market-forces narrative Gazzo articulates. Indian companies building robust Scope 1 and Scope 2 measurement infrastructure now will be better positioned to monetise carbon credits under CCTS and to price transition risk accurately for ISSB S2-compliant scenario analysis, which SEBI is likely to embed in BRSR revisions by FY2026-27.
sections.what_to_do: CFO / Treasury: Within 90 days, audit all outstanding credit facilities, green bonds, and ECB agreements for ESG-linked covenants and transition plan clauses; engage relationship banks to understand their climate risk assessment timelines under the RBI framework - do not wait for formal requests; being proactive protects pricing and relationship continuity Chief Sustainability Officer / ESG Team: Commission a water dependency and physical climate risk assessment mapped to the TNFD LEAP methodology and BRSR Core KPI E9 (water intensity) and P8 (business continuity impacts from climate events) - this is the disclosure gap most likely to attract auditor qualification comments in FY2025-26 assurance reviews Procurement / Supply Chain Head: Map Tier 1 and Tier 2 supplier exposure to EU CSRD-obligated customers; issue ESG data collection templates to key suppliers now, modelled on GHG Protocol Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods) and Category 11 (use of sold products); non-compliant suppliers create contractual liability and EU customer de-listing risk from FY2025 onwards Board Risk Committee: Request management to present a climate scenario analysis (consistent with ISSB IFRS S2 requirements) covering at least a 1.5°C and 2°C warming scenario across a 5-year and 10-year horizon - this is already expected under BRSR Core Principle 6 disclosures and will be a standard FII due diligence question at AGMs from FY2026 Investor Relations: Update equity story to explicitly frame sustainability investments - renewable PPAs, water efficiency capex, supply chain decarbonisation programmes - as risk-adjusted return drivers and capital cost management tools, not CSR expenditure; FIIs and ESG-mandated funds evaluate this framing in roadshow materials and BRSR disclosures together Legal / Compliance: Assess CBAM liability for any EU-bound exports of covered products (steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers); engage an EU-accredited verifier now for product-level embedded emissions verification - the January 2026 financial liability phase leaves less than 18 months for data infrastructure build
sections.our_take: Gazzo's framing - sustainability as market-led growth imperative rather than compliance burden - is accurate as a description of where leading global markets are. But for India, the more consequential insight is this: the compliance architecture that Indian companies have been building under BRSR, PAT, EPR, and CCTS is about to become the commercial infrastructure that determines who can access foreign capital, EU export markets, and MNC supply chains. The companies treating BRSR Core assurance as a box-ticking exercise are building on sand. Within 24-36 months, I expect three structural shifts in India: first, RBI-supervised climate risk requirements will cascade into large corporate borrower assessments, making climate transition plans a de facto credit condition for investment-grade Indian companies; second, SEBI will revise BRSR to incorporate ISSB S1/S2-aligned scenario analysis and nature-related disclosures, closing the gap between Indian and global standards and triggering a second wave of ESG data infrastructure investment; and third, EU CSRD-driven supply chain screening will eliminate from European value chains any Indian supplier that cannot provide verified Scope 1-3 emissions data and a credible transition roadmap - creating a bifurcation between ESG-mature Indian exporters and those who lose market access. The boards that authorise ESG investment as a growth and resilience strategy today - not as a regulatory response - will be materially better positioned in 2027. The window for proactive positioning is open, but it is not indefinitely wide.

title: U.S. State AGs Sue ISS Over ESG Proxy Advice: What the Anti-ESG Legal Offensive Means for Indian Institutional Investors, BRSR Disclosures, and Cross-Border Capital
summary: Four U.S. state Attorneys General - Texas, Nebraska, Iowa, and West Virginia - have filed coordinated lawsuits against proxy advisory giant Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS), alleging consumer protection violations, conflicts of interest, and ideologically motivated investment advice disguise

sections.summary: Four U.S. state Attorneys General - Texas, Nebraska, Iowa, and West Virginia - have filed coordinated lawsuits against proxy advisory giant Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS), alleging consumer protection violations, conflicts of interest, and ideologically motivated investment advice disguised as objective analysis. Reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/four-states-launch-lawsuits-against-proxy-advisor-iss-over-esg-policies/), this is the most consequential legal escalation yet in the U.S. anti-ESG campaign - and it directly threatens the global infrastructure that Indian listed companies, foreign institutional investors (FIIs) in Indian markets, and SEBI-regulated proxy advisers all rely upon. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, the critical India-specific insight is this: if ISS is legally forced to abandon or dilute ESG-integrated proxy recommendations in U.S. markets, the voting behaviour of FIIs holding positions in BSE 500 companies will shift - and BRSR-linked shareholder engagement strategies built on the assumption of ESG-aligned institutional ownership will need to be reassessed from the board level down.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/four-states-launch-lawsuits-against-proxy-advisor-iss-over-esg-policies/) confirms that the Attorneys General of Texas, Nebraska, Iowa, and West Virginia have each independently filed suits against ISS under state consumer protection and deceptive trade practices statutes. The suits allege that ISS marketed its proxy advice as objective and financially grounded while embedding DEI, climate, and broader ESG considerations that the AGs argue are ideological rather than return-driven. Conflict of interest allegations are central: the suits claim ISS provided ESG consulting services to the very companies it covered in research - a structural duality that SEBI's own Proxy Adviser Regulations 2013 (amended 2019) explicitly seek to prevent through independence and disclosure requirements for SEBI-registered proxy advisers operating in India. These lawsuits build on Florida's prior suit against both ISS and Glass Lewis, and follow President Trump's December 2025 executive order directing federal agencies to increase oversight of both firms over ESG and DEI voting recommendations. SEC Chair Paul Atkins has also signalled plans to examine proxy advisory firms' role in 'weaponising' shareholder proposals. The cumulative regulatory and legal pressure is substantial: ISS is not a peripheral player - it advises institutional investors controlling trillions of dollars in assets, including major FIIs, sovereign wealth funds, and global pension funds with significant positions in Indian equities across the Nifty 50 and BSE 200. If ISS is compelled - whether by court order, regulatory settlement, or commercial survival instinct - to strip ESG factors from its voting recommendations to U.S.-regulated clients, the downstream effect on shareholder engagement at Indian AGMs becomes real and near-term. Indian companies that have invested in BRSR Core disclosures, SBTi-aligned net zero targets, and CDP responses specifically to satisfy FII stewardship expectations may find those institutional shareholders suddenly voting differently - or disengaging from ESG-linked engagement altogether.
sections.who_is_affected: BSE 500 and Nifty 50 companies with significant FII ownership (above 15-20%) where ISS and Glass Lewis recommendations directly influence institutional voting at AGMs - particularly on board composition, executive pay, and climate-related resolutions Indian listed companies in carbon-intensive sectors - steel, cement, oil & gas, thermal power, chemicals - that face ISS-flagged ESG resolutions or have received 'against' recommendations on remuneration reports tied to sustainability KPI linkage SEBI-registered proxy advisory firms operating in India (InGovern, IiAS, Stakeholders Empowerment Services) who could face reputational and regulatory scrutiny if the ISS conflict-of-interest model is seen to have parallels domestically CFOs and CSOs at Indian multinationals with U.S.-listed ADRs or cross-listed entities - they face dual proxy advisory scrutiny under both SEBI and SEC-regulated frameworks simultaneously Institutional investors: foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) registered with SEBI under the FPI Regulations 2019, global pension funds (CPPIB, APG, GPIF), and ESG-mandated asset managers (BlackRock, Vanguard, Norges Bank) with India allocations who subscribe to ISS recommendations Risk committees and audit committees at Indian listed companies who must now assess whether their shareholder engagement and IR strategies remain calibrated to evolving institutional voting behaviour in a post-ISS-litigation environment India-focused ESG fund managers and PMS providers who use ISS ESG ratings or data in their portfolio construction and stewardship frameworks
sections.key_obligations: SEBI Proxy Adviser Regulations 2013 (amended 2019): SEBI-registered proxy advisers must ensure independence from conflicts of interest and disclose any consulting relationships with covered companies - Indian boards should audit whether their proxy advisers are compliant, given that the ISS conflict-of-interest model is now the subject of active U.S. litigation SEBI Stewardship Code (2019, applicable to mutual funds and AIFs): Indian institutional investors must publish stewardship policies, including how they engage with investee companies on ESG issues and how they vote - the ISS legal pressure in the U.S. does not relieve Indian institutions of these obligations; if anything, SEBI may tighten domestic proxy advisory oversight in response BRSR Core (mandatory for top 150 listed companies by market cap from FY2023-24, top 250 from FY2024-25): Companies must disclose assurance-backed ESG metrics - these disclosures remain mandatory regardless of the U.S. anti-ESG legal climate; Indian companies cannot use the ISS litigation as justification to reduce BRSR disclosure quality SEBI Circular on Related Party Transactions and Board Independence: Indian companies should proactively review whether their engagement with any proxy adviser (domestic or international) could be construed as a conflict - particularly if the same firm provides governance consulting and voting recommendations Ongoing: FPIs registered under SEBI FPI Regulations 2019 should review their internal policies on proxy voting to ensure they are not mechanically reliant on ISS recommendations in ways that could expose them to regulatory scrutiny under both SEBI and their home jurisdiction regulators
sections.climate_angle: The ISS lawsuits strike at one of the most consequential transmission mechanisms linking global capital to corporate climate action in India: the institutional shareholder voting channel. Over the past four years, a significant portion of climate-related shareholder resolutions at Indian AGMs - covering net zero target-setting, Scope 3 disclosure, board climate competency, and executive remuneration linked to sustainability KPIs - have been advanced or supported by FIIs whose voting posture was shaped, in part, by ISS ESG research and voting recommendations. If ISS retreats from climate-integrated recommendations under legal pressure, this transmission mechanism weakens precisely at the moment SEBI's BRSR Core framework is maturing and ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) is gaining traction as the global baseline. Indian companies that have structured their climate transition roadmaps - including SBTi commitments, internal carbon pricing, and net zero pathway alignment - partly to satisfy FII engagement expectations now face a scenario where that engagement becomes episodic or inconsistent. Furthermore, the EU's CSRD and its ESRS E1 (Climate) standard continue to impose mandatory climate disclosure obligations on EU-headquartered firms with Indian supply chain partners. European institutional investors with India allocations are subject to SFDR (Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation) Article 8 and Article 9 obligations that require them to assess and report on climate risks in their portfolios. These investors cannot simply follow the U.S. anti-ESG lead - their regulatory architecture compels climate integration. This creates a bifurcated FII landscape in India: U.S.-domiciled FIIs potentially reducing ESG engagement pressure, EU-domiciled FIIs maintaining or intensifying it. Indian boards navigating this divergence will need a more sophisticated, jurisdiction-differentiated investor relations strategy rather than a single ESG narrative directed at all institutional shareholders.
sections.what_to_do: Investor Relations and Company Secretary teams (immediate, within 60 days): Map your top 20 FII shareholders by jurisdiction - U.S. versus EU versus Asia-Pacific - and assess which are ISS subscribers versus Glass Lewis subscribers versus internal policy voters; this segmentation is now essential for calibrating AGM engagement strategy CFO and CSO jointly (Q3 FY2026): Commission an internal review of all ESG disclosures made in BRSR Core, CDP, and investor presentations to identify which were framed specifically to satisfy ISS-style ESG screens; assess whether those disclosures remain material under ISSB IFRS S1/S2 and BRSR frameworks independent of the ISS advocacy channel Board Risk Committee (next board meeting): Add the ISS litigation and U.S. anti-ESG regulatory escalation as a standing agenda item under geopolitical and regulatory risk; formally assess the company's FII ownership concentration risk if U.S. institutional shareholders shift away from ESG-mandated voting Legal and Compliance (within 90 days): Review all agreements with SEBI-registered proxy advisers and any ESG consulting or rating firms for conflict-of-interest provisions - benchmark against SEBI Proxy Adviser Regulations 2013 disclosure requirements; ensure no dual-role arrangements exist that mirror the ISS conflict allegations Sustainability/ESG team (ongoing): Do not reduce the quality or rigour of BRSR Core disclosures in response to anti-ESG political noise - SEBI's mandatory disclosure framework is independent of U.S. political developments, and diluting BRSR quality creates domestic regulatory and audit exposure under SEBI's reasonable assurance requirement Institutional investor stewardship teams at Indian mutual funds and AIFs (within 30 days): Review published stewardship policies and voting records to ensure they articulate an independent, India-regulatory-grounded rationale for ESG-integrated voting - reducing mechanical reliance on ISS recommendations is now both a legal risk management measure and a regulatory expectation under SEBI's Stewardship Code
sections.our_take: The coordinated lawsuits against ISS by four U.S. state AGs are not a fringe political event - they represent a structural assault on the global ESG information architecture that has underpinned institutional shareholder engagement for over a decade. For Indian boards and investors, the most important signal is not whether ISS wins or loses in U.S. courts; it is that the certainty and uniformity of ESG-driven institutional pressure on corporate governance is fracturing along geopolitical and jurisdictional lines. We are entering a period of deep ESG governance pluralism: SEBI and the EU are tightening their ESG disclosure and stewardship frameworks simultaneously, while the U.S. federal apparatus - SEC, DOL, and executive orders - is actively dismantling or delegitimising the same frameworks. Indian listed companies will increasingly face FII shareholders whose voting mandates are diametrically opposed depending on their home jurisdiction. Within 12-24 months, I expect SEBI to respond to this global fragmentation in one of two ways: either by explicitly strengthening domestic proxy adviser independence standards to differentiate the Indian framework from the politically compromised U.S. model, or by issuing guidance on how Indian companies should handle conflicting FII stewardship expectations. Boards that wait for regulatory clarity before acting are already behind. The smarter move is to anchor ESG disclosure and governance practices firmly to BRSR Core, ISSB S1/S2, and the Indian Companies Act - frameworks with domestic legal force - rather than to the commercial products of globally contested proxy advisory firms. Over the next 36 months, the capital market consequence will be a bifurcation of FII flows: ESG-mandated EU and Asian institutional capital will continue to price BRSR quality and climate transition credibility into valuation, while some U.S. capital rotates away from ESG screens entirely. Indian companies that have built genuine ESG substance - not just ISS-pleasing disclosure - will be better positioned to retain the EU and Asian capital that will matter most to long-term cost of equity.
