Boards that are watching Caudal Energy today, and building it into their 10-year energy strategy, will be ahead of the compliance curve rather than behind it.

title: Climate Adaptation Startups 2026: What the Next Wave of Climate Tech Means for Indian Capital Allocation, BRSR Disclosure, and Supply Chain Resilience
summary: GreenBiz's Climate Tech Startups to Watch 2026 pitch competition - focused explicitly on climate adaptation rather than mitigation - signals a structural pivot in global climate finance toward resilience, physical risk management, and adaptive infrastructure. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, this is not a

sections.summary: GreenBiz's Climate Tech Startups to Watch 2026 pitch competition - focused explicitly on climate adaptation rather than mitigation - signals a structural pivot in global climate finance toward resilience, physical risk management, and adaptive infrastructure. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, this is not a spectator event: India's BSE 500 companies face mounting BRSR Core obligations to disclose physical climate risks, and the adaptation technology stack being commercialised through competitions like this will directly shape which Indian sectors can credibly demonstrate resilience to institutional investors and export buyers by 2027. The India-specific consequence is immediate - boards that treat adaptation as a future concern rather than a present capital allocation decision are already behind. Reported by GreenBiz (Trellis Network) at https://trellis.net/webinar/climate-tech-startups-to-watch-2026-climate-adaptation-pitch-competition/, this competition underscores that the global climate tech investment community has moved decisively beyond carbon accounting software and renewable energy hardware into the harder, commercially earlier-stage problem of helping businesses survive a warming world - a problem India faces more acutely than almost any other major economy.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by GreenBiz (Trellis Network) highlights that the 2026 edition of its flagship climate tech startup showcase is structured specifically around climate adaptation - a deliberate editorial and investment signal that separates this cycle from prior years where mitigation technologies (solar, EV, green hydrogen, carbon markets) dominated deal flow and media attention. Climate adaptation technologies encompass a wide and commercially diverse landscape: precision agriculture tools that reduce crop failure under erratic monsoons, early-warning systems for flood and heat events, climate-resilient building materials, coastal and watershed management platforms, AI-powered supply chain stress-testing tools, water recycling and reuse infrastructure, and parametric insurance products tied to climate triggers. These are not hypothetical - they are the product categories that infrastructure-heavy economies like India will need to procure, finance, and integrate at scale within the next five to ten years. What this overturns is the implicit assumption in Indian boardrooms that climate risk management is synonymous with emissions reduction. SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 and expanding to the top 1,000 by FY2026-27, requires assurance-backed disclosure on physical climate risks - including water stress, extreme weather events, and temperature-related operational disruption - under Principle 6 of the National Guidelines on Responsible Business Conduct. ISSB IFRS S2, which India's Institute of Chartered Accountants (ICAI) has acknowledged as the baseline for future Indian sustainability reporting standards, requires entities to disclose their physical risk exposure using scenario analysis - a task that requires exactly the kind of data infrastructure and technology solutions these adaptation startups are building. The gap between what Indian listed companies are currently disclosing and what ISSB S2 demands is enormous, and adaptation tech is a core part of closing it. The RBI's draft framework on climate-related financial risks for regulated entities, circulated in 2024, further embeds physical risk assessment into the credit and lending ecosystem. Banks and NBFCs are now expected to evaluate borrower exposure to physical climate hazards - which means Indian corporates seeking project finance, green bonds, or working capital from climate-aware lenders must be able to quantify and mitigate adaptation risk. The startups emerging from competitions like GreenBiz's 2026 cohort are, in effect, building the vendor ecosystem that Indian treasury and risk teams will need to engage within the next 24 months.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian agriculture, food processing, and FMCG sector companies - facing the highest direct physical climate risk from monsoon variability, heat stress on supply chains, and water scarcity across Tier 2 and Tier 3 sourcing geographies Infrastructure, real estate, and construction companies - particularly those with coastal, riverine, or flood-prone asset footprints requiring climate-resilient design and asset revaluation BSE 500 listed companies in the top 1,000 threshold bracket - approaching mandatory BRSR Core assurance requirements from FY2026-27 with inadequate physical risk data infrastructure Export-oriented manufacturers in textiles, chemicals, auto components, and pharmaceuticals - whose European buyers are subject to EU CSRD value chain due diligence obligations requiring supplier-level climate resilience evidence CFOs and treasury heads at investment-grade Indian corporates - managing green bond and sustainability-linked loan covenants that increasingly reference physical risk KPIs Chief Sustainability Officers at conglomerates with diverse geographic and sectoral exposure - needing enterprise-level climate scenario tools aligned to ISSB S2 and TCFD Institutional investors - domestic mutual funds, insurance companies, and foreign portfolio investors with India equity and debt exposure - needing portfolio-level physical risk aggregation Indian climate tech VCs and corporate venture arms - for whom this global competition signals where the next vintage of adaptation-focused deal flow will emerge
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core - Physical risk disclosure with reasonable assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 reporting cycle; expanding to top 1,000 listed entities by FY2026-27 per SEBI's phased roadmap SEBI BRSR Principle 6 (NGRBC): All top 1,000 listed companies must disclose risks and opportunities from climate change, water stress, and biodiversity loss - requires structured physical risk identification that adaptation tech platforms directly enable ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis requirement: While not yet formally mandated in India, ICAI's 2024 acknowledgment of ISSB as the reference standard means Indian companies pursuing international capital must begin scenario-based physical risk quantification now - ahead of formal adoption expected in India's updated sustainability reporting standards post-2025 RBI climate risk framework for banks (draft 2024): Regulated entities expected to integrate physical and transition risk into credit underwriting; corporate borrowers will face climate risk questionnaires from lenders by FY2025-26 EU CSRD supply chain disclosure (ESRS E1, E2): Indian exporters supplying EU-listed companies face value chain data requests on physical climate exposure from FY2025 onwards as EU parent companies complete their own CSRD filings India's Long-Term Low Emissions Development Strategy (LT-LEDS) and NDC update: Government commitments include climate-resilient development as a co-equal priority to mitigation - creating procurement and policy tailwinds for adaptation technology vendors in public sector contracts
sections.climate_angle: The global conversation on climate transition has long privileged mitigation - reducing emissions through clean energy, electrification, and efficiency - over adaptation, which addresses the physical consequences of warming that is now locked in regardless of mitigation success. GreenBiz's 2026 competition focus on adaptation reflects a growing consensus among institutional investors and multilateral development banks that the two are inseparable, and that companies without a credible adaptation strategy carry unpriced physical risk on their balance sheets. For India, this distinction is existentially important. India's NDC commitments and the Paris Agreement Article 7 adaptation goals explicitly require India to develop national and sub-national adaptation plans - yet corporate India has largely treated adaptation as a government responsibility rather than a board-level risk management imperative. ISSB IFRS S2 directly challenges this posture. The standard requires companies to disclose not only their Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions (transition risk) but also their exposure to chronic and acute physical risks - including sea-level rise, heat stress, water scarcity, and extreme precipitation - under at least two climate scenarios, including a 1.5°C and a higher-warming scenario. For Indian companies in the BSE 500, many of whom operate in agriculture-linked supply chains, coastal manufacturing zones, or water-stressed industrial corridors (Gujarat, Rajasthan, parts of Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu), the physical risk scenario analysis required by ISSB S2 will reveal material financial exposures that are not currently reflected in annual reports or investor presentations. The adaptation startups being showcased in GreenBiz's 2026 cohort are building the data, modelling, and intervention tools that will allow Indian companies to move from disclosure to action - which is what SBTi's emerging adaptation target-setting framework, TNFD's nature-related risk guidance, and CDP's water security questionnaire are all pushing toward simultaneously. Indian CSOs who wait for regulatory compulsion to engage this space will find themselves behind on both disclosure quality and actual operational resilience.
sections.what_to_do: Sustainability and Risk teams - within the next 90 days, commission a physical climate risk screening of top 10 operational and supply chain geographies using at least one IPCC-aligned scenario dataset; this is the minimum credible input for BRSR Core Principle 6 disclosure and ISSB S2 scenario analysis CFOs and Treasury - review existing green bond frameworks, SLL covenants, and ESG-linked financing documentation to identify whether physical risk KPIs are referenced; engage lenders proactively ahead of RBI climate risk framework formalisation, expected FY2025-26 Procurement and Supply Chain heads - map Tier 1 and Tier 2 supplier concentration in climate-vulnerable geographies (coastal, flood-prone, water-stressed districts per NITI Aayog Composite Water Management Index); initiate supplier resilience dialogues aligned to EU CSRD value chain requirements now arriving from European customers CSOs and ESG leads - monitor GreenBiz's 2026 climate adaptation cohort (https://trellis.net/webinar/climate-tech-startups-to-watch-2026-climate-adaptation-pitch-competition/) as a live vendor scouting exercise; identify 2-3 adaptation technology categories relevant to your sector for pilot procurement or strategic partnership by H1 FY2026 Board Risk Committees - add physical climate risk as a standing agenda item with quantified exposure estimates; ensure the board's climate literacy extends to adaptation, not just net zero targets, ahead of BRSR Core assurance reviews by statutory auditors Corporate Venture Arms and Strategic Investment Teams - the 2026 GreenBiz adaptation cohort represents an early window into the adaptation tech stack; Indian conglomerates and sector leaders should evaluate minority investments or co-development partnerships with shortlisted startups before Series A valuations reflect mainstream institutional demand
sections.our_take: The pivot to climate adaptation in global climate tech investment is not a cyclical trend - it is a structural correction. For the past decade, climate finance flowed overwhelmingly toward mitigation: renewable energy, carbon markets, EV infrastructure, green buildings. This was rational when the regulatory and commercial imperative was emissions reduction. But the science is now unambiguous that 1.5°C is nearly unreachable without severe near-term policy escalation, and even well-managed transition pathways involve decades of compounding physical risk. Investors, insurers, and export buyers are beginning to price this reality, and the startup ecosystem is following capital. GreenBiz's decision to centre its 2026 competition on adaptation is a leading indicator of where institutional climate capital will rotate in the 2026-2030 vintage - and Indian boards that recognise this early will have a meaningful first-mover advantage in securing resilient supply chains, differentiated investor narratives, and access to concessional adaptation finance from multilateral sources including the Green Climate Fund, ADB's climate finance window, and the newly operationalised Loss and Damage Fund from COP28. For Indian listed companies specifically, the next 36 months will see a simultaneous tightening from three directions: SEBI will expand BRSR Core assurance requirements to the top 1,000 companies, RBI will formalise climate risk integration for lenders (changing the cost and availability of capital for physically exposed borrowers), and EU CSRD value chain obligations will require Indian exporters to provide climate resilience data to their European counterparts or risk losing preferred supplier status. The adaptation tech startups emerging from competitions like GreenBiz's 2026 cohort are not peripheral to this story - they are the vendors, platforms, and tools that will separate disclosure-ready, resilience-credible Indian companies from those caught flat-footed in the next climate event. Boards and CSOs should treat the adaptation tech landscape as a strategic procurement and investment priority - not a CSR budget line.

title: 99 Sustainability Certifications for 2026: What India's CSOs, CFOs, and ESG Teams Must Know Before They Invest in Credentials
summary: Trellis (GreenBiz) has published its 2026 list of 99 sustainability certifications across 10 subcategories - the most comprehensive industry reference of its kind - reflecting an accelerating professionalisation of the ESG function globally. For Indian listed companies navigating SEBI's BRSR Core ma

sections.summary: Trellis (GreenBiz) has published its 2026 list of 99 sustainability certifications across 10 subcategories - the most comprehensive industry reference of its kind - reflecting an accelerating professionalisation of the ESG function globally. For Indian listed companies navigating SEBI's BRSR Core mandatory assurance requirements, ISSB-aligned disclosures, and EU CSRD supply chain due diligence demands, this proliferation of credentials signals a structural shift: sustainability is no longer a communications function but a technical discipline requiring auditable, verifiable human capital. Indian boards and CFOs who have not yet mapped their ESG team's competency gaps against the specific frameworks now embedded in regulatory obligations - GHG Protocol Scope 3, SBTi target-setting methodology, ISSB IFRS S2 climate risk quantification - are carrying unpriced organisational risk heading into FY2026-27 assurance cycles.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Trellis (greenbiz.com), at https://trellis.net/article/99-sustainability-certifications-for-2026/, confirms that the 2026 edition of the annual sustainability certifications list now covers 99 credentials across categories including sustainability strategy, renewable energy and decarbonisation, GHG accounting, ESG reporting and disclosure, climate risk, sustainable finance, circular economy, nature and biodiversity, and supply chain sustainability. Notably, new certifications have been added this year from the Sustainability Reporting Institute and from the Science-Based Targets initiative (SBTi) itself - a development with direct implications for Indian companies that have made SBTi commitments but whose internal teams lack the methodological rigour to defend those targets under third-party scrutiny. This is not a minor annual refresh. The expansion from a handful of credentials six years ago to 99 in 2026 mirrors the regulatory intensification that has occurred in parallel: SEBI's BRSR Core framework (mandated for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25) now requires reasonable assurance on nine Key Performance Indicators including GHG emissions intensity, energy consumption, and water withdrawal. ISSB IFRS S1 and S2, which India's MCA and ICAI are actively tracking for potential adoption, demand climate-related financial disclosures that require the same technical competency these certifications are designed to build. The EU's CSRD, effective for large EU companies from FY2024, creates upstream pressure on Indian exporters and supply chain partners - particularly in textiles, pharmaceuticals, auto components, and IT services - to demonstrate ESG data quality that only credentialled professionals can produce and defend. The inclusion of SBTi's own certification programme is particularly significant. India currently has over 150 companies with SBTi commitments, including major names across BSE 500. The SBTi's recent operational disruptions, controversies around Scope 3 target flexibility, and the launch of its Corporate Net-Zero Standard Version 2.0 consultation have placed additional scrutiny on whether Indian companies' internal teams genuinely understand the methodology underpinning their published targets - or whether those targets are reputational assets without technical foundations.
sections.who_is_affected: BSE 500 listed companies subject to SEBI BRSR Core mandatory reasonable assurance - particularly the top 250 now in scope - whose sustainability teams lack GHG Protocol or ISSB-aligned technical credentials Indian exporters to the EU in textiles, pharma, auto components, chemicals, and agri-processing who face CSRD value chain disclosure requests from European customers and must demonstrate data integrity CFOs and finance controllers at listed companies who are now co-responsible for BRSR Core KPI assurance sign-off and need to understand what their ESG teams are certifying and measuring Chief Sustainability Officers and Head of ESG roles at Indian conglomerates, PSUs, and MNC subsidiaries navigating multi-framework disclosure obligations simultaneously (BRSR, CDP, ISSB, GRI) ESG audit and assurance teams at the Big 4 and mid-tier CA firms providing limited or reasonable assurance on BRSR Core - the competency bar for their own practitioners is rising sharply Institutional investors - domestic mutual funds under SEBI's stewardship code, FPIs with ESG mandates, and DFIs including IFC and ADB - who assess investee company ESG data quality as part of due diligence HR and talent acquisition heads at large listed companies who must define ESG role competency frameworks and hiring criteria that align with regulatory-grade disclosure requirements
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core reasonable assurance on 9 KPIs including GHG Scope 1 and 2 intensity, energy intensity, and water intensity - mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 disclosures (already in force); top 250 from FY2024-25 (disclosures due in annual reports filed by September 2025) SEBI has signalled expansion of BRSR Core assurance requirements progressively to top 500 and eventually all listed companies - companies outside the current threshold should treat this as an 18-24 month preparation window, not a deferral EU CSRD value chain data requests: Indian suppliers and service providers to large EU-listed companies should expect formal ESG data requests under ESRS E1 (climate), E2 (pollution), S1 (own workforce), and G1 (governance) from calendar year 2025 onwards as EU companies file their first CSRD reports SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard Version 2.0 (under consultation): Indian SBTi-committed companies must ensure internal teams can revalidate targets under the revised methodology - credentials in GHG accounting and SBTi methodology are now a prerequisite, not a nice-to-have ISSB IFRS S1/S2 - MCA and ICAI are monitoring for adoption; Indian companies with cross-listed securities or international debt issuances should treat ISSB alignment as a near-term disclosure obligation, not a future one BEE's Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) Scheme Cycle 8 compliance and energy audit requirements under the Energy Conservation Act 2001 (amended 2022) - designated consumers must ensure energy managers hold BEE-recognised certifications; AEE's CEM is among the globally recognised equivalents
sections.climate_angle: The professionalisation of sustainability credentials is inseparable from India's climate transition financing architecture. India's updated NDC commits to reducing emissions intensity of GDP by 45% by 2030 from 2005 levels and achieving 50% cumulative electric power capacity from non-fossil sources. Translating these national commitments into company-level transition plans - the kind now expected under ISSB IFRS S2's climate-related financial disclosures and BRSR Core's forward-looking indicators - requires practitioners who understand both the physical and transition risk taxonomy of the TCFD framework (which ISSB S2 is built on) and the sector-specific decarbonisation pathways relevant to Indian industry: steel, cement, aluminium, chemicals, and textiles. India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), notified by MoEFCC in June 2023, and its linkage with the PAT scheme under BEE, is creating a new class of carbon accounting obligation for designated industries. The GHG Management Institute's Diploma in Carbon Management and GHG Accounting certifications - listed in Trellis' 2026 compilation - are directly relevant to Indian compliance officers who must navigate measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) requirements under the CCTS registry once it operationalises. Companies that build this internal capability now will have a structural advantage in the voluntary and compliance carbon markets India is developing through ISGST (Indian Carbon Market). The capital market consequence is equally concrete: green bond issuers on BSE and NSE, sustainability-linked loan borrowers, and companies seeking access to SEBI-regulated ESG funds must demonstrate the quality of their ESG data - and that quality is ultimately a function of the human capital producing it.
sections.what_to_do: CSO and HR: Within the next 60 days, conduct a competency gap audit of your sustainability team mapped against the specific frameworks embedded in BRSR Core KPIs - GHG Protocol (Scope 1, 2, 3), ISSB IFRS S2, and TCFD. Identify which team members hold recognised credentials and which do not CFO and Finance: Map your BRSR Core reasonable assurance obligations for FY2025-26 against the technical competency of both your internal ESG team and your external assurance provider - if neither can demonstrate GHG Protocol or ISSB-aligned certification, this is an audit quality risk you must disclose to your audit committee Procurement and Supply Chain Head: If your company exports to the EU or supplies Tier 1 to EU-listed companies, begin training procurement managers on ESRS E1 and S1 data collection requirements; certifications in supply chain sustainability (listed in the Trellis 2026 compilation) provide a credible baseline Board Risk Committee: Add 'ESG human capital and credential adequacy' as a standing agenda item - the risk that your disclosed BRSR or ISSB-aligned data cannot withstand regulatory scrutiny or third-party challenge is a governance risk, not merely an operational one Legal and Compliance: Monitor SEBI's evolving guidance on BRSR assurance standards and the MCA/ICAI consultation on ISSB adoption; build a regulatory calendar that anticipates credential requirements before they become mandatory thresholds Investor Relations: When institutional investors - particularly FPIs with PRI signatory status or domestic ESG fund managers - ask about ESG data quality controls, be prepared to reference the professional credentials and internal governance structures that underpin your disclosures; this is becoming a standard due diligence question in ESG-linked financing
sections.our_take: The Trellis 2026 certifications list is a lagging indicator of a leading trend: ESG is completing its transition from a narrative and stakeholder management function into a technical compliance discipline with the same rigour expectations as financial accounting. The addition of SBTi's own certification and the Sustainability Reporting Institute's credential to this year's list is not incidental - it reflects a deliberate move by standard-setting bodies to professionalise and defend the integrity of their own frameworks against the growing tide of greenwashing enforcement globally. In India, the trajectory is unambiguous: SEBI's BRSR assurance architecture is a direct precursor to a regime where sustainability disclosures face the same audit standards as financial statements. Within 36 months, we expect SEBI to mandate BRSR Core reasonable assurance for the top 500 listed companies, and to introduce competency criteria for empanelled ESG assurance providers - mirroring the ICAI's existing statutory audit framework. Companies that have invested in credentialled internal ESG teams will face materially lower assurance costs, fewer restatement risks, and stronger positions in ESG-linked financing negotiations. Those that have not will find themselves in the same position as companies that tried to meet IndAS adoption deadlines without trained accountants. The credential gap is not an HR issue. It is a balance sheet risk.

title: UBS Elevates Sustainability and Climate Risk to a Dedicated C-Suite Function: What This Signals for Indian Borrowers, Exporters, and ESG-Linked Capital
summary: UBS has appointed Alexander Seidler as Head of Sustainability and Climate Risk, consolidating the bank's SCR framework under a single accountable leader with explicit authority over client onboarding, transaction approvals, supplier assessments, and internal climate risk disclosures. Reporting by ES

sections.summary: UBS has appointed Alexander Seidler as Head of Sustainability and Climate Risk, consolidating the bank's SCR framework under a single accountable leader with explicit authority over client onboarding, transaction approvals, supplier assessments, and internal climate risk disclosures. Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/ubs-appoints-alexander-seidler-as-head-of-sustainability-and-climate-risk/) confirms that Seidler's remit spans risk appetite setting, supervisory compliance, and SCR escalation - meaning his function is a direct gateway to UBS's balance sheet for any Indian corporate seeking syndicated loans, green bonds, trade finance, or cross-border M&A advisory. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at BSE 500 companies with UBS as a lender, bookrunner, or anchor investor, this appointment is not an HR footnote - it is a policy signal that climate and sustainability risk assessments will be more systematically embedded into every UBS deal touchpoint.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/ubs-appoints-alexander-seidler-as-head-of-sustainability-and-climate-risk/) details that Alexander Seidler's newly formalised role at UBS includes maintaining the bank's SCR risk appetite and standards, integrating SCR into traditional risk management frameworks, overseeing SCR assessments on products, transactions, clients, and supplier onboarding, and monitoring implementation of supervisory requirements. This is not simply an advisory or communications function - it is an underwriting and risk control function with escalation authority. The structural significance is that SCR is now positioned as a risk class equivalent to credit risk or market risk within UBS's internal architecture, not a sustainability overlay bolted onto existing processes. This appointment also reflects the post-Credit Suisse integration imperative: UBS absorbed Credit Suisse's sustainability risk team in 2023 and has spent two years harmonising what were two distinct SCR frameworks. Seidler's elevation signals that this integration is now complete enough to be institutionalised at the senior leadership level. For Indian corporates, the practical consequence is that UBS's SCR standards - which are calibrated against TCFD, ISSB IFRS S2, the EU Taxonomy, and the Swiss Federal Act on Climate Protection - will now be applied with greater consistency and rigour across every geography, including India. Indian borrowers who previously benefited from inconsistent application of ESG criteria across UBS's different regional desks should expect that window to narrow. The India-specific hook here is direct and consequential. RBI's Draft Disclosure Framework on Climate-Related Financial Risks (2024) - which mandates TCFD-aligned disclosures for scheduled commercial banks and large NBFCs - explicitly anticipates that Indian financial institutions will need to build SCR capabilities analogous to what UBS has now formalised. UBS's structural move is therefore a leading indicator of the institutional design that RBI will eventually require Indian banks to replicate. CFOs at Indian conglomerates, infrastructure developers, and exporters who deal with UBS on international capital markets transactions need to understand that Seidler's team will be assessing their climate risk profile before UBS commits capital - and that this assessment will reference ISSB S2-aligned physical and transition risk disclosures that most Indian companies are only beginning to produce under SEBI's BRSR Core framework.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian large-cap and mid-cap companies with UBS as a lender, bond bookrunner, M&A advisor, or equity research coverage - particularly in energy, metals and mining, real estate, chemicals, and infrastructure sectors where SCR scrutiny is highest Indian exporters in carbon-intensive supply chains (steel, cement, textiles, leather, agriculture) whose European and Swiss institutional buyers or lenders are subject to CSRD-level due diligence requirements that UBS's SCR function is designed to assess BSE 500 companies subject to SEBI BRSR Core mandatory disclosures from FY2023-24 onwards, whose ESG data quality will now be directly evaluated by global lenders like UBS as part of transaction onboarding CFOs and Treasurers at Indian companies planning international bond issuances, sustainability-linked loans (SLLs), or green bonds where UBS is a likely bookrunner or arranger Chief Sustainability Officers and ESG heads responsible for preparing climate risk disclosures aligned with ISSB IFRS S2, which UBS's SCR framework explicitly references Indian institutional investors - domestic mutual funds, insurance companies, and pension funds - with UBS co-investment relationships or fund-of-fund exposure, who should understand that UBS's portfolio-level SCR standards will influence asset selection Risk committees and audit committees at Indian listed companies with UBS banking relationships, who need to understand that SCR assessments now carry escalation authority within UBS's deal approval process
sections.key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core - Mandatory ESG disclosures including Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions, energy intensity, water intensity, and supply chain ESG assessments: applicable to top 150 listed companies by market cap from FY2023-24 (filed in FY2024-25 annual reports); extended to top 250 from FY2024-25 - Indian companies seeking UBS capital market access should treat BRSR Core data as the minimum input to any SCR assessment UBS will conduct RBI Draft Climate Risk Disclosure Framework (2024) - Indian scheduled commercial banks and large NBFCs will be required to publish TCFD-aligned physical and transition risk disclosures; timeline for final rules expected in 2025-26 - Indian banks acting as co-arrangers with UBS on syndicated deals will face increasing SCR alignment requirements ISSB IFRS S2 (effective for reporting periods beginning 1 January 2024 in adopting jurisdictions) - UBS's SCR framework is calibrated against IFRS S2 climate risk disclosure requirements; Indian companies in UBS deal pipelines should prepare scenario analysis and transition plan documentation consistent with IFRS S2 requirements even ahead of mandatory Indian adoption EU CSRD / ESRS E1 (phased from FY2024 for large EU companies, FY2025 for listed SMEs) - Indian exporters and subsidiaries of EU-listed parents will face mandatory climate disclosure requirements that UBS's SCR team will reference during due diligence on cross-border transactions SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard - UBS's SCR risk appetite framework increasingly preferences counterparties with validated science-based targets; Indian companies without SBTi commitments face higher SCR risk scoring in UBS's transaction assessments - no fixed deadline, but competitive disadvantage in deal pricing is already materialising
sections.climate_angle: UBS's formalisation of an SCR function with underwriting authority is a structural expression of the transition risk thesis: that the repricing of carbon-intensive assets is no longer a future scenario but an active credit and counterparty risk being managed today by the world's largest wealth manager. For Indian companies, the transition angle is layered. First, India's NDC commitments under the Paris Agreement - targeting 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 and 45% emissions intensity reduction from 2005 levels - create a bifurcated investment landscape that UBS's SCR team will use to differentiate between transition-aligned and stranded-asset-risk counterparties. Indian power sector companies, oil and gas majors, and coal-dependent industrials should expect UBS's SCR framework to apply explicit transition risk haircuts to their creditworthiness assessments. Second, SEBI's BRSR Core requirement for value chain emissions disclosures (Scope 3, Category 1 and 2) from FY2024-25 means that Indian companies are for the first time generating the data that SCR functions like UBS's need to conduct credible assessments. The quality and assurance level of this data - BRSR Core requires reasonable assurance from a SEBI-registered professional - will directly influence how UBS's SCR team scores Indian counterparties. Third, India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022, and the operationalisation of the domestic carbon market expected in 2025-26, will create a new asset class that UBS's SCR function will need to assess for greenwashing risk, additionality, and alignment with Article 6 of the Paris Agreement. Indian companies developing carbon offset projects or purchasing credits under the PAT scheme or CCTS should anticipate heightened SCR scrutiny from global lenders including UBS.
sections.what_to_do: CFO / Treasury - Within 60 days, audit your company's existing UBS banking relationships and identify all transactions in pipeline where UBS is acting as lender, bookrunner, or advisor; brief your treasury team that UBS's SCR function now has escalation authority in deal approvals and that climate risk data gaps can delay or price-adjust transactions Chief Sustainability Officer - Map your BRSR Core disclosures (Scope 1, Scope 2, energy intensity, water intensity, supply chain ESG) against ISSB IFRS S2 requirements for physical and transition risk scenario analysis; identify gaps and commission scenario analysis work in Q3 FY2025-26 before the next deal cycle ESG / Sustainability Team - Obtain or prepare a transition plan document consistent with ISSB S2 paragraph 14-22 requirements covering your decarbonisation pathway, capital allocation toward low-carbon assets, and progress against SBTi or equivalent targets; this document will become a standard SCR due diligence input at UBS and peer global banks within 12-18 months Board Risk Committee - Add 'global lender SCR framework evolution' as a standing agenda item; request a briefing from management on the company's current SCR risk profile as assessed by UBS, HSBC, BNP Paribas, and other international banking counterparties; link this to the board's BRSR oversight responsibilities under SEBI Circular dated 12 July 2023 Investor Relations - When engaging UBS's equity research or investment banking teams, proactively share your BRSR Core ESG data pack and any third-party assured sustainability report; signal awareness of SCR requirements to differentiate your company from peers with weaker disclosure quality Legal / Compliance - Review any sustainability-linked loan (SLL) covenants with UBS or co-lenders for KPI recalibration clauses that may be triggered as UBS's SCR standards are updated under Seidler's tenure; ensure your SPT (Sustainability Performance Target) methodology is documented and defensible under ISSB S2 and SEBI BRSR Core standards
sections.our_take: Alexander Seidler's appointment at UBS is a microcosm of a structural shift that Indian boards must internalise: sustainability and climate risk is completing its migration from the ESG advisory silo into the core risk architecture of global financial institutions. When an SCR head has explicit underwriting authority - the power to escalate, delay, or block transactions - it changes the commercial calculus for every Indian company seeking international capital. This is not about values or branding; it is about deal execution risk. The next 24-36 months will see this model replicated across HSBC, Standard Chartered, Deutsche Bank, and their Indian counterparts as RBI's climate risk framework moves from draft to mandate. Indian banks - SBI, HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, Axis Bank - will face regulatory pressure to build equivalent SCR functions, and their corporate borrowers will face the same data demands from domestic lenders that they currently face only from international ones. The Indian companies that will be best positioned are those that treat BRSR Core not as a compliance checkbox but as the foundation of a credible climate risk narrative - one that can withstand a Seidler-level SCR assessment. The companies that will be disadvantaged are those still filing BRSR disclosures with missing Scope 2 data, unassured energy intensity metrics, and no transition plan. In a world where SCR has underwriting authority, disclosure quality is no longer just a reporting matter - it is a cost-of-capital matter. Boards that have not yet made this connection should do so before their next international capital markets transaction is delayed by a risk escalation they did not see coming.

title: Rio Tinto's $1.5B Low-Carbon Aluminium Commissioning: What AP60 and ELYSIS Mean for Indian Aluminium Importers, Auto OEMs, and BRSR Scope 3 Disclosers
summary: Rio Tinto has begun commissioning a $1.5 billion AP60 aluminium smelter expansion at its Complexe Arvida facility in Quebec, producing metal at just 1.6 tCO2e per tonne - one-seventh of the global industry average of 10.9 tCO2e/t and half the emissions of the adjacent legacy smelter it is replacing.

sections.summary: Rio Tinto has begun commissioning a $1.5 billion AP60 aluminium smelter expansion at its Complexe Arvida facility in Quebec, producing metal at just 1.6 tCO2e per tonne - one-seventh of the global industry average of 10.9 tCO2e/t and half the emissions of the adjacent legacy smelter it is replacing. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at auto OEMs, electrical cable manufacturers, and construction material companies sourcing or competing with primary aluminium, this is not a distant Canadian infrastructure story: it is a forward signal that embodied carbon in aluminium supply chains is being institutionalised as a procurement and disclosure criterion, and India's BRSR Core Scope 3 obligations - applicable to the top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25 - are now directly implicated. As ELYSIS carbon-free anode technology moves toward commercial demonstration backed by the Canadian government, the delta between 'green aluminium' and Indian domestically produced aluminium (Hindalco, NALCO, Vedanta - largely coal-grid powered) will widen, creating export competitiveness risk, supply chain Scope 3 exposure, and a looming green procurement premium that Indian buyers and regulators have yet to fully price.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/rio-tinto-commissions-1-5-billion-low-carbon-aluminum-project-in-canada/) on 1 June 2026 confirmed that Rio Tinto has moved from project approval to active commissioning of its AP60 smelter expansion at Arvida, Quebec. The facility began start-up in March 2026 and is expected to reach full capacity by end-2026, adding 160,000 metric tonnes per annum (mtpa) of primary aluminium capacity for a total of 220,000 mtpa. The technology produces 1.6 tCO2e per tonne of aluminium, powered by Quebec hydroelectricity, versus a global industry average of 10.9 tCO2e/t - the majority of which reflects coal-fired grid power used in smelting in China, India, and parts of the Middle East. The strategic importance lies not just in what Rio Tinto is producing, but in what it is retiring: older coal-era smelting technology at the adjacent Arvida potrooms, whose closure is expected by June 2026. Simultaneously, the company is constructing an aluminium recycling centre at Arvida, and its ELYSIS joint venture with Alcoa - supported by the Government of Canada's Strategic Innovation Fund - is building a demonstration plant for inert anode technology that produces zero process CO2 and emits oxygen as a by-product. When commercialised, ELYSIS would eliminate Scope 1 process emissions from smelting entirely - a paradigm shift for an industry whose emissions are structurally tied to anode oxidation chemistry unchanged for over 130 years. For Indian companies, the critical regulatory hook is SEBI's BRSR Core framework, which mandates assured disclosure of Scope 3 emissions for the top 150 listed entities from FY2024-25 onward. BRSR Core's Principle 6 Essential Indicators and the Leadership Indicators on value chain emissions require companies to map and disclose emissions intensity across their supply chain. Auto OEMs (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Maruti Suzuki), electrical equipment manufacturers (Polycab, Havells, KEI Industries), and construction conglomerates (L&T, UltraTech's aluminium-intensive subsidiaries) that source primary aluminium will increasingly face investor and auditor scrutiny on whether their Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods) emissions reflect actual supplier-level carbon intensity - and whether they are taking steps to shift procurement toward lower-carbon sources. Rio Tinto's Arvida expansion creates a concrete benchmark: 1.6 tCO2e/t is now commercially available at scale in the West.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian auto OEMs and Tier-1 auto component manufacturers using primary aluminium for lightweighting (Tata Motors, Mahindra & Mahindra, Bharat Forge, Minda Industries) - facing Scope 3 Category 1 disclosure pressure under BRSR Core and SBTi FLAG/industry sector pathways Indian electrical and cables sector companies (Polycab, Havells, KEI Industries, Sterlite Power) sourcing aluminium conductors - significant Scope 3 embedded carbon exposure in a sector with active EU CBAM adjacent exposure Indian primary aluminium producers (Hindalco Industries, Vedanta/Balco, NALCO) - facing competitive and reputational risk as their grid-emission-weighted carbon intensity (typically 12-16 tCO2e/t on coal grid) diverges sharply from global low-carbon benchmarks ESG-focused institutional investors and FIIs with holdings in Indian metals and auto sectors - specifically those with net-zero portfolio alignment commitments under NZAM (Net Zero Asset Managers Initiative) or CA100+ engagement mandates CFOs and Chief Sustainability Officers at BSE 500 companies in manufacturing sectors with material aluminium content in their bill of materials Procurement heads at Indian exporters to the EU, UK, and US - particularly where customer sustainability questionnaires, CDP supply chain programmes, or EU CSRD value chain disclosure obligations are beginning to require embodied carbon data from Indian suppliers Risk committees and audit committees at top 150 BRSR Core companies where third-party assurance of Scope 3 data is now a regulatory expectation, not a voluntary commitment
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core - Scope 3 (Category 1: Purchased Goods & Services) assured disclosure: mandatory for top 150 NSE/BSE listed companies from FY2024-25 annual reports (filed by September 2025); top 250 companies from FY2025-26 - companies must now establish whether their aluminium supply contracts include embodied carbon data from smelters SEBI BRSR Core - Leadership Indicator L6 on value chain ESG disclosures: companies must report on the percentage of value chain partners (by spend) assessed on ESG criteria - a low-carbon aluminium sourcing policy directly addresses this obligation ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) - for companies voluntarily adopting or preparing for ISSB-aligned reporting: requires disclosure of climate-related risks in the value chain, including transition risks arising from carbon-intensive input materials; India's ICAI and SEBI are actively aligning BRSR with ISSB S1/S2 EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) - aluminium is a covered sector; Indian aluminium exporters to the EU must report embedded emissions per tonne from January 2026 under the transitional reporting period, with financial liability beginning 2026 under the permanent regime - AP60-grade aluminium at 1.6 tCO2e/t creates a structural cost advantage over Indian coal-grid smelted aluminium at the CBAM border SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard - companies with validated SBTi targets that include aluminium as a material Scope 3 Category 1 input must demonstrate credible supplier engagement or switching strategies; the existence of commercial low-carbon aluminium at scale weakens the 'no commercially available alternative' defence CDP Supply Chain Programme - multinational customers of Indian auto and electrical component exporters who are CDP Supply Chain signatories are required to request emissions data from Indian suppliers; from 2026, CDP is increasing the weighting on Scope 3 supplier-level carbon intensity in scoring
sections.climate_angle: The aluminium sector sits at a structural intersection of India's industrial decarbonisation challenge and its export competitiveness future. India is the world's second-largest aluminium producer, and the sector is among the most emissions-intensive industries in the country - largely because Indian smelters draw from a grid that remains over 70% coal-dependent. NALCO's smelter at Angul, Vedanta's Jharsuguda complex, and Hindalco's Hirakud operations produce aluminium at carbon intensities that are multiples of what Rio Tinto's Arvida expansion now delivers commercially. The BEE's Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme has pushed energy efficiency improvements at these facilities, and the upcoming Indian Carbon Market (ICM) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 will begin to put a domestic price on these emissions - but neither mechanism yet bridges the 7-9x carbon intensity gap between Indian coal-grid aluminium and hydropower-backed AP60 aluminium. For Indian auto OEMs pursuing SBTi-aligned Scope 3 targets - including Tata Motors, which has a formal net-zero commitment, and Mahindra, which has set 2040 targets - the commercialisation of 1.6 tCO2e/t aluminium creates both an opportunity and a pressure point. As EU OEM customers begin embedding embodied carbon thresholds into procurement contracts (BMW, Volkswagen, and Stellantis have all signalled this in their own CSRD-aligned supplier codes), Indian Tier-1 auto component exporters who cannot demonstrate low-carbon aluminium sourcing will face qualification risk. The BRSR Core Scope 3 disclosure regime, combined with ISSB S2 transition risk disclosure, means that this supply chain carbon gap will be visible to investors and auditors - it is no longer an internal sustainability aspiration but a governed, assured reporting obligation. The ELYSIS technology pathway - zero process CO2, oxygen as a by-product - is the most important long-term signal here. When it reaches commercial scale (likely 2028-2032 on current demonstration timelines), it will create a two-tier global aluminium market: primary aluminium with near-zero Scope 1 emissions, and legacy coal-smelted aluminium carrying a significant carbon liability. Indian producers who have not begun the transition - through renewable power purchase agreements for smelters, green hydrogen pilots, or strategic investment in next-generation anode technologies - will find themselves on the wrong side of this bifurcation precisely as India's own carbon market begins to impose a domestic price on those emissions.
sections.what_to_do: CFOs at top 150 BRSR Core companies in auto, electricals, and construction: within Q2 FY2026, instruct procurement and sustainability teams to map aluminium spend by smelter source and obtain supplier-level Scope 1+2 emission intensity data (tCO2e/t) - this is the foundational input for BRSR Core Scope 3 Category 1 assured disclosure Chief Sustainability Officers at Hindalco, Vedanta Aluminium, and NALCO: commission an independent carbon intensity benchmarking exercise against AP60 (1.6 tCO2e/t) and the emerging ELYSIS standard; use this to build a credible transition narrative for BRSR Core Leadership Indicators, SBTi engagement, and CDP scoring - the gap is now quantified and public Heads of Export Compliance and Trade at Indian aluminium product exporters: urgently review EU CBAM embedded emissions reporting obligations for aluminium exports - the transitional reporting regime requires quarterly submissions; Rio Tinto's AP60 benchmark will become a reference point that European importers use to negotiate CBAM cost allocation in contracts Board Risk Committees at auto OEMs with SBTi Scope 3 commitments: add 'low-carbon aluminium sourcing transition risk' as a named climate transition risk in the next board-level climate risk review; instruct management to present a 24-month roadmap for supplier engagement aligned to BRSR Core value chain disclosure requirements ESG analysts at institutional investors holding Indian metals and auto stocks: build a carbon intensity spread into your valuation models for Indian aluminium producers - the gap between 1.6 tCO2e/t (AP60) and 12-16 tCO2e/t (Indian coal-grid) will translate into CBAM tariff differentials, green premium pricing gaps, and SBTi-driven customer switching; this is a material and underpriced transition risk in current valuations Sustainability and legal teams preparing for ISSB S2 alignment: use Rio Tinto's Arvida commissioning as a concrete 'transition risk scenario' input in IFRS S2 climate scenario analysis - specifically as evidence that commercially available low-carbon alternatives exist at scale in the aluminium sector, which strengthens the transition risk narrative for Scope 3-heavy Indian manufacturers
sections.our_take: Rio Tinto's Arvida commissioning is a commercial proof point that will reverberate through Indian boardrooms over the next 18-36 months in ways that are not yet fully priced - either in equity valuations or in compliance planning. Here is the trajectory I expect: First, as ELYSIS moves from demonstration to commercial scale between 2028 and 2032, the global aluminium market will bifurcate into a 'green aluminium' tier trading at a carbon premium and a 'brown aluminium' tier carrying increasing regulatory liability - through CBAM in Europe, Scope 3 disclosure obligations in the US (if the SEC rule survives), and India's own emerging carbon market. Indian smelters, which collectively produce roughly 4 million tonnes per annum on a coal-dominated grid, are structurally positioned in the brown tier unless they act now. Second, SEBI's BRSR Core framework is not standing still - I expect SEBI to tighten value chain emission intensity thresholds in the FY2026-27 BRSR review cycle, potentially introducing sector-specific Scope 3 intensity benchmarks for auto, electricals, and metals, aligned to India's NDC 2030 targets and the emerging ICM architecture under the Bureau of Energy Efficiency. When that happens, companies that have not already engaged their aluminium supply chains on embodied carbon will face disclosure gaps that invite regulatory scrutiny and auditor qualifications. Third, and most importantly for boards to understand: the green aluminium premium is not just a European phenomenon. India's own EV supply chain - battery enclosures, motor housings, structural components - will be built to global OEM specifications, and global OEMs are already embedding embodied carbon thresholds in their supplier qualification criteria. The Indian auto component exporter that cannot demonstrate a credible low-carbon aluminium sourcing pathway by 2027 will not qualify for the global EV supply chain - and that is a strategic business risk, not a sustainability aspiration. Boards should treat this as a capital allocation and market access question, not an ESG reporting exercise.

title: Gigascale Capital's $250M Climate Tech Fund: What Early-Stage Physical Economy Investing Signals for India's Clean Energy Capital Stack
summary: Gigascale Capital, founded by former Meta CTO Mike Schroepfer, has closed its first institutional fund at $250 million, targeting early-stage companies rebuilding the physical economy - clean energy, grid infrastructure, advanced manufacturing, and physical AI - with a clear thesis that cost competi

sections.summary: Gigascale Capital, founded by former Meta CTO Mike Schroepfer, has closed its first institutional fund at $250 million, targeting early-stage companies rebuilding the physical economy - clean energy, grid infrastructure, advanced manufacturing, and physical AI - with a clear thesis that cost competitiveness, not ESG virtue, drives climate impact at scale. Reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/gigascale-capital-launches-250-million-early-stage-climate-tech-fund/), this fund signals a decisive pivot in global climate capital toward deep tech infrastructure plays that directly intersect with India's most pressing industrial decarbonisation and energy security challenges. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, this matters not as distant venture news, but as an early indicator of where technology licensing, supply chain partnerships, and competitive disruption will come from in the next five to eight years - and which Indian sectors will be exposed if they lag.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today on June 1, 2026 confirms that Gigascale Capital Fund I is now live at $250 million, having already built a portfolio of over 25 companies spanning small modular reactors (Radiant), fusion energy (Xcimer), sustainable chemical production (Dioxycle), and clean baseload power (Arbor Energy). This is not incremental green-bond recycling or ESG-labelled growth equity - it is seed-to-deployment capital explicitly targeting physical systems that must win on cost and performance, not regulatory mandate. The Schroepfer thesis - 'climate impact comes when the cleaner system is also simpler, more scalable, and cost competitive' - marks a philosophical break from the compliance-driven ESG capital of the 2015-2022 vintage and aligns with a harder, commercially grounded wave of climate technology investment. For India, the implications operate at three levels. First, grid infrastructure and clean baseload power technologies in Gigascale's portfolio are directly relevant to India's 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030 under India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC), and the operational mandates flowing from MoEFCC's National Action Plan on Climate Change. India's grid flexibility deficit - the inability to balance intermittent renewables at scale - is precisely the problem that companies like Arbor Energy are designed to solve. Second, advanced manufacturing and physical AI technologies in this portfolio will reshape global supply chains for solar, wind, battery, and semiconductor manufacturing - all sectors where Indian PLI scheme beneficiaries (under the Production-Linked Incentive for Advanced Chemistry Cells, Solar PV, and White Goods) face growing competitiveness pressure from technology-superior foreign entrants. Third, sustainable chemistry plays like Dioxycle - which converts CO2 into industrial chemicals - have direct relevance to India's emerging carbon credit market under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) 2023, where verified carbon utilisation could generate tradeable credits under the Bureau of Energy Efficiency's expanding registry.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian power and utilities sector: NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, and CESC - all managing the transition from coal baseload to intermittent renewables and facing the exact grid reliability gap that Gigascale's clean baseload portfolio targets Indian chemicals and petrochemicals majors: Reliance Industries, UPL, Deepak Nitrite, and Aarti Industries - all facing SBTi-aligned decarbonisation pressure and potential disruption from electro-synthesis and CO2-utilisation technologies like those in Gigascale's portfolio Indian advanced manufacturing exporters under EU CBAM exposure: steel, aluminium, cement, and fertiliser producers who face EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism levies from January 2026 and need cleaner process technology to remain export-competitive PLI scheme beneficiaries in solar, ACC battery, and white goods manufacturing - competing against global players who will increasingly have access to Gigascale-funded manufacturing AI and process innovations Chief Sustainability Officers and CTO/R&D heads at BSE 100 companies who need to track technology cost curves for their BRSR Core climate transition plan disclosures - particularly Scope 3 technology substitution risk Indian ESG-focused asset managers and infrastructure investors: Kotak Infrastructure Fund, Edelweiss Alternatives, Avendus, and NIIF - who need to understand whether domestic climate tech allocation is being structurally undercut by US-based deep tech VC at the early stage Foreign institutional investors (FIIs) with India climate exposure: those benchmarking Indian companies against global peers will increasingly ask whether Indian portfolio companies have supply chain access to these emerging technologies
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core - Climate Transition Plan disclosure: SEBI's BRSR Core framework (mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25, expanding to top 1000 by FY2026-27) requires companies to disclose transition risks and technology pathways; boards must now assess whether physical economy disruptions from technologies like those in Gigascale's portfolio constitute material transition risks requiring disclosure under Principle 6 of BRSR India CCTS 2023 - Carbon Credit Trading Scheme: BEE-administered CCTS is moving toward its first compliance cycle with obligated entities in energy-intensive sectors; companies should assess whether CO2 utilisation technologies (analogous to Dioxycle's approach) could generate credits under the Non-Obligated Entity (NOE) pathway - rules expected to be finalised by Q3 2026 EU CBAM - Embedded carbon reporting: Indian exporters of steel, aluminium, cement, and fertilisers must submit verified embedded emissions data to EU importers from January 2026 under the CBAM definitive regime; access to clean process technology comparable to Gigascale's portfolio companies directly affects levy exposure BEE PAT Scheme Cycle III targets - Energy-intensive designated consumers (DCs) in 13 sectors must meet cycle targets by March 2026; technology gap assessments must be submitted with compliance documentation, and clean manufacturing technologies from US deep tech funds will increasingly be referenced in gap closure plans ISSB IFRS S2 - Climate-related financial disclosures: Indian companies with international debt listings or FII ownership above 25% are under growing investor pressure to align with IFRS S2; Gigascale-type technology disruptions must be incorporated into scenario analysis under the transition risk assessment component of IFRS S2 paragraph 25
sections.climate_angle: The Gigascale Capital thesis crystallises the most important structural shift in climate finance since the Paris Agreement: the move from subsidised deployment capital toward cost-curve-crossing deep technology investment. Victoria Beasley's observation - 'cost curves have moved, founders can build and deploy faster than ever' - is not venture marketing; it is an empirical statement confirmed by the levelised cost trajectories of solar, lithium-ion, and onshore wind over the past decade, and it now applies to the harder frontier: grid-scale baseload, industrial heat, sustainable chemicals, and physical AI for manufacturing optimisation. For India, this transition angle is acutely material. India's NDC commits to 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030 and net zero by 2070, but the pathway is constrained by three hard physical limits: grid instability from intermittency, industrial heat decarbonisation for cement, steel, and chemicals, and supply chain bottlenecks for critical minerals and advanced manufacturing inputs. All three are precisely the domains Gigascale is funding at the early stage. If Gigascale's portfolio companies scale successfully over the next five to eight years - the typical horizon from Series A to commercial deployment - Indian industrial companies operating under BRSR Core climate transition plan requirements will face a new class of technology substitution risk: global competitors accessing cheaper, cleaner, more automated physical production systems that Indian incumbents have not co-invested in or licensed. This is the under-appreciated Scope 3 transition risk that most Indian BRSR Core disclosures currently understate. Under ISSB IFRS S2's scenario analysis requirements, transition risk from technology displacement in the 1.5°C pathway must be quantified - and the Gigascale portfolio is exactly the type of development that should be modelled in those scenarios.
sections.what_to_do: CSO and CTO offices (immediate, within 90 days): Map Gigascale's disclosed portfolio - Radiant, Xcimer, Dioxycle, Arbor Energy - against your company's decarbonisation technology roadmap; assess whether any of these represent substitution threats to existing capex commitments or partnership opportunities; this analysis should feed directly into the BRSR Core 'Transition Risk' disclosure under Principle 6, Essential Indicator 6 CFO and Treasury teams (within 6 months): Evaluate whether early-stage co-investment or licensing partnerships with US-based climate deep tech funds is a viable capital allocation strategy; several Indian conglomerates (Tata, Mahindra, Reliance) already operate US VC co-investment vehicles - this is a moment to formalise that pipeline with climate tech focus, given SEBI's BRSR disclosure incentives for companies demonstrating climate technology investment Investor Relations and ESG Reporting teams (before next BRSR filing): Incorporate physical economy technology disruption as a named transition risk scenario in your BRSR Core climate disclosure; FII investors aligned with ISSB IFRS S2 will increasingly probe whether Indian companies have modelled cost-curve disruption from clean energy and advanced manufacturing tech as a material risk - vague 'technology risk' disclosures will no longer satisfy sophisticated ESG analysts at Norges, CDPQ, or APG Board Risk Committees (within next board cycle): Commission a briefing on how India's PLI scheme competitiveness assumptions hold up against the technology cost curves being targeted by Gigascale and comparable funds (Breakthrough Energy Ventures, Lowercarbon Capital, Prelude Ventures); this is a strategic board-level risk item, not just a sustainability team update Procurement and Supply Chain heads at energy-intensive manufacturers (within 6 months): Initiate a technology watch function that tracks commercialisation timelines of clean process technologies in chemicals, grid infrastructure, and advanced manufacturing - specifically mapping against EU CBAM embedded carbon thresholds and BEE PAT compliance gaps, where technology upgrade decisions have a direct regulatory and financial consequence
sections.our_take: Gigascale Capital Fund I is a $250 million bet on a thesis that will define the next decade of industrial competition: that decarbonisation and economic advantage are convergent, not conflicting, objectives. Mike Schroepfer is not alone - Breakthrough Energy Ventures, Lowercarbon Capital, and the US Department of Energy's Loan Programs Office are all capitalising the same conviction with increasingly large cheques. What this signals for Indian boards and ESG investors is a three-to-five year countdown before these technologies exit early-stage and begin appearing in global supply chains at competitive price points that will materially alter the economics of Indian industry's existing decarbonisation pathways. India's regulatory architecture - BRSR Core, CCTS, BEE PAT, and the forthcoming SEBI-mandated assurance requirements for sustainability disclosures - was designed with the current technology landscape in mind. It will need to evolve as cost-competitive clean technologies begin displacing not just fossil fuels but also the first-generation renewable infrastructure that India spent the last decade deploying. The boards and CFOs who recognise this now will make better capital allocation decisions, write better BRSR transition risk disclosures, and avoid the trap of locking into technology commitments that are overtaken by the cost curves Gigascale is explicitly targeting. Watch for two developments in the next 24-36 months: first, SEBI expanding BRSR Core to require quantitative scenario analysis (aligned with ISSB IFRS S2 paragraph 22-25), which will force Indian companies to model exactly these technology disruption pathways; second, EU taxonomy eligibility criteria tightening for Indian exporters accessing green-labelled European capital, with technology performance benchmarks that only companies tracking these deep tech developments will be positioned to meet. The $250 million raised by Gigascale today is a small number relative to India's energy transition financing need - estimated at $10 trillion through 2070 - but the technologies it funds will set the performance and cost benchmarks that define whether Indian industrial capital deployed today is stranded or competitive a decade from now.

title: Rabobank's New CSO Appointment: What Leadership Continuity at a Top Agri-Finance Bank Signals for Indian Food & Agri ESG Borrowers
summary: Rabobank - the world's leading agri-food bank with an active lending and investment book across Indian food processing, dairy, horticulture, and agribusiness - has appointed Dries Lagerberg as its new Chief Sustainability Officer, succeeding Aafke Keizer. Lagerberg's background as Head of Group Sust

sections.summary: Rabobank - the world's leading agri-food bank with an active lending and investment book across Indian food processing, dairy, horticulture, and agribusiness - has appointed Dries Lagerberg as its new Chief Sustainability Officer, succeeding Aafke Keizer. Lagerberg's background as Head of Group Sustainability Regulations & Commitments makes this an internal promotion of a regulatory compliance specialist, not a strategic pivot - a signal that Rabobank is hardening, not softening, its ESG credit and counterparty standards. For Indian agri-exporters, food processors, and cooperatives that access Rabobank financing or sell into European supply chains that Rabobank partially finances, this is a governance moment with direct credit and commercial consequences. Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/rabobank-appoints-dries-lagerberg-as-new-chief-sustainability-officer/) confirms Lagerberg will report directly to CEO Stefaan Decraene, elevating sustainability to the apex of the bank's decision-making structure - consistent with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the European Banking Authority's (EBA) ESG risk management expectations for large European financial institutions.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/rabobank-appoints-dries-lagerberg-as-new-chief-sustainability-officer/), the appointment of Dries Lagerberg as Rabobank's CSO is notable not because a bank changed personnel, but because of who Lagerberg is: a 20-year Rabobank insider whose most recent role was Head of Group Sustainability Regulations & Commitments. That title is the operational engine behind Rabobank's alignment with the EU Taxonomy, CSRD supply chain due diligence obligations under ESRS, and the bank's own financed emissions accounting under the Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials (PCAF) framework. Promoting this person to CSO signals that regulatory compliance - not just purpose-led storytelling - is now the spine of Rabobank's sustainability strategy. This matters for Indian businesses because Rabobank is not an arm's-length European institution. It operates directly in India through Rabobank India, with a loan book concentrated in agri-commodities, food processing, rural finance, and agri-infrastructure. It is also a significant trade finance counterparty for Indian exporters selling sugar, rice, spices, seafood, and processed foods into European markets. Under the EU's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CS3D) and EUDR (EU Deforestation Regulation), Rabobank as a lender has obligations that cascade into the ESG documentation it demands from Indian borrowers and supply chain counterparties. A CSO with a regulatory compliance pedigree will tighten - not relax - those upstream data demands. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at mid-to-large agri-business firms, this is the moment to audit what ESG disclosures, traceability data, and sustainability metrics your Rabobank relationship manager is already requesting informally - because within 12-24 months, those requests will become formal credit covenants or KYC preconditions, aligned with CSRD's value chain disclosure requirements under ESRS E1 (climate), E4 (biodiversity), and S2 (workers in the value chain).
sections.who_is_affected: Indian agri-commodity exporters - sugar mills, rice exporters, spice processors, seafood exporters - with Rabobank as a trade finance or working capital lender Indian food and beverage companies (listed and unlisted) that access Rabobank's project finance or structured agri-lending in India Farmer Producer Organisations (FPOs) and dairy cooperatives that participate in Rabobank-linked rural finance programs Indian agri-input companies (seeds, fertilisers, crop protection) in European supply chains partially financed by Rabobank CFOs at BSE 500 agri-business and FMCG companies with European export revenue - Rabobank credit terms will increasingly embed ESG covenants CSOs and sustainability heads at Indian food processors who must now prepare EUDR-compliant traceability documentation and supply chain ESG data ESG analysts and credit teams at Indian banks co-lending with Rabobank in agri-infrastructure deals - partner bank ESG standards will rise Institutional investors in Indian agri-focused NBFCs or listed agri-companies that sell into European markets financed by Rabobank
sections.key_obligations: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR): Indian exporters of soy, palm, cattle products, cocoa, coffee, rubber, and wood products must provide geolocation and due diligence data to EU importers - enforcement begins December 2025 (large operators) and June 2026 (SMEs); Rabobank as a lender to EU importers will require supply chain ESG compliance from Indian counterparties CSRD / ESRS Value Chain Disclosure: EU companies (including Rabobank's EU corporate borrowers) must report Scope 3 and supply chain sustainability data under ESRS standards - this creates upstream data demands on Indian suppliers and exporters from FY2025 reporting cycles onward EBA ESG Risk Guidelines for European Banks: Rabobank must integrate ESG risk into credit assessment, loan origination, and counterparty due diligence - Indian borrowers should expect ESG questionnaires and scoring to become standard in credit reviews by FY2025-26 PCAF Financed Emissions Accounting: Rabobank's Scope 3 Category 15 (financed emissions) disclosures require emissions data from Indian borrowers - agri-sector borrowers must prepare Scope 1 and 2 emissions data at entity level, consistent with GHG Protocol standards India BRSR Core (SEBI, applicable to top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24): Indian listed agri-companies must disclose Scope 1 and 2 emissions, energy intensity, and water withdrawal - this BRSR data is increasingly what Rabobank's ESG credit team will request from listed Indian counterparties SEBI's BRSR supply chain disclosure pilot (top 250 companies, voluntary from FY2024-25, likely mandatory by FY2026-27): Indian agri-companies should begin supply chain ESG data collection now to pre-empt both SEBI and Rabobank demand simultaneously
sections.climate_angle: Rabobank's agri-finance portfolio sits at the intersection of the two most climate-exposed sectors globally: food systems and land use. The bank has published Science Based Targets (SBTi-aligned) for its financed emissions, with commitments to halve portfolio emissions by 2030 in key agri-sub-sectors. Lagerberg's appointment as CSO - with his regulatory compliance background - means the bank's SBTi pathway will now be enforced through lending conditions, not just reported in an annual sustainability report. For Indian borrowers, this means transition risk is becoming credit risk: a sugar mill without a credible bagasse-to-energy plan, or a rice processor with no methane reduction roadmap, will face higher pricing or covenant triggers in Rabobank-linked financing structures. From an Indian regulatory angle, this aligns precisely with SEBI's BRSR Core requirement for assured Scope 1 and 2 disclosures from India's top 150 listed companies - and the trajectory toward Scope 3 and supply chain disclosures that SEBI has telegraphed in its ESG advisory committee recommendations. Indian agri-companies that build credible, ISSB IFRS S2-aligned climate transition plans now will be better positioned to access Rabobank credit, attract European institutional capital (where SFDR Article 8/9 fund allocations increasingly require Paris-aligned investees), and comply with SEBI's forward disclosure agenda. The Rabobank CSO transition is, in microcosm, the institutionalisation of the Paris Agreement in agricultural credit markets - and Indian agri-business sits squarely in its path.
sections.what_to_do: CFO / Treasury teams at Indian agri-exporters and food processors: within 60 days, audit all Rabobank credit agreements and trade finance facilities for existing ESG covenants or sustainability-linked pricing conditions - map what ESG data you are already required to provide versus what you are actually tracking CSO / Sustainability teams: begin GHG Protocol-aligned Scope 1 and 2 inventory for your Indian operations immediately, using methodologies compatible with PCAF agricultural emission factors - this data will be demanded by Rabobank's credit team within 12-18 months as part of financed emissions accounting Supply chain / procurement heads at companies with European agri-export exposure: initiate EUDR traceability mapping for commodity supply chains (especially sugar, rice, seafood, spices) - Rabobank's new CSO's regulatory background means EUDR compliance will be a credit prerequisite, not a voluntary gesture Board risk committees at BSE 500 agri-business and FMCG companies: add 'European lender ESG covenant risk' to the enterprise risk register - escalating ESG credit conditions from institutions like Rabobank represent a material financing risk that requires board-level visibility and a defined response strategy IR teams and CFOs at listed Indian agri-companies: proactively share BRSR Core disclosures (Scope 1/2, water, energy intensity) with Rabobank relationship managers - framing India's SEBI-mandated disclosures as evidence of ESG governance quality will strengthen credit positioning ahead of formal covenant demands Investor relations at agri-focused Indian companies: brief institutional investors on how your ESG roadmap aligns with the EU's agri-finance ESG standards that institutions like Rabobank are operationalising - this is a differentiation opportunity in a sector where ESG disclosure quality is still highly uneven
sections.our_take: Rabobank's CSO appointment is a personnel story on the surface. Beneath it is a structural signal: the world's most consequential agri-finance institution has just placed a regulatory compliance specialist at the helm of its sustainability function, reporting directly to the CEO. This is not a communications upgrade. This is enforcement architecture. In the next 12-36 months, we expect Rabobank - and by competitive pressure, other European agri-lenders like ING, Crédit Agricole, and BNP Paribas - to formalise ESG performance thresholds as hard credit covenants in agri-lending agreements, particularly for high-deforestation-risk commodities and methane-intensive food production systems. Indian agri-exporters and food processors who have treated ESG disclosure as a compliance checkbox exercise will find themselves repriced or excluded from European credit facilities as these covenants take hold. The India-specific trajectory is equally important to watch: SEBI's BRSR framework is evolving toward mandatory value chain disclosures and third-party assured Scope 3 reporting - a direction explicitly aligned with what CSRD and institutions like Rabobank now demand from their global counterparties. Indian boards that invest in building credible, auditable ESG data infrastructure now are making a capital access decision, not just a governance one. The companies that align their BRSR disclosures with ISSB IFRS S2 and GHG Protocol standards in the next two years will hold a measurable financing advantage over peers when Rabobank and its European peers formalise these demands. The window to get ahead of this curve is 18-24 months. After that, it becomes a crisis management exercise.

title: EU's Anti-Greenwashing Directive Enforcement Begins: What India's Exporters, Listed Companies, and ESG Communicators Must Recalibrate Now
summary: The European Commission has opened formal infringement procedures against 20 of 27 EU member states - including France, Germany's neighbours, Spain, and the Netherlands - for failing to transpose the Directive on Empowering Consumers for the Green Transition (ECGT) into national law by the March 27,
