sections.summary: The European Commission has opened formal infringement procedures against 20 of 27 EU member states - including France, Germany's neighbours, Spain, and the Netherlands - for failing to transpose the Directive on Empowering Consumers for the Green Transition (ECGT) into national law by the March 27, 2026 deadline, as reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/eu-commission-warns-20-member-states-over-failure-to-implement-anti-greenwashing-rules/). With the directive set to enter application in September 2026, this enforcement signal is unambiguous: the EU's ban on unverified generic green claims - 'eco-friendly,' 'sustainable,' 'carbon neutral,' 'biodegradable' - will be legally operative and enforceable against products sold in EU markets, regardless of where they are manufactured. For Indian exporters of textiles, FMCG, chemicals, consumer electronics, and packaging-intensive goods - sectors that routinely use such claims on product labels and marketing collateral targeting EU buyers - this is a direct and immediate compliance exposure, not a future risk to monitor.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today's Mark Segal (June 2, 2026) details the European Commission's dispatch of formal letters to 17 member states and its broader call on 20 states to urgently complete national transposition of the ECGT - the EU's landmark anti-greenwashing consumer directive adopted in 2024. The directive operationalises what was previously a patchwork of voluntary guidance into hard consumer protection law: it bans generic, unsubstantiated environmental claims on product labels and in advertising, restricts sustainability labels to those backed by official certification schemes or public authority mandates, and extends obligations into product durability - prohibiting misleading repairability claims and artificial early-obsolescence prompts. The Commission's own research underpinning the ECGT found that over 50% of green claims in EU markets were vague or misleading, and 40% were completely unsubstantiated. That evidence base now has the force of law behind it. The infringement procedure mechanism is graduated but credible: formal notice letters (now issued) → reasoned opinion if no response within two months → referral to the Court of Justice of the EU with financial penalties. The September 2026 application date means the substantive rules become enforceable against businesses - including non-EU manufacturers selling into EU markets - regardless of whether all member states have completed national transposition. For Indian companies, the critical legal pathway is not EU public law enforcement against Indian entities (which is jurisdictionally limited) but rather private enforcement by EU retailers, distributors, and consumers who can reject or delist products bearing non-compliant claims, combined with EU border and market surveillance authorities acting under existing product regulation frameworks. Indian exporters who have built 'green premium' positioning in EU markets on the basis of unverified claims face delisting, reputational damage, and contractual breach exposure with EU buyers who are themselves liable under national transpositions of the ECGT. The India-specific regulatory hook is also material at the domestic disclosure level. SEBI's BRSR Core framework - mandatory for the top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 and expanding to the top 250 from FY2024-25 - requires assured disclosure on environmental claims and sustainability performance. The BRSR's Principle 8 (inclusive growth) and Principle 2 (sustainable consumption) disclosures explicitly require companies to substantiate claims about environmental attributes of their products. An Indian listed company that makes green claims in EU marketing collateral inconsistent with its BRSR-disclosed environmental data faces a dual exposure: regulatory scrutiny from SEBI on disclosure integrity, and commercial/legal exposure from EU market enforcement. SEBI has been clear, in its 2023 circular on ESG rating providers and its BRSR Core assured indicators, that it intends to bring the rigour of financial audit to ESG claims - the EU is now showing what enforcement of that standard looks like in practice.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian textile and apparel exporters targeting EU retail - particularly those using 'sustainable cotton,' 'eco-dyed,' 'biodegradable fabric,' or 'circular design' claims without third-party certification backed by an official EU-recognised scheme Indian FMCG, personal care, and household products companies selling to EU markets or EU-linked private label contracts, where packaging and label claims ('planet-friendly,' 'green formula,' 'environmentally responsible') are common but unverified Indian chemical and polymer manufacturers supplying EU industrial buyers or consumer goods chains, especially those claiming 'bio-based,' 'recyclable,' or 'low-impact' attributes without substantiation aligned to EU certification standards Indian consumer electronics and EV component exporters making durability or repairability claims that may not meet the ECGT's new requirements for verified guarantee periods and repairability disclosures BSE 500 and NSE-listed companies with EU revenue exposure and green product premiums built on unverified environmental messaging - where BRSR Core disclosures may now be stress-tested against EU-standard substantiation requirements CFOs and Chief Sustainability Officers at Indian exporters who must now assess whether green product claims in EU-facing marketing are aligned with assured BRSR disclosures and can withstand EU regulatory scrutiny ESG-focused FIIs and institutional investors with holdings in Indian export-oriented consumer goods, textiles, or packaging companies - who face portfolio risk from potential EU market access disruption or green premium erosion Procurement and sustainability heads at Indian multinationals supplying European retailers (e.g., H&M, Inditex, Tesco, Carrefour supply chains) who are themselves under ECGT compliance obligations and will cascade requirements to suppliers
sections.key_obligations: September 2026 (ECGT application date): All green claims on products sold in EU markets must be substantiated; generic unverified claims ('eco-friendly,' 'sustainable,' 'biodegradable,' 'carbon neutral') become non-compliant - Indian exporters must audit all EU-facing product labels and marketing materials before this date March 27, 2026 (already passed - transposition deadline): 20 EU member states missed the national transposition deadline; Indian exporters should not interpret this delay as a compliance reprieve - the September application date and ongoing EU market surveillance remain operative Within 2 months of June 2026 letters: Member states must respond to the Commission or face a reasoned opinion - this indicates the final legal architecture will be in place in key Indian export markets (Netherlands, Spain, France, Belgium, Poland) before Q4 2026 Immediate obligation for Indian exporters: Sustainability labels used on EU-facing products must be based on official EU certification schemes (e.g., EU Ecolabel, organic certifications under EU Regulation 2018/848) or established by EU public authorities - proprietary or industry-invented labels are non-compliant Ongoing obligation: Durability and repairability claims must be factually supported and visible; extended guarantee information must meet the ECGT's harmonised label requirements - relevant for Indian electronics, appliances, and consumer goods exporters BRSR Core (India domestic): Top 150 listed entities (FY2023-24 onwards) must provide third-party assured disclosures on environmental performance - any inconsistency between BRSR-assured data and EU-facing green claims creates a cross-jurisdictional disclosure integrity risk requiring board-level attention
sections.climate_angle: The ECGT's explicit ban on environmental claims 'based on emissions offsetting schemes' is among the most commercially consequential provisions for India's transition economy. A significant number of Indian companies - particularly in textiles, consumer goods, and IT services - have built 'carbon neutral' or 'net zero' brand positioning for EU customers on the basis of voluntary carbon offset purchases, often from domestic forestry or renewable energy projects. Under the ECGT, such claims will be legally non-compliant in EU consumer markets from September 2026 unless backed by verified, science-aligned emissions reduction (not offsetting). This directly challenges the credibility of SBTi-unvalidated net zero claims and offset-based carbon neutrality certifications that many Indian companies have adopted as a lower-cost alternative to genuine Scope 1 and 2 decarbonisation. This provision also has a direct bearing on India's domestic carbon market architecture. The Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022, and the Bureau of Energy Efficiency's oversight of the Indian Carbon Market (ICM), are designed to generate tradeable carbon credits from Indian industries and projects. If EU market access increasingly requires verified emissions reductions - not offset purchases - then the commercial value proposition of Indian carbon credits for export-facing companies shifts materially. Indian companies that have been considering purchasing ICM credits to support EU-facing green claims will need to fundamentally reassess that strategy. The Paris Agreement's Article 6.2 bilateral trading framework (under which India is negotiating), ISSB IFRS S2's requirement for transparent disclosure of offset reliance versus actual reductions, and the EU's own Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) - which applies to cement, steel, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity, and hydrogen from October 2023 with full financial liability from 2026 - together create a coherent regulatory direction: verified, measurable decarbonisation is the only durable currency. Boards of Indian export-oriented companies should treat ECGT not as a consumer labelling technicality but as an early signal of the broader EU regulatory direction that will eventually reach B2B industrial supply chains through CSRD value chain disclosure requirements (ESRS E1) and CBAM expansion.
sections.what_to_do: Legal and compliance teams (immediate, within 60 days): Conduct a full audit of all EU-facing product labels, packaging, marketing collateral, and digital claims - map every environmental claim against the ECGT's prohibited categories (generic unverified claims, offset-based neutrality claims, unverified durability/repairability assertions) and identify gap closures required before September 2026 Sustainability / ESG teams (within 90 days): Cross-reference EU product claim audit findings against BRSR Core-assured environmental disclosures - any inconsistency between what is claimed in EU markets and what is disclosed under SEBI-mandated BRSR reporting must be resolved before it becomes a dual regulatory exposure; escalate material gaps to the CFO and audit committee Supply chain and procurement heads (immediate): Issue supplier communication to Indian contract manufacturers and raw material suppliers who provide 'green attribute' certifications or claims used in EU-facing product documentation - require alignment with EU Ecolabel, GOTS, OEKO-TEX, or other officially recognised EU certification schemes; discontinue use of proprietary 'eco' labels without this backing CFOs (board reporting priority, next board cycle): Add ECGT compliance status and EU green claims exposure to the board risk register - quantify EU revenue at risk from non-compliant claims, estimate cost of certification upgrades, and disclose material exposure in next BRSR/Annual Report as a regulatory risk under Principle 1 and Principle 6 of the BRSR National Guidelines on Responsible Business Conduct (NGRBC) Investor relations teams: Proactively brief ESG-focused FII analysts and domestic institutional investors on the company's ECGT compliance readiness - in particular, any 'carbon neutral' or 'net zero' product claims that rely on offsets and will need to be withdrawn or restructured; frame this as controlled risk management rather than reactive compliance Strategy and product development (6-12 month horizon): Begin investment planning for genuine emissions reduction initiatives in EU-facing product lines - decarbonisation of manufacturing processes, material substitution, and design for durability - so that the next generation of green claims can be substantiated to ECGT and ESRS E1 standards rather than relying on offset purchases or unverified assertions
sections.our_take: The EU's infringement action against 20 member states over ECGT transposition is not a story about bureaucratic delay - it is a story about the EU demonstrating that its ESG regulatory architecture has enforcement teeth and that political resistance from member states will be overcome through legal process. For Indian boards and investors, the signal is unambiguous: the EU is building a legally enforceable, consumer-protection-grounded anti-greenwashing regime that will operate at the product level, the label level, and the marketing claim level - and it will apply to every product sold in EU markets regardless of origin. India's export community has, in many cases, benefited from a 'sustainability narrative gap' - the ability to make forward-looking or loosely substantiated green claims in EU markets without facing the legal consequences that EU-domiciled companies face. The ECGT closes that gap permanently. Looking 12-36 months ahead, boards should anticipate three compounding developments. First, the ECGT will be followed by the EU Green Claims Directive (GCD) - a more detailed, sector-specific framework currently in legislative process - which will require pre-market verification of environmental claims by accredited third parties before they can be made. This will raise the substantiation bar significantly beyond what the ECGT requires and will create a formal certification industry around EU green claims. Indian companies that begin building certified green credentials now (EU Ecolabel, verified LCA-based claims, SBTi-validated targets) will have a structural advantage over those that wait. Second, EU retailers and brand owners - already under CSRD/ESRS value chain disclosure obligations - will increasingly make ECGT compliance by their Indian suppliers a contractual prerequisite. The cascade from EU brand owner to Indian manufacturer is already visible in textiles (H&M, Inditex supplier codes), FMCG, and automotive components; ECGT will accelerate and formalise this dynamic. Third, SEBI's own trajectory - visible in the BRSR Core assured disclosure expansion, the ESG rating provider regulation, and its participation in IOSCO's sustainability disclosure working groups - is directionally aligned with the EU's approach. Indian listed companies should expect SEBI to progressively tighten domestic green claims standards, potentially through amendments to the BRSR's Principle 2 (sustainable products) and advertising-related disclosures, in a manner informed by ECGT and the forthcoming GCD. The companies that treat ECGT compliance as a one-time export market exercise will be unprepared for this domestic convergence. The companies that treat it as the first indicator of a permanent, global shift in the legal standard for environmental claims - and invest accordingly - will be better positioned for capital access, supply chain retention, and regulatory resilience across every market they serve.

title: TD Bank's 10-Year CDR Portfolio Deal with Climeworks: What India's Financial Sector and Listed Companies Must Read Into the Carbon Removal Signal
summary: TD Bank's 10-year, multi-technology carbon dioxide removal (CDR) agreement with Climeworks - spanning enhanced rock weathering, biochar, BECCS, and direct air capture - signals that Tier-1 global financial institutions are now institutionalising high-durability carbon removal as a structured, long-t

sections.summary: TD Bank's 10-year, multi-technology carbon dioxide removal (CDR) agreement with Climeworks - spanning enhanced rock weathering, biochar, BECCS, and direct air capture - signals that Tier-1 global financial institutions are now institutionalising high-durability carbon removal as a structured, long-term balance-sheet commitment, not a one-off offset purchase. Reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/td-signs-long-term-carbon-removal-agreement-with-climeworks/), the deal is Climeworks Solutions' first Canadian financial services client and reflects a maturing CDR procurement architecture that Indian banks, NBFCs, and large listed companies cannot afford to dismiss as a Western niche. For Indian CFOs and CSOs operating under SEBI's BRSR Core framework and eyeing SBTi Corporate Net-Zero certification, this deal crystallises an uncomfortable near-term reality: the global carbon market is stratifying rapidly between high-durability removals and low-quality offsets, and India's regulatory and voluntary carbon infrastructure is dangerously under-prepared for that stratification.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/td-signs-long-term-carbon-removal-agreement-with-climeworks/), the Climeworks-TD agreement is architecturally significant in three ways that go beyond the headline. First, it is a portfolio CDR contract - not a single-technology offset purchase - bundling ERW, biochar, BECCS, and future DAC credits from North American sources under a single managed mandate, with Climeworks owning project sourcing, due diligence, and portfolio management. This is a CDR-as-a-service model, and it tells us that institutional buyers no longer want to navigate fragmented voluntary carbon markets themselves; they want a fiduciary-grade counterparty to manage delivery risk. Second, the 10-year tenor signals that TD is treating CDR not as a reputational hedge but as a long-horizon liability management tool - consistent with SBTi's Corporate Net-Zero Standard, which requires companies to neutralise residual emissions (those hardest to abate) through 'beyond value chain mitigation,' a category where high-durability CDR is increasingly the only credible option. Third, TD's Managing Director of Sustainable Finance explicitly flags 'evolving carbon market standards and methodologies' as a risk the portfolio approach helps mitigate - a direct reference to the ongoing quality wars in voluntary carbon markets following the Verra/Gold Standard credibility crises of 2023-24 and the tightening of ICVCM's Core Carbon Principles. For Indian stakeholders, the India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), notified under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 and operationalised through BEE's draft framework, is still in early architecture mode. The CCTS has not yet defined a CDR-eligible credit category, and India's voluntary carbon market players - many of whom rely on REDD+ and clean cookstove credits of contested additionality - will face brutal devaluation if ICVCM exclusions harden globally. Indian companies with net-zero commitments who are banking on low-cost nature-based offsets to close their 2040-2050 residual emissions gap are now looking at a structural credibility problem with global investors and SBTi validators.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian public sector and private sector banks and NBFCs with SBTi commitments or net-zero pledges (SBI, HDFC Bank, Axis Bank, Kotak, Yes Bank, IDFC First) - their Scope 1, 2, and financed emissions (Scope 3 Category 15) residual abatement strategies are directly implicated BSE 500 listed companies in hard-to-abate sectors - steel, cement, aluminium, chemicals, aviation - where residual emissions after maximum technical abatement will require high-durability CDR under SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard compliance Indian conglomerates and exporters facing EU CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) obligations from 2026 and needing credible carbon accounting that distinguishes abatement from removal CFOs and treasury heads at large listed companies managing voluntary carbon credit portfolios as part of ESG-linked financing covenants or green bond framework commitments Chief Sustainability Officers at Top 1000 listed companies under SEBI BRSR Core - responsible for Scope 3 disclosure accuracy and residual emissions quantification ESG analysts and fund managers at domestic and FII institutions running SBTi-aligned or net-zero-aligned equity and fixed income mandates across Indian financials and industrials India's emerging CDR and carbon project developers - biochar, afforestation, and soil carbon project originators who need to understand where the global quality bar is being set by institutional buyers like TD Bank
sections.key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core - mandatory for Top 150 listed entities (by market cap) from FY2023-24, Top 250 from FY2024-25: Principle 6 requires disclosure of GHG emissions (Scope 1, 2, and optionally Scope 3) and any carbon offset/removal strategy must be accurately characterised - confusing low-quality avoidance offsets with high-durability removals creates material misstatement risk under SEBI's LODR (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements) Regulations SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Companies with validated near-term and long-term science-based targets must neutralise residual emissions (typically 5-10% of base-year footprint) using 'beyond value chain mitigation' - SBTi's guidance increasingly points toward high-permanence CDR for this category; companies that have made SBTi commitments without a credible CDR procurement roadmap face validation rejection or target withdrawal India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) - BEE operationalisation timeline: Obligated entities under the PAT scheme and new CCTS framework must begin understanding how CDR credits will or will not be recognised under India's compliance carbon market; engage with BEE and MoEFCC now before registry rules are locked EU CBAM - transitional phase reporting obligations active from October 2023, financial obligations from January 2026: Indian steel, aluminium, cement, and fertiliser exporters to the EU must report embedded carbon accurately; any offset or removal claims in carbon accounting will be scrutinised under CBAM's methodological requirements, which do not currently recognise voluntary offsets as reducing CBAM liability GHG Protocol Corporate Standard - the foundational accounting rule: companies must clearly separate 'avoided emissions,' 'carbon offsets,' and 'carbon removals' in their net-zero accounting; conflating these categories in BRSR or sustainability reports is an audit and investor relations risk
sections.climate_angle: The TD-Climeworks deal reflects the hardening global consensus - embedded in ISSB IFRS S2 climate-related disclosures, the SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard, and the Paris Agreement's Article 6.4 mechanism - that net-zero credibility ultimately rests on the quality and permanence of carbon removals used to neutralise residual emissions, not on the volume of cheap avoidance credits accumulated. For Indian companies, this has a direct BRSR and transition finance consequence. SEBI's BRSR Core framework, which SEBI intends to evolve toward ISSB IFRS S2 alignment (as signalled in SEBI's April 2023 consultation and subsequent communications), will increasingly require companies to disclose not just that they are purchasing carbon credits, but the durability class, vintage, registry, and third-party verification standard of those credits. Indian companies that have built their net-zero narratives on Verra VCS or Gold Standard nature-based credits - many of which are now under ICVCM scrutiny and failing Core Carbon Principles assessments - face a credibility reset with institutional investors running ISSB-aligned or TCFD-successor due diligence. The transition finance implication is equally stark: green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, and ESG-rated instruments issued by Indian companies often embed carbon neutrality or net-zero milestone covenants. If the carbon credits underpinning those milestones are downgraded or invalidated by evolving global standards, covenant breach risk becomes a real treasury and credit risk - not just a reputational one. India's CCTS, when it matures, must grapple with this hierarchy: will it create a high-durability CDR credit category that aligns with global institutional standards, or will it produce a domestic credit architecture that is commercially isolated from the emerging global CDR market that TD Bank and peers are now institutionalising?
sections.what_to_do: CFOs and Treasury teams at SBTi-committed or net-zero-pledged companies: Within the next 90 days, commission an internal audit of your current voluntary carbon credit portfolio - map every credit by technology type, durability class (avoidance vs. removal), registry standard, and ICVCM Core Carbon Principles compliance status; quantify the exposure to credit invalidation or devaluation Chief Sustainability Officers at Top 250 BRSR Core companies: Review your BRSR Principle 6 disclosures and any sustainability report carbon neutrality claims for alignment with GHG Protocol's distinction between 'offsets' and 'removals'; ensure you are not conflating the two categories in ways that create audit or investor relations exposure under SEBI LODR Sustainability and finance teams at hard-to-abate sector companies (steel, cement, aluminium, chemicals): Begin scenario modelling for residual emissions under a high-durability CDR procurement requirement; build a 10-year CDR cost curve into your transition plan, recognising that DAC and BECCS credits will likely cost USD 200-600/tonne vs. USD 5-15/tonne for nature-based avoidance credits - this is a material capex and opex planning issue Board Risk Committees at listed financial institutions with financed emissions disclosures: Place 'carbon credit quality risk in net-zero commitments' on the next board agenda; seek management assurance that CDR procurement strategy is consistent with SBTi validation requirements and forthcoming ISSB S2-aligned SEBI expectations Government affairs and regulatory engagement teams: Engage proactively with BEE and MoEFCC on CCTS registry design to advocate for a CDR credit category aligned with ICVCM Core Carbon Principles and GHG Protocol removal accounting standards - first-mover input into CCTS architecture is a material competitive advantage ESG analysts and fund managers: Upgrade your due diligence questionnaire for Indian corporates to explicitly probe the durability class and ICVCM compliance status of carbon credits being used in net-zero claims; companies that cannot answer this question clearly represent an elevated greenwashing and covenant breach risk in your portfolio
sections.our_take: The TD-Climeworks deal is not a curiosity from the Canadian financial services market - it is a leading indicator of where the global institutional standard for net-zero credibility is heading, and India's corporate and regulatory ecosystem has perhaps 24-36 months before that standard becomes an enforceable expectation at the capital markets interface. Here is what I expect to unfold. First, SEBI's BRSR evolution toward ISSB IFRS S2 alignment - which SEBI has signalled at multiple levels - will force a quality distinction between carbon avoidance credits and carbon removal credits in Indian disclosure frameworks, likely within the next two BRSR revision cycles. Companies that have not prepared for this distinction will face restatement risk and investor scrutiny. Second, the CCTS, once it moves beyond PAT-scheme legacy architecture into a genuine carbon price discovery mechanism, will face pressure from international investors and India's own export-sector companies to recognise high-durability CDR credits - particularly as EU CBAM interoperability becomes a commercial necessity. Third, global CDR procurement is entering a decade of rapid cost decline and quality standardisation: DAC costs are projected to fall from USD 1,000/tonne today toward USD 200-300/tonne by 2035 as facilities scale, and institutional buyers like TD Bank are locking in long-term supply now precisely to ride that cost curve. Indian companies that wait until CDR is cheap before building procurement relationships will find themselves at the back of the queue when SBTi or SEBI-equivalent frameworks make high-durability removals a compliance requirement. The boards that understand this today - and begin building CDR literacy into their climate finance strategy now - will have a meaningful first-mover advantage in transition finance access, SBTi validation, and investor-grade net-zero credibility. The boards that do not will be explaining to their audit committees in 2028 why their carbon neutrality claims no longer withstand scrutiny.

title: UK's 87% Emissions Cut by 2040: What India's Exporters, Investors, and Climate Policymakers Must Read Between the Lines
summary: The UK government's proposed Seventh Carbon Budget - capping economy-wide GHG emissions at 535 MtCO2e for 2038-2042 and targeting an 87% reduction from 1990 levels by 2040 - is not a distant policy signal. For Indian exporters with UK supply chain exposure, Indian companies seeking London-listed cap

sections.summary: The UK government's proposed Seventh Carbon Budget - capping economy-wide GHG emissions at 535 MtCO2e for 2038-2042 and targeting an 87% reduction from 1990 levels by 2040 - is not a distant policy signal. For Indian exporters with UK supply chain exposure, Indian companies seeking London-listed capital or UK institutional investment, and India's own domestic climate policy architects, this budget redraws the commercial and regulatory landscape well before 2038. Reporting by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/uk-sets-target-to-reduce-carbon-emissions-87-by-2040/) confirms the budget aligns with the Climate Change Committee's prior recommendation and is now before Parliament for approval. The single most important insight for Indian stakeholders: the UK's sectoral decarbonisation sequencing - energy first, then buildings and transport, then agriculture and land use - will cascade directly into UK procurement standards, import carbon requirements, and investor mandates affecting Indian steel, textiles, chemicals, leather, and agri-commodity exporters. Indian CFOs and CSOs who treat this as a UK-domestic story do so at material commercial risk.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today confirms that UK Energy Secretary Ed Miliband introduced the Seventh Carbon Budget to Parliament on June 2, 2026, adopting the Climate Change Committee's recommended trajectory in full - an 87% reduction in economy-wide emissions against a 1990 baseline, covering the 2038-2042 budget period. This is the most aggressive legally-binding national carbon budget any G7 economy has legislated to date for the 2040 horizon, and it extends a statutory architecture - the Climate Change Act 2008 - that has already delivered a 54% emissions reduction as of 2025, with the pace of reduction more than doubling since carbon budgets were introduced. What this overturns or builds on is critical context: the UK's previous carbon budget trajectory was already considered among the world's most ambitious, but the Seventh Budget explicitly shifts the decarbonisation focus away from the electricity sector (largely accomplished) toward buildings, surface transport, agriculture, and industrial processes. This is the inflection point Indian exporters must internalize. The UK's Scope 3 import supply chain - steel for construction, textiles for retail, leather for fashion, processed foods and agri-commodities - will face mounting carbon content scrutiny as UK buyers themselves come under statutory pressure to decarbonise their value chains under CSRD-equivalent UK reporting obligations (the UK's Sustainability Disclosure Standards, or UK SDS, currently in final DESNZ/FCA consultation). The CCC's analysis explicitly states that net savings only emerge during the Seventh Budget period itself, meaning the near-term (2026-2037) is a period of front-loaded capital deployment and supply chain transformation - precisely when UK corporate procurement will tighten supplier emission requirements. For India's domestic policy architecture, this matters in three specific ways. First, it strengthens the political economy case for India's own Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022, and for the PAT Scheme's evolution into a more rigorous cap-and-trade instrument - UK precedent of 18 years of statutory carbon budgets delivering results will be cited by MoEFCC and BEE in inter-ministerial debates. Second, SEBI's BRSR Core mandatory disclosures (applicable to the top 150 listed companies by market cap for FY2023-24, expanding to top 250 for FY2024-25 and beyond) already require Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG disclosures with reasonable assurance - the UK budget accelerates the timeline by which Indian companies' Scope 3 data will be demanded by UK-based institutional investors and lenders. Third, Indian companies with UK-listed GDRs, UK institutional shareholders, or UK bank credit facilities will find that their UK counterparties' own Seventh Budget compliance plans will require them to engage, monitor, and in some cases set contractual carbon reduction milestones with Indian suppliers.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian steel and aluminium manufacturers exporting to UK construction, automotive, and infrastructure supply chains - facing embedded carbon scrutiny as UK industry decarbonises under the Seventh Budget's industrial emissions pathway Indian textile, apparel, and leather exporters with UK retail customers - UK brands under UK SDS and CSRD-equivalent rules will demand Scope 3 supplier emission disclosures and reduction roadmaps Indian agri-commodity processors and food exporters - the Seventh Budget's explicit targeting of agriculture and land use emissions in the 2038-2042 window will accelerate UK buyer due diligence on deforestation, methane, and fertiliser-linked emissions in Indian supply chains BSE 500 listed companies with UK institutional shareholders (Schroders, Aviva, Legal & General, HSBC Asset Management) - these investors are now operating under the most stringent domestic statutory carbon obligation in the G7 and will intensify engagement on Indian portfolio company climate targets, BRSR Core disclosures, and SBTi alignment Indian companies raising capital via London Stock Exchange (LSE) or seeking UK Export Finance (UKEF) or British International Investment (BII) funding - carbon budget compliance will tighten ESG due diligence requirements for UK public finance institutions CFOs and Treasurers at Indian conglomerates with UK subsidiaries or JVs - intercompany transfer pricing and consolidated GHG accounting will need to reflect UK subsidiary decarbonisation capex obligations CSOs and Sustainability Heads at Indian listed companies - BRSR Core's Scope 3 disclosure trajectory will increasingly be benchmarked against UK and EU supply chain standards Risk Committees and Audit Committees at Indian banks with UK correspondent banking relationships or UK wholesale funding - climate transition risk in UK-exposed loan books requires updated scenario analysis under RBI's climate risk guidance
sections.key_obligations: UK Seventh Carbon Budget (535 MtCO2e cap): Parliament approval expected H2 2026; delivery plan to follow - Indian companies with UK exposure should begin supply chain carbon mapping now, ahead of UK procurement tightening in 2027-2030 SEBI BRSR Core - Scope 1 & 2 GHG disclosures with reasonable assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24; top 250 from FY2024-25; Scope 3 value chain disclosures currently on a comply-or-explain basis but expected to become mandatory by FY2026-27 per SEBI's stated trajectory UK Sustainability Disclosure Standards (UK SDS): FCA and DESNZ consultation ongoing as of mid-2026; expected mandatory applicability to UK-listed and large UK-registered companies from 2027 - Indian exporters supplying UK corporates should anticipate Scope 3 supplier data requests from 2027 onwards ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures): India has not yet mandated ISSB adoption, but ICAI's Sustainability Standards Board (SSB) is developing India-specific sustainability standards aligned to ISSB - the UK budget's ambition accelerates ISSB's relevance for Indian companies with UK investor or lender exposure EU CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism): While UK-specific CBAM legislation is still under consultation, the Seventh Budget's industrial decarbonisation ambition makes a UK CBAM for steel, aluminium, cement, chemicals, and fertilisers politically and technically more likely by 2028-2030 - Indian exporters in these sectors must begin embedded carbon accounting now India's CCTS (Carbon Credit Trading Scheme) under Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022: Draft rules notified; market launch expected 2026-2027 - the UK's statutory carbon budget precedent will be cited in regulatory impact assessments; Indian companies in energy-intensive sectors should engage BEE on PAT-to-CCTS transition planning SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Indian companies with UK institutional shareholders or UK supply chain customers will face accelerating pressure to submit SBTi targets - CFOs should assess SBTi submission timelines against the UK's 2040 milestone as a reference point for near-term target ambition
sections.climate_angle: The UK's Seventh Carbon Budget is structurally significant for India's transition finance architecture because it confirms that the world's most advanced statutory carbon budget regimes are now moving past power sector decarbonisation into the hard-to-abate sectors - industry, buildings, transport, and agriculture - that represent the core of India's own emissions challenge and export economy. The CCC's sequencing analysis is a roadmap that will be mirrored, with a lag, in India's own policy evolution under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) and India's updated NDC (targeting a 45% emissions intensity reduction by 2030 against 2005 levels and 50% non-fossil electricity capacity). For Indian transition finance, the UK budget reinforces three capital flow trends that CFOs and ESG investors must price. First, UK and European institutional capital will increasingly apply a carbon budget lens to emerging market allocations - Indian companies without credible Scope 1, 2, and 3 reduction pathways aligned to a 1.5°C or well-below-2°C trajectory will face higher cost of capital from UK asset managers operating under Paris-aligned investment mandates. Second, the UK's emphasis on electrification as the primary mechanism - consistent with ISSB IFRS S2's requirement to disclose climate-related transition risks and opportunities - creates a direct commercial opportunity for Indian renewable energy companies, EV manufacturers, and battery storage players seeking UK partnership or investment. Third, the agriculture and land use decarbonisation focus in the Seventh Budget's later phase directly intersects with TNFD's nature-related financial disclosure framework, which is gaining traction with UK institutional investors - Indian agri-business companies with UK exposure will need to demonstrate land use and biodiversity risk management capabilities that go well beyond current BRSR reporting. The BRSR's Principal 6 (environment) disclosures on water, land, and biodiversity are a starting point, but are not yet calibrated to TNFD's LEAP approach or to the granularity UK supply chain due diligence will eventually require.
sections.what_to_do: Sustainability/ESG teams at Indian exporters with UK revenue exposure (>5% of turnover): initiate a Scope 3 Category 11 (use of sold products) and Category 1 (purchased goods/services) mapping exercise within 90 days - UK buyers will begin including carbon data requirements in RFPs from 2027 onwards CFOs at BSE 500 companies with UK institutional shareholders: schedule a climate disclosure readiness review against BRSR Core's current reasonable assurance requirements and map gaps to ISSB IFRS S2 - proactively brief UK investors on your Scope 1/2 trajectory before your next AGM season Board Risk Committees: add UK CBAM watch-item to climate transition risk register - commission a scenario analysis on the financial impact of a UK carbon border levy on your top 3 export product lines, using EU CBAM pricing as a reference scenario CSOs at energy-intensive manufacturers (steel, cement, chemicals, aluminium, textiles): engage BEE now on PAT-to-CCTS transition planning and assess whether your current PAT cycle performance supports an SBTi submission aligned to a well-below-2°C pathway - use the UK's 87% by 2040 milestone as an external benchmark in board discussions on target ambition Procurement and supply chain heads at Indian companies with UK subsidiary operations: audit your UK subsidiary's exposure to Seventh Budget sectoral decarbonisation obligations - particularly in buildings, transport fleet, and industrial energy - and model the capex implications for FY2027-2032 planning cycles Investor Relations teams: proactively disclose in your next sustainability report or BRSR that you are monitoring the UK Seventh Carbon Budget's supply chain implications - this signals climate risk governance maturity to UK and EU ESG-screened funds and differentiates you in a tightening capital allocation environment Company Secretaries and Audit Committee Chairs: ensure that the UK Seventh Carbon Budget and anticipated UK SDS obligations are reflected in your climate-related material risk disclosures under BRSR's Leadership Indicators and, where applicable, under any ISSB-aligned disclosures you are piloting ahead of mandatory adoption
sections.our_take: The UK's Seventh Carbon Budget is a 14-year statutory commitment that will reshape global supply chain carbon economics more profoundly than any single corporate ESG framework update. What boards and investors must watch is not the 2040 headline - it is the 2026-2030 delivery plan that will follow Parliamentary approval. That plan will specify sectoral decarbonisation trajectories, building regulation upgrades, industrial energy transition subsidies, and, critically, the UK's approach to carbon leakage prevention - which is where India's export economy faces its most material near-term exposure. A UK CBAM covering steel, aluminium, cement, and chemicals is no longer a question of if but when, and the Seventh Budget's ambition makes a 2029-2030 UK CBAM introduction politically and technically credible. For India's own climate policy trajectory, I expect the UK's statutory carbon budget model to feature prominently in MoEFCC and CCTS design debates over the next 24 months. India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme will face domestic industry pressure to remain 'flexible,' but the international evidence - 54% UK emissions reduction since 2008, delivered through statutory budget discipline - will be a powerful counter-argument for rigour. SEBI's BRSR trajectory is already moving in this direction: reasonable assurance on Scope 1/2, voluntary Scope 3, and a clear runway toward mandatory value chain disclosures. The UK budget accelerates the timeline by which Indian listed companies will find their BRSR disclosures being stress-tested by UK and European institutional investors against externally validated, Paris-aligned benchmarks. Indian CFOs who treat BRSR as a compliance checkbox rather than a capital markets communication tool are systematically underestimating the repricing risk they carry into the next investment cycle. The boards that invest in transition planning now - before UK procurement standards, UK SDS obligations, and a potential UK CBAM crystallise into contractual and regulatory hard stops - will have a material cost of capital and market access advantage over those that wait for regulatory compulsion.

title: SLR's Acquisition of Anchor Environmental Signals a Global Arms Race in Natural Capital Advisory - Here's What Indian Boards Must Read Into It
summary: SLR Consulting's acquisition of Cape Town-based Anchor Environmental Consultants - reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/slr-acquires-natural-capital-advisory-firm-anchor-environmental-consultants/) - is not merely an M&A footnote. It is a leading indicator that global ESG consulting infra

sections.summary: SLR Consulting's acquisition of Cape Town-based Anchor Environmental Consultants - reported by ESG Today (https://www.esgtoday.com/slr-acquires-natural-capital-advisory-firm-anchor-environmental-consultants/) - is not merely an M&A footnote. It is a leading indicator that global ESG consulting infrastructure is rapidly consolidating around natural capital, marine biodiversity, and ecosystem services valuation capabilities, driven by the hardening of TNFD disclosure expectations and the emergence of nature-as-risk in institutional investment mandates. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at companies with coastal, port, offshore energy, mining, or aquaculture exposure, this signals that natural capital accounting and biodiversity impact assessment are transitioning from voluntary best practice to audit-grade disclosure requirements - and that the specialist advisory capacity to meet those requirements is being locked up by global consulting platforms. Indian companies that delay building internal capability or securing credible external advisory relationships risk being materially under-resourced precisely when regulatory and investor scrutiny peaks.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by ESG Today on 2 June 2026 confirms that SLR has acquired Anchor Environmental Consultants, a 45-person South Africa and Namibia-based specialist firm with deep expertise in marine taxonomy, ecosystem services assessment, natural capital accounting, and ecological-social-economic (ESE) impact evaluation. Anchor's client roster spans governments, international finance institutions (IFIs), mining majors, offshore energy developers, port operators, and aquaculture companies - an almost precise map of the sectors that face the sharpest near-term scrutiny under the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) framework adopted in September 2023 and now being embedded into regulatory architecture globally. The deal follows SLR's acquisitions of climate-analytics platforms Planetrics and ClimSystems, sustainability advisory firm SB&CO, and ESG data provider WAP Sustainability - a deliberate platform-building strategy combining physical climate risk modelling, ESG data infrastructure, corporate sustainability advisory, and now nature and marine science. The strategic logic is unmistakable: SLR is assembling the full-stack capability stack that large-cap companies and project finance lenders will need to satisfy TNFD-aligned disclosures, biodiversity net gain assessments, and natural capital valuation for financial reporting. For India, the implications are concrete and urgent. SEBI's BRSR Core framework - mandatory for the top 150 listed companies by market capitalisation for FY2023-24, expanding to the top 250 for FY2024-25 onwards - already requires disclosure under Principle 6 on biodiversity, ecosystems, and environmental impacts. BRSR Core's Leadership Indicators ask companies about proximity of operations to biodiversity-sensitive areas, impacts on ecosystem services, and biodiversity conservation measures. These disclosures are currently qualitative and self-reported, but SEBI has signalled progressive assurance requirements. Simultaneously, MoEFCC's EIA Notification 2006 and its Draft EIA Notification 2020 (still under deliberation), the Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification 2019, and the Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules 2017 collectively create a regulatory perimeter around natural capital impacts for Indian project developers that is already legally enforceable - but systematically under-disclosed in capital market reporting. The gap between project-level environmental compliance and investor-grade natural capital disclosure is the precise gap that firms like Anchor-SLR are positioning to fill globally, and that gap exists in India at significant scale.
sections.who_is_affected: Port and maritime infrastructure developers: Adani Ports, JSW Infrastructure, and state port trusts with coastal and estuarine project pipelines facing CRZ and EIA compliance plus emerging TNFD disclosure pressure Offshore energy developers: Companies pursuing offshore wind (Gujarat, Tamil Nadu coast), offshore gas (ONGC, Reliance), and green hydrogen/ammonia export projects requiring marine ESIA and natural capital baseline assessments Mining and minerals companies: Coal India, NMDC, Vedanta, Hindalco - operating near forest, wetland, and coastal biodiversity-sensitive zones - facing both MoEFCC Forest Conservation Act compliance and TNFD-linked investor disclosure expectations Aquaculture and fisheries sector: Mid-to-large aquaculture exporters (shrimp processing, marine products) supplying EU and US markets where buyers and lenders are beginning to require TNFD-aligned supply chain disclosures Infrastructure and industrial conglomerates: BSE 500 companies with operations in coastal, riverine, or forested geographies for whom BRSR Core Principle 6 disclosures require ecosystem services impact assessment CFOs and CSOs at top-150 listed companies: Responsible for BRSR Core assurance, now facing the prospect that biodiversity disclosures will require third-party verification aligned to TNFD LEAP methodology ESG-focused institutional investors and DFIs: IFC, ADB, NaBFID, and domestic ESG funds with project finance or equity exposure to coastal or resource-extraction sectors where natural capital risk is a material but unquantified liability Audit committee members and statutory auditors: As SEBI moves toward reasonable assurance on BRSR Core metrics, auditors will need to assess the quality of biodiversity and ecosystem service disclosures - currently an area with almost no Indian audit firm capability
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core - Principle 6 biodiversity disclosures: Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 filings; expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25; limited assurance required on Core KPIs - companies must disclose operations near biodiversity-sensitive areas and impacts on ecosystem services TNFD v1.0 Framework (September 2023): While not yet mandatorily integrated into SEBI rules, TNFD adoption is being tracked by global institutional investors; companies with foreign institutional investor (FII) shareholding above 15-20% face informal disclosure expectations from FY2025-26 onwards MoEFCC EIA Notification 2006 / Draft EIA 2020: Any project requiring environmental clearance in CRZ, forest, or ecologically sensitive zone must conduct biodiversity impact assessment - but this data is rarely translated into investor-grade natural capital accounting CRZ Notification 2019: Coastal development projects must demonstrate no net loss to mangrove, coral, and coastal wetland ecosystems - a natural capital obligation that will increasingly be referenced in ESG due diligence by lenders and equity investors Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules 2017: Operations near notified wetlands require state wetland authority clearance - material for ESG risk disclosure but routinely absent from BRSR or annual report disclosures SEBI's ESG Disclosure Consultation (anticipated FY2026-27): SEBI is expected to consult on aligning BRSR more explicitly with ISSB IFRS S1/S2 and TNFD; companies should anticipate that nature-related financial disclosures will be formally incorporated within a 24-36 month horizon International project finance lenders (IFC Performance Standards, Equator Principles IV): Any project seeking DFI or Equator Principles-aligned financing must already meet IFC PS6 on biodiversity - this is contractually binding today, not aspirational
sections.climate_angle: The SLR-Anchor deal crystallises a critical but underappreciated dimension of India's climate transition: nature and climate are inseparable risk categories, and the infrastructure to manage both is now being consolidated at speed in global consulting markets. India's NDC 2.0 commitments include creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5-3 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent through forests and tree cover - a nature-based solution target that requires precisely the kind of ecosystem services accounting and natural capital valuation that Anchor specialises in. Yet India's domestic capacity in this space - particularly for marine, coastal, and freshwater ecosystems - remains thin and fragmented. For companies pursuing green hydrogen and ammonia export projects along India's western and southern coastlines (a pipeline now exceeding 50 GW in announced capacity), ESIA work for coastal and offshore infrastructure is a non-negotiable regulatory and lender requirement. The overlap with SLR-Anchor's exact project experience - including prior collaboration on green hydrogen/ammonia ESIA in Southern Africa - is direct. Indian project developers partnering with SLR or equivalent global platforms will need to budget for natural capital baseline assessments as a standard project development cost, not a discretionary add-on. More broadly, as ISSB IFRS S2 climate disclosures (which SEBI is expected to use as the basis for future BRSR evolution) explicitly require physical risk assessments that incorporate ecosystem dependencies - water, coastal protection, soil stability - the boundary between climate disclosure and nature disclosure is dissolving. Indian companies that treat these as separate compliance workstreams are building a disclosure architecture that will require costly reconstruction within three to five years. The BRSR-to-TNFD integration is not a question of if, but when, and the companies that begin TNFD LEAP assessments now - identifying their locate, evaluate, assess, and prepare obligations - will be positioned to lead, not scramble.
sections.what_to_do: Sustainability / ESG team (immediate, within 60 days): Conduct a rapid screen of all operating sites and project pipelines against MoEFCC's list of ecologically sensitive zones, CRZ categories, notified wetlands, and proximity to Protected Area networks - map this against current BRSR Principle 6 disclosures to identify gaps before the next annual report cycle CFO / treasury team (within 90 days): Assess all project finance facilities, green bonds, and sustainability-linked loans for IFC Performance Standard 6 and Equator Principles IV covenants - identify where natural capital and biodiversity obligations are contractually binding today and whether current monitoring data satisfies those covenants CSO / Head of Sustainability (within 6 months): Commission a TNFD LEAP pilot assessment for the company's top two or three highest-impact business units - this creates the internal data architecture needed for future BRSR Core assurance on biodiversity metrics and demonstrates proactive governance to ESG-focused institutional investors Board Risk Committee (next quarterly meeting): Add nature-related financial risk as a standing agenda item, distinct from but linked to climate risk - request management to report on ecosystem service dependencies (water, coastal protection, pollination, soil) that are material to business continuity, referencing TNFD and SEBI BRSR Principle 6 as the governance framework Procurement / Supply Chain team (within 6 months): For companies in aquaculture, forestry products, mining, or coastal manufacturing - begin mapping Tier 1 supplier exposure to biodiversity-sensitive geographies, as EU CSRD's ESRS E4 (biodiversity and ecosystems) will apply to EU-market supply chains and will flow upstream to Indian exporters via contractual due diligence requirements by FY2027-28 Investor Relations team (before next AGM): Proactively brief institutional investors - particularly FIIs and ESG-mandated domestic funds - on the company's approach to natural capital risk, referencing TNFD and BRSR Principle 6, to pre-empt shareholder questions and signal governance maturity ahead of SEBI's expected tightening of biodiversity disclosure standards
sections.our_take: The SLR-Anchor acquisition is a small transaction by global M&A standards, but it is a highly legible signal about where ESG consulting infrastructure and regulatory pressure are converging. The global consulting market is quietly but decisively reorganising itself around nature and biodiversity capability - because the regulatory demand signal is now strong enough to justify platform-scale investment. TNFD has achieved in 18 months what TCFD took five years to accomplish: it is being embedded into financial regulation, lender covenants, and investment mandates at a pace that has surprised even its architects. In India, the regulatory hook is already in place - BRSR Principle 6, CRZ, EIA, Wetlands Rules, Forest Conservation Act - but the translation from project-level environmental compliance to investor-grade natural capital disclosure has not happened. That translation is now being forced by three simultaneous pressures: SEBI's progressive assurance requirements on BRSR Core, DFI and green finance lender covenants referencing IFC PS6 and Equator Principles IV, and EU CSRD's extraterritorial reach into Indian export supply chains via ESRS E4. Indian boards should expect that by FY2028, natural capital disclosures will be subject to the same level of third-party assurance scrutiny as Scope 1 and 2 emissions data today - and the companies that have not begun building the internal data systems and external advisory relationships to support that assurance will face the same credibility crisis that unprepared companies faced when BRSR Core GHG disclosures were first required. The competitive advantage in the next phase of ESG maturity in India will not go to companies that comply fastest with minimum disclosure requirements - it will go to companies that convert natural capital data into a genuine strategic asset: lower cost of capital from nature-positive financing instruments, stronger export market access as EU and UK biodiversity due diligence regulations mature, and reduced regulatory risk from proactive MoEFCC and state environmental authority engagement. The SLR-Anchor deal is a prompt for Indian boards to ask a simple question: do we know what our nature-related financial risks are? For most BSE 500 companies, the honest answer is no - and that is exactly the gap that will define the next frontier of ESG accountability in India.

title: NDC Ambition Gap 2025: Why India's Next Climate Pledge Will Define Its Capital Market Credibility, Export Access, and Net Zero Trajectory
summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis - published under the Systems Change Lab project and available at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/ - delivers an unambiguous verdict: current global policies, including India's existing NDC commitmen

sections.summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis - published under the Systems Change Lab project and available at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/ - delivers an unambiguous verdict: current global policies, including India's existing NDC commitments, will achieve less than 1% GHG emissions reduction by 2030 against 2023 baselines, against a scientifically required 42% cut to hold 1.5°C. With the next round of nationally determined contributions due by early 2025, India faces a structural fork in the road: submit a credible, sector-specific NDC that unlocks green capital and preserves export market access, or risk accelerating sovereign climate risk downgrades, carbon border exposure, and SBTi non-alignment penalties that are already beginning to cascade through institutional investor mandates. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, the immediate operational consequence is this: whatever sectoral targets India embeds in its 2025 NDC will become the regulatory scaffolding against which BRSR Core disclosures, BEE PAT cycle targets, CPCB compliance trajectories, and investor net zero alignment assessments are benchmarked over the next decade. This is not a distant policy event - it is the reference frame being set right now.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/), authored by a 14-member team including Sophie Boehm, Kelly Levin, and Joel Jaeger under the Systems Change Lab project, provides the most granular public breakdown yet of what 1.5°C-aligned NDCs must contain - sector by sector, with quantified 2030 and 2035 benchmarks. The key numbers: a 42% global GHG reduction by 2030 and 56% by 2035, both relative to 2023 levels. Today's full policy implementation trajectory delivers less than 1% by either milestone year. Even full implementation of current NDCs puts the world on a 2.6-2.8°C warming pathway. What this overturns is the comfortable narrative that incremental NDC upgrades and long-term net zero pledges are sufficient. The analysis makes explicit that without a 'quantum leap in ambition' in the 2025 NDC round - backed by timebound, quantitative, sector-specific targets for power, transport, land use, industry, and buildings - the 1.5°C window closes irreversibly before 2035. For India, whose NDC submitted in 2022 committed to 45% emissions intensity reduction by 2030 (against 2005 levels) and 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil sources by 2030, the 2025 round demands a material step-up: absolute economy-wide targets for 2035, sector-specific EV sales benchmarks, renewable energy deployment floors, and methane reduction timelines aligned with the Global Methane Pledge India has not yet formally endorsed. The India-specific regulatory consequence is direct: SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 and expanding to the top 1,000 by FY2026-27, already requires Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG intensity disclosure with third-party assurance. ISSB IFRS S2 - which MCA's sustainability reporting roadmap is expected to reference - requires companies to disclose alignment with jurisdictional climate targets. If India's 2025 NDC raises the ambition bar with sector-specific 2035 targets, it automatically tightens the compliance and disclosure baseline for every listed Indian company operating in power, cement, steel, chemicals, automotive, and logistics sectors.
sections.who_is_affected: Power sector: IPPs, coal-dependent utilities, and RE developers - NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, JSW Energy - whose capex plans will be benchmarked against any 2035 power sector decarbonisation target in the new NDC Hard-to-abate industries: Steel (Tata Steel, JSW Steel, SAIL), cement (UltraTech, ACC, Ambuja), and petrochemicals (Reliance, ONGC) facing SBTi Science-Based Target setting timelines and potential EU CBAM exposure if India's NDC fails to credibly reflect sectoral transition Automotive OEMs and component suppliers: Maruti Suzuki, Mahindra, Tata Motors, Bosch India - EV sales benchmarks in the NDC will directly influence CAFE norms, PLI scheme continuity, and EU EV export market access Export-oriented manufacturers in textiles, chemicals, leather, and agri-processing supplying EU buyers under CSRD/ESRS supply chain due diligence obligations, where India's NDC ambition signals sovereign transition credibility CFOs and Heads of Treasury at BSE 500 companies accessing green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, or foreign institutional investment - NDC credibility is a direct input to ESG ratings (MSCI, Sustainalytics, S&P CSA) which drive FII allocation decisions Chief Sustainability Officers and BRSR compliance leads responsible for FY2025-26 disclosures requiring Scope 3 ambition statements and transition plan outlines under evolving SEBI guidance Board Risk Committees with fiduciary exposure to stranded asset risk in fossil fuel-linked balance sheets and climate litigation risk if transition planning is demonstrably misaligned with science Institutional investors - domestic MFs, insurance funds under IRDAI's ESG disclosure framework, pension funds, and FIIs with Paris-aligned mandates - reassessing India sovereign and corporate climate risk premiums
sections.key_obligations: India NDC 2025 submission: Due by early 2025 (UNFCCC deadline approximately February 2025) - MoEFCC and MoF coordination required; expected to include enhanced 2035 targets and sector-specific benchmarks following COP29 Baku signals BRSR Core third-party assured GHG disclosures: Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 (already in effect); expanding to top 1,000 companies by FY2026-27 - Scope 1 and Scope 2 intensity metrics must be reported with limited assurance BEE PAT Cycle VIII targets (2023-2026): Designated Consumers in power, cement, steel, aluminium, fertiliser, pulp & paper, chlor-alkali, and textiles must meet specific energy consumption norms - NDC sectoral targets will inform Cycle IX design post-2026 EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM): Transition phase reporting live from October 2023; definitive pricing phase from January 2026 - Indian exporters of steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and hydrogen must track embedded carbon against EU benchmarks now tied to NDC credibility signals SBTi Corporate Net Zero Standard: Companies with SBTi commitments must submit near-term targets (42-46% Scope 1+2 reduction by 2030 from a base year) within 24 months of commitment - India's NDC 2035 target provides the jurisdictional reference baseline SBTi uses for FLAG and ISIC-aligned target validation ISSB IFRS S2 adoption: MCA sustainability standards roadmap (expected consultation 2025) will align Indian reporting with ISSB S2 climate scenario analysis, transition plan, and physical risk disclosure requirements - NDC sector targets become the policy scenario anchor SEBI ESG rating provider framework (SEBI circular CIR/2023): ESG Rating Providers must incorporate sovereign climate commitment credibility into methodology - a weak India NDC 2025 directly depresses Indian corporate ESG ratings through the sovereign anchor effect
sections.climate_angle: The Climate Action Tracker analysis makes visible a structural problem Indian boards must internalise: the gap between current policy trajectories and 1.5°C is not a linear shortfall that time and incremental improvement will close. It is a compounding credibility deficit that, if unaddressed in the 2025 NDC round, will trigger a cascade of capital market consequences over the 2025-2030 window. Specifically, ISSB IFRS S2 requires companies to assess climate scenarios consistent with the goals of the Paris Agreement - if India's NDC signals a 2.6-2.8°C national pathway, multinational institutional investors using physical risk models (NGFS scenarios, IPCC AR6) will reprice Indian infrastructure, agriculture, and coastal real estate assets upward on physical risk and downward on transition readiness simultaneously. For Indian companies on SBTi pathways, the 2025 NDC round matters because SBTi's Country Pathway Tool and FLAG (Forest, Land, and Agriculture Guidance) use NDC sector benchmarks as policy context for target validation. A stronger NDC with explicit 2035 power sector and land-use targets would make it easier - not harder - for Indian steel, cement, and agri-business companies to set credible near-term SBTi targets without being penalised for sector-level ambiguity. Conversely, a weak NDC increases the probability that SBTi and CDP frameworks classify Indian sectoral pathways as misaligned, triggering disclosure flags that FIIs and ESG-screened funds use to underweight Indian equities. India's transition to a green industrial economy - via the PLI scheme for solar, battery storage, green hydrogen, and EV manufacturing - requires a credible NDC as its sovereign-level anchor to attract the USD 2.5 trillion in climate finance the country needs through 2030, per the International Energy Agency's India Energy Outlook.
sections.what_to_do: CFO / Treasury (immediate, Q1 2025): Map your company's Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions intensity trajectory against both India's current 2022 NDC benchmarks AND the 1.5°C-aligned sector pathways quantified by Climate Action Tracker - identify the delta and stress-test your green bond covenants and SLL pricing step-downs against the more ambitious scenario Chief Sustainability Officer (within 60 days): Conduct a sector-specific NDC scenario analysis: if India submits a 2025 NDC with explicit 2035 power, transport, or industry targets, what does that mean for your BEE PAT obligations in Cycle IX, your BRSR Core intensity targets, and your SBTi validation timeline? Present the gap analysis to the board risk committee before FY2024-25 annual report sign-off Board Risk Committee (Q1 2025 board meeting): Commission a formal climate scenario analysis under ISSB IFRS S2 framework - use the NGFS 'Delayed Transition' and 'Net Zero 2050' scenarios with India-specific policy assumptions bracketed around the NDC 2025 outcome - this is both fiduciary prudence and pre-emptive preparation for MCA's forthcoming ISSB-aligned standards Export-oriented businesses (steel, aluminium, chemicals, textiles) - Head of Regulatory Affairs: Engage immediately with CBAM reporting infrastructure - the current CBAM transition phase requires quarterly embedded carbon reports; model the cost impact under both a weak and strong India NDC scenario, since EU benchmark evolution is tied to member state NDC credibility assessments IR and ESG Communications teams (before AGM season, April-May 2025): Proactively address the NDC ambition gap in investor engagement materials - institutional investors with Paris-aligned mandates will ask direct questions about your company's resilience to a 2.6°C versus 1.5°C policy pathway; have a quantified answer ready, not boilerplate language Procurement and supply chain heads (within 90 days): Initiate Scope 3 Category 1 and Category 11 (use of sold products) baseline assessments - as India's NDC raises sectoral ambition, downstream and upstream supply chain emissions will become material BRSR Core disclosures and CSRD-driven buyer requirements simultaneously; the window to establish baselines before these become hard obligations is closing
sections.our_take: The 2025 NDC round is the single most consequential climate policy event of this decade for Indian business, and it is receiving a fraction of the boardroom attention it deserves. Here is what I expect to unfold over the next 12-36 months. First, India will submit an enhanced NDC in early 2025 that raises the absolute emissions reduction ambition modestly and includes sector-specific signals - particularly on renewable energy capacity, EV sales penetration, and potentially green hydrogen production targets - but will likely stop short of an absolute economy-wide 2035 GHG cap. This will be received by international climate finance bodies as 'improved but insufficient,' which will sustain India's 'Highly Insufficient' CAT rating and create a persistent sovereign climate credibility discount that institutional ESG frameworks will price into Indian corporate ratings. Second, the EU's CBAM definitive phase beginning January 2026 will accelerate a reckoning for Indian heavy industry: companies in steel, cement, and fertilisers that have not invested in transition credibility - measured partly through NDC alignment and SBTi commitments - will face compounding disadvantage in EU export markets just as domestic BEE PAT Cycle IX tightens energy norms post-2026. The companies that are already running 1.5°C scenario analysis, have third-party assured BRSR Core data, and have initiated SBTi target-setting will be able to demonstrate transition readiness to both regulators and capital providers. Those that have not will face the twin pressure of higher cost of capital and shrinking export market access simultaneously. Third, and most importantly for boards: MCA's sustainability reporting standards, expected for consultation in 2025 and aligned with ISSB IFRS S1/S2, will require Indian listed companies to disclose transition plans - which are, by definition, anchored to government climate policy. A company whose transition plan references India's NDC as its policy context will need that NDC to be credible, quantified, and sector-specific to withstand audit scrutiny and investor challenge. The boards and CFOs who are building their transition plans now, using the 1.5°C-aligned sector benchmarks that Climate Action Tracker has quantified, are not just doing good ESG - they are building audit-defensible, investor-grade climate disclosures ahead of a regulatory wave that will arrive whether India's NDC is bold or timid. The gap between action and ambition is not just a climate problem. For Indian business in 2025, it is a capital markets problem.

title: Fossil Fuel Export Emissions: The Accountability Gap That Threatens India's Climate Commitments and Supply Chain Credibility
summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis exposes a structural accountability gap in global climate governance: the top fossil fuel exporting nations - Russia, Australia, the US, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada - account for 50% of total exported fossil fuel emissions, yet these emissions appear

sections.summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis exposes a structural accountability gap in global climate governance: the top fossil fuel exporting nations - Russia, Australia, the US, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada - account for 50% of total exported fossil fuel emissions, yet these emissions appear in no national inventory and no corporate Scope 3 accounting obligation. For India, this is not an abstract geopolitical debate. India is one of the world's largest importers of coal (from Indonesia and Australia) and oil (from Russia and Saudi Arabia), meaning the 'exported emissions' of these nations are India's Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions in waiting - embedded in the energy and industrial base of every BSE 500 company. The immediate consequence for Indian CFOs and CSOs is a compounding disclosure risk: as ISSB IFRS S2 adoption accelerates globally and SEBI's BRSR Core Scope 3 disclosures tighten, the combustion-side emissions of imported fossil fuels will become a mandatory reporting obligation - exposing Indian corporates to stranded asset risk, SBTi target misalignment, and investor scrutiny that their export-nation counterparts are currently escaping.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/highlighting-the-hypocrisy-fossil-fuel-export-emissions/), authored by Sofia Gonzales-Zuniga, Danial Riaz, and Mia Moisio, quantifies for the first time the scale of what CAT terms the 'fossil fuel exports iceberg effect': countries like Norway and Australia have exported emissions that exceed their entire domestic greenhouse gas inventory. Australia's exported emissions are more than double its domestic footprint. Canada has had exported emissions exceeding domestic emissions since 2015. The US, meanwhile, has significantly grown its exported emissions over the past decade and - per US EIA 2023 projections - plans to increase oil and gas exports to 2050. What this analysis overturns is the comfortable diplomatic fiction that COP28's 'transition away from fossil fuels' language represents a coherent global decarbonisation pathway. It does not. The GHG Protocol's territorial accounting boundary means that Scope 1 emissions from Australian coal combustion in an Indian steel plant appear in India's national inventory and in Indian steelmakers' Scope 1 disclosures - not in Australia's. This is not merely a moral asymmetry; it is a live regulatory and capital market risk for Indian industry. Under SEBI's BRSR Core framework, top 150 listed entities (by market capitalisation) are already required to disclose Scope 3 emissions on a reasonable assurance basis for FY2024-25 onward. For energy-intensive sectors - steel, cement, power, chemicals - Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods and services) and Category 3 (fuel and energy-related activities) will directly capture the embedded emissions of imported coal, LNG, and crude oil. The CAT analysis makes clear that the upstream supply of these fuels is not being decarbonised: Indian companies cannot rely on supplier-side action to reduce their Scope 3 numbers. The India-specific regulatory hook deepens further: India's Perform Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme under the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) sets specific energy consumption reduction targets for designated consumers, many of whom are large importers of thermal coal and fuel oil. If the PAT scheme evolves - as expected under India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified in June 2023 - into a carbon price-linked offset mechanism, the emissions intensity of imported fuels will directly affect the cost of compliance credits. Companies importing high-emission-intensity coal from Australia or Indonesia will face structurally higher compliance costs compared to peers transitioning to domestic renewable energy or cleaner fuel mixes.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian steel manufacturers (primary steel via blast furnace route) importing coking and thermal coal from Australia and Indonesia - direct Scope 1 and Scope 3 Category 1 exposure Indian power generation companies (thermal IPPs and state DISCOMs) relying on imported coal from Indonesia, South Africa, and Australia for capacity balancing - exposed to fuel cost volatility and future carbon border adjustments Indian oil refiners and petrochemical companies importing Russian crude under current pricing arrangements - Scope 3 Category 3 exposure and potential EU CBAM indirect risk if refined products are exported to European markets Top 150 SEBI-listed companies by market cap required to disclose Scope 3 under BRSR Core from FY2024-25 - audit committees face assurance risk if upstream emission factors for imported fossil fuels are not accurately sourced CFOs and treasury heads at energy-intensive BSE 500 companies negotiating long-term fuel supply contracts - need to price in forward carbon cost of imported fossil fuel emissions CSOs and sustainability heads at Indian conglomerates with SBTi near-term or net-zero commitments - Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 targets will be unachievable without a credible imported fuel decarbonisation pathway ESG analysts and institutional investors at domestic mutual funds, foreign portfolio investors (FPIs), and development finance institutions (DFIs) with exposure to Indian energy and industrials - need to reassess Scope 3 transition risk embedded in portfolio companies Export-oriented Indian manufacturers (textiles, auto components, chemicals) supplying EU buyers subject to CSRD - upstream Scope 3 disclosure requirements from EU customers will cascade to Indian suppliers' own fuel and energy emission disclosures
