sections.summary: Carbon Tracker's February 2024 report on Snam - Europe's largest gas transmission infrastructure operator - delivers a systemic warning that energy transition is rendering future cash flows from gas transport and distribution structurally uncertain, with asset life assumptions, hydrogen decarbonisation narratives, and regulatory frameworks failing to adequately reflect Paris-aligned scenarios. For Indian stakeholders, this is not a European story: it is a direct preview of the stranded asset and disclosure risk trajectory facing India's gas infrastructure sector - GAIL, Gujarat Gas, Indraprastha Gas, Mahanagar Gas, Petronet LNG - and the institutional investors who hold their equity and bonds. With SEBI's BRSR Core mandating climate-related financial disclosures aligned to ISSB IFRS S2 for India's top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25, the transition risk valuation methodology Carbon Tracker applies to Snam is the same analytical lens that global asset managers and proxy advisors will shortly apply to Indian gas utilities.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Carbon Tracker (https://carbontracker.org/reports/decarbonising-gas-networks-part-1-snam/), published 08 February 2024, forms Part 1 of a three-report series covering Snam, Italgas, and Enagas - the three major European listed gas network companies whose stocks and bonds are embedded in European infrastructure and utility fund portfolios. The analytical framework Carbon Tracker deploys is instructive: it assesses Paris alignment of each operator's decarbonisation pathway, the credibility of hydrogen retrofit narratives as a transition strategy, the appropriateness of existing asset economic lives given declining gas demand projections, and whether national regulators are building sufficient decarbonisation incentives into the tariff and concession frameworks that underpin these companies' regulated asset base (RAB) valuations. The core finding - that gas network decarbonisation is activity-dependent (transmission versus distribution) and geography-dependent, and that hydrogen as a like-for-like gas substitute is increasingly challenged as a mass network decarbonisation pathway - has direct structural parallels in India. India's City Gas Distribution (CGD) sector, regulated under PNGRB, is mid-build across 295 Geographical Areas (GAs) under the 9th and 10th CGD bidding rounds. The network economic life assumptions embedded in PNGRB's tariff authorisations and the capital work-in-progress (CWIP) on the balance sheets of IGL, MGL, Gujarat Gas and their peers reflect a demand trajectory that has not been stress-tested against India's own net zero by 2070 commitment or the IEA's Net Zero Emissions by 2050 scenario. Carbon Tracker's methodology - shortening asset economic lives, stress-testing RAB against demand scenarios, and requiring quantified methane leak reduction commitments - is precisely the analytical lens that ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis (paragraph 18, disclosure of climate-related risks over short, medium, and long-term horizons) will require Indian gas utilities to apply and disclose under BRSR Core. The report also highlights that investor engagement with gas network operators must extend beyond company-level action to regulatory advocacy - companies must actively push national regulators to reform frameworks to incentivise decarbonisation. In India's context, this means GAIL, Gujarat Gas, and Petronet LNG boards should be preparing to engage PNGRB and MoPNG on whether the CGD regulatory framework - currently designed to incentivise network rollout, not network managed decline or hydrogen transition - is fit for a 2070 net zero pathway. This is a board governance question, not merely a technical one.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian City Gas Distribution companies: Indraprastha Gas (IGL), Mahanagar Gas (MGL), Gujarat Gas, Adani Total Gas - mid-rollout on 9th and 10th CGD round networks, with long asset life assumptions embedded in balance sheets and tariff orders Gas transmission and processing majors: GAIL India, Petronet LNG, GSPL - with pipeline RAB, LNG terminal capex, and long-term offtake commitments exposed to demand scenario risk Institutional investors and debt holders: Infrastructure debt funds, insurance companies (LIC, GIC Re), and pension funds with India CGD equity or NCD exposure; ESG-screened funds benchmarking against MSCI ESG or FTSE4Good indices where stranded asset methodology is being tightened CFOs and treasury teams at listed gas utilities preparing BRSR Core disclosures for FY2024-25 under SEBI's mandatory ISSB-aligned climate scenario analysis requirements Chief Sustainability Officers at BSE 500 energy companies where Scope 1 methane emissions from gas infrastructure will come under increasing scrutiny from CDP, SBTi Oil & Gas pathway, and proxy advisors (ISS, Glass Lewis) Board risk committees at Indian gas sector companies where the intersection of regulated asset base valuation, Paris-aligned scenario analysis, and audit committee sign-off on climate-related financial disclosures creates a new governance exposure Export-facing Indian energy companies and project developers where EU Taxonomy alignment and CBAM-adjacent green finance criteria require demonstration that gas assets are transition-credible or time-limited
sections.key_obligations: SEBI BRSR Core - FY2024-25 (mandatory for top 150 listed companies by market cap): Climate-related financial disclosures including Scope 1, 2, 3 GHG emissions, transition risk assessment, and scenario analysis aligned to ISSB IFRS S2 - gas utilities must now disclose the financial materiality of demand scenario risk on asset valuations ISSB IFRS S2 (effective for reporting periods beginning on or after 01 January 2024): Paragraph 18 requires disclosure of climate-related risks and opportunities over short, medium, and long-term horizons - Indian companies voluntarily adopting or compelled through BRSR Core alignment must stress-test gas network asset lives against 1.5°C and 2°C scenarios GHG Protocol / CDP disclosure: Methane fugitive emissions from gas transmission and distribution must be quantified and disclosed under Scope 1 - CPCB's draft methane monitoring guidelines and India's updated UNFCCC NDC both reference improved methane accounting; CDP Oil & Gas questionnaire 2024 explicitly asks for methane intensity targets SBTi Oil & Gas pathway (applicable to GAIL, ONGC, and gas-sector companies with SBTi commitments or under investor pressure to commit): Requires absolute Scope 1+2 reduction targets and methane intensity targets aligned to IEA NZE scenario - asset life and demand assumptions are implicit in any credible SBTi submission PNGRB CGD network regulatory review: While no formal deadline exists, the Carbon Tracker methodology signals that RAB-based valuations and concession period assumptions in CGD GAs must be reviewed for Paris alignment - boards should proactively engage PNGRB before investor pressure forces reactive disclosure EU Green Bond Standard and SFDR Article 9 fund eligibility: Indian gas infrastructure bonds or green bonds sold into European markets will face increased scrutiny on Paris alignment, asset life assumptions, and hydrogen transition credibility - CFOs planning ECB or Masala Bond issuance in 2024-25 must pre-empt this
sections.climate_angle: Carbon Tracker's Snam analysis crystallises the central tension in gas infrastructure globally: network operators are stranded between the physical reality of continued gas demand in the near term and the financial reality that long-duration gas network assets - pipelines with 40-60 year economic lives, LNG regasification terminals with 25-30 year amortisation schedules, CGD network steel embedded in urban geographies - are being valued on demand assumptions that are inconsistent with Paris-aligned scenarios. In India, this tension is acutely visible: the government's Gas Vision 2030 targets raising natural gas's share of the primary energy mix from approximately 6% to 15%, while simultaneously India's NDC commits to achieving net zero by 2070 and 50% non-fossil electricity capacity by 2030. These two policy vectors are not currently reconciled in any publicly disclosed scenario analysis by any major Indian gas utility. The hydrogen dimension is particularly important for India. Snam has heavily promoted hydrogen blending and hydrogen-ready infrastructure as a decarbonisation narrative - Carbon Tracker's analysis interrogates whether this is Paris-credible or a form of transition risk obfuscation. Indian gas majors and the government's National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM), which targets 5 MMTPA of green hydrogen by 2030, face the same credibility test. If green hydrogen at scale for gas network blending remains technically and commercially constrained (as Carbon Tracker argues for Europe), then the transition narrative underpinning Indian CGD network expansion capex requires much harder scrutiny - both from audit committees signing off on impairment assessments and from investors evaluating BRSR Core climate disclosures. For India's capital markets, the stranded asset risk embedded in gas network balance sheets connects directly to the RBI's climate risk framework (RBI Discussion Paper on Climate Risk, July 2022) and the expected incorporation of climate scenario analysis into bank credit risk assessments. Lenders to GAIL, IGL, Gujarat Gas, and Petronet LNG - SBI, HDFC Bank, Axis Bank - will increasingly be required to assess whether loan books to gas infrastructure are consistent with 1.5°C or 2°C transition pathways. The Carbon Tracker methodology is the international template for that assessment.
sections.what_to_do: CFOs at IGL, MGL, Gujarat Gas, GAIL, Petronet LNG: Commission an independent asset life stress-test of your gas network RAB and CWIP against IEA NZE 2050 and IEA STEPS scenarios - this is both an ISSB IFRS S2 compliance requirement under BRSR Core FY2024-25 and a proactive defence against activist investor or proxy advisor challenge; timeline: Q1 FY2025-26 at latest Chief Sustainability Officers at BSE 500 gas and energy utilities: Map your Scope 1 methane fugitive emissions against GHG Protocol Oil and Gas Sector guidance and CDP 2024 Oil & Gas questionnaire requirements - quantify methane intensity ratios and set time-bound reduction targets before your next AGM season; connect this to SBTi Oil & Gas pathway if your company is under investor pressure to commit Board Risk Committees at gas infrastructure companies: Add 'Paris alignment of gas network asset life and demand assumptions' as a standing agenda item under climate-related financial risk - require management to present scenario analysis results and compare against Carbon Tracker's Snam methodology; this is a governance best practice that proxy advisors (ISS, Glass Lewis) will increasingly score Investor Relations teams at listed gas utilities: Proactively engage your top 10 institutional shareholders - particularly any ESG-screened funds or European asset managers with SFDR Article 8/9 mandates - to brief them on your climate scenario analysis work and your regulatory engagement strategy with PNGRB and MoPNG; do not wait for a Carbon Tracker-style report on your company to force reactive disclosure Treasury and finance teams planning green bond or ECB issuance: Engage your international legal counsel and ESG rating advisors now to assess whether your gas infrastructure assets meet EU Green Bond Standard or Climate Bonds Initiative (CBI) certification thresholds - the Carbon Tracker methodology is being used by EU fund managers to challenge gas infrastructure green bond eligibility Legal and compliance teams: Review impairment testing assumptions for gas network assets in your next annual accounts - auditors (Big Four, in particular) are beginning to challenge whether climate scenario analysis is adequately reflected in IND AS 36 impairment assessments; the Carbon Tracker report creates a public analytical benchmark that auditors can cite
sections.our_take: Carbon Tracker's Snam report is not primarily about Snam. It is the opening salvo in what will become a systematic, methodology-driven reassessment of gas infrastructure equity and debt valuations globally - and India's listed gas sector is squarely in the trajectory. The analytical tools Carbon Tracker deploys - Paris alignment scoring, asset life stress-testing, hydrogen transition credibility assessment, regulatory framework adequacy review - are the same tools that ISSB IFRS S2, BRSR Core, and increasingly RBI's climate risk guidelines are building into mandatory disclosure and credit assessment architecture. What is voluntary institutional investor engagement in Europe today becomes mandatory disclosure in India within 12-24 months. The boards of India's gas utilities face a compound governance challenge that is not yet fully visible in their boardrooms: they are mid-cycle on massive capex rollout (CGD rounds 9 and 10, GAIL's pipeline expansion, Petronet's LNG terminal capacity additions) based on demand assumptions and asset life conventions that have not been publicly stress-tested against India's own NDC commitments, let alone a 1.5°C scenario. When Indian institutional investors - LIC, EPFO, NPS trust funds - are required to incorporate climate risk into their asset allocation and stewardship frameworks (which RBI and SEBI are progressively mandating), the gas sector will face a repricing event unless companies have pre-emptively built credible, Paris-aligned transition narratives backed by quantified commitments. Watch for three developments in the next 24-36 months: first, a Carbon Tracker or IEEFA equivalent analysis targeting Indian CGD and gas transmission companies by name - GAIL's pipeline RAB and IGL's network asset life assumptions are analytically vulnerable on the same dimensions as Snam's; second, SEBI tightening BRSR Core to explicitly require asset-level climate scenario analysis for energy infrastructure companies, mirroring ISSB IFRS S2's sector-specific requirements; and third, EU CBAM-adjacent green finance criteria and supply chain due diligence requirements (CSDDD) creating a de facto Paris alignment test for any Indian gas sector company seeking European capital or operating in European supply chains. Companies that treat this as a future compliance problem rather than a present capital strategy problem will find themselves on the wrong side of a valuation gap that, once visible to markets, is very difficult to close.

title: Uniper's State-Owned Decarbonisation Model: What Germany's Coal Exit Strategy Signals for India's PSU Power Sector and Transition Finance
summary: Carbon Tracker's July 2024 report on Uniper SE (available at https://carbontracker.org/reports/uniper-se-government-ownership-key-to-decarbonisation/) poses a question that Indian policymakers and capital market participants cannot afford to ignore: can government ownership of a coal-heavy utility b

sections.summary: Carbon Tracker's July 2024 report on Uniper SE (available at https://carbontracker.org/reports/uniper-se-government-ownership-key-to-decarbonisation/) poses a question that Indian policymakers and capital market participants cannot afford to ignore: can government ownership of a coal-heavy utility be the decisive lever for accelerating decarbonisation, rather than an obstacle to it? For India, where NTPC, NLCIL, and SJVN are state-controlled, BSE-listed utilities sitting at the centre of both coal dependency and the clean energy buildout, this framing has direct and urgent implications for transition finance, BRSR Core disclosures, and the credibility of India's 2070 net zero commitment. The report's central finding - that Uniper's pathway to net zero is inseparable from the German federal government's ownership stake and policy mandate - reframes the standard ESG investor complaint about state ownership diluting governance and decarbonisation ambition. In the Indian context, it raises an equally uncomfortable inversion: state ownership of coal assets, without an explicit policy-linked decarbonisation mandate and transparent Scope 1 and Scope 3 target architecture, may be the single largest source of stranded asset risk sitting inside domestic institutional investor portfolios today.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Carbon Tracker (carbontracker.org), published 1 July 2024, presents paired corporate research on RWE and Uniper - Germany's two dominant publicly traded coal utilities - assessing their readiness to deliver Germany's accelerated coal exit before 2030. The Uniper report specifically interrogates whether continued German federal government ownership (the government took a ~99% stake in Uniper during the 2022 energy crisis bailout before partial re-privatisation) is a structural advantage or liability for decarbonisation. Carbon Tracker's conclusion is nuanced but directionally clear: government ownership, when paired with explicit policy mandates, interim emissions targets, and capital allocation discipline, can accelerate transition outcomes that private shareholders alone would not enforce - particularly where coal assets are politically entangled with energy security narratives. The report identifies three critical gaps in Uniper's current transition posture that are strikingly familiar in the Indian PSU power context: first, the absence of interim Scope 3 emissions targets (Carbon Tracker flags Uniper's ambition on Scope 3 as insufficient); second, the risk that gas capacity modernisation and new-build programmes crowd out renewables capital expenditure; and third, the structural question of whether declining coal cashflows can fund a credible renewables capex programme without government-backed debt capacity. Each of these three failure modes maps almost exactly onto the disclosure and strategic gaps visible in NTPC's and NLCIL's current BRSR and sustainability reporting. NTPC's FY2024 BRSR Core disclosures, for instance, do not present a Paris-aligned Scope 3 target for upstream coal procurement or downstream power offtake emissions - a gap that Carbon Tracker explicitly calls out as a governance red flag in the Uniper context. The India-specific regulatory hook is direct: SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed entities from FY2024 and expanding to the top 250 by FY2025, now requires third-party assured disclosures on Scope 1, 2, and - for certain indicators - Scope 3 emissions. PSU power utilities listed on BSE/NSE are inside this perimeter and are currently the weakest link in the assured disclosure chain.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian PSU power utilities listed on BSE/NSE: NTPC Ltd, NLCIL (NLC India), SJVN, Damodar Valley Corporation (where listed), Neyveli Lignite - all holding material coal generation assets alongside renewable buildout mandates Private Indian coal and gas IPPs with institutional equity or debt exposure: Adani Power, Torrent Power, JSW Energy - particularly those with international bond issuances or CSRD-scope European offtake counterparties Domestic institutional investors - LIC, SBI Mutual Fund, EPFO-linked funds - with large passive and active positions in BSE 500 power sector stocks, now facing stranded asset concentration risk as transition timelines compress Foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) and ESG-labelled funds with India power sector exposure, subject to SFDR Article 8/9 compliance and EU Taxonomy alignment requirements that will increasingly penalise undisclosed coal transition risk CFOs and CSOs of top-150 listed Indian companies under BRSR Core assured disclosure obligation, especially those in energy-intensive sectors with coal-linked Scope 2 and Scope 3 exposure through grid electricity or captive coal power India's Ministry of Power, MoEFCC, and MNRE - as policy architects of the Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) implementation framework, where the Uniper government-ownership model offers a directly applicable governance template Green bond and sustainability-linked bond (SLB) structurers and underwriters - given that Uniper's debt capacity question for renewables capex is a live issue for NTPC's Rs 15,000+ crore green bond programme and similar instruments in the Indian market
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core assured disclosures - Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions with third-party assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed Indian companies from FY2023-24 reporting cycle (filed by September 2024); expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25 BRSR Core - Scope 3 emissions disclosure: currently a 'leadership indicator' (voluntary but disclosed) under SEBI's BRSR framework; Carbon Tracker's Uniper finding strengthens the case for SEBI to elevate Scope 3 to a core assured indicator in the next BRSR revision cycle, expected 2025-26 SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Indian companies with SBTi commitments (currently ~85 Indian companies have targets set or committed) must demonstrate Scope 3 coverage exceeding 67% of total emissions - a threshold most Indian power utilities with upstream coal procurement have not yet met ISSB IFRS S2 - Climate-related disclosures: while not yet mandated in India, SEBI has explicitly signalled alignment with ISSB as the interoperability baseline for future BRSR evolution; companies preparing voluntary ISSB S2 disclosures should treat Carbon Tracker's Scope 3 ambition critique as a direct indicator gap India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022: BEE's draft regulations for the compliance carbon market are expected to be notified in 2024-25; PSU coal utilities will be obligated participants, making transition capex planning and coal exit timelines financially material immediately PAT Scheme Cycle VII (BEE): Designated Consumers in the power sector face revised energy intensity reduction targets; coal plant efficiency improvements are increasingly cost-competitive with transition capex deferral, and Carbon Tracker's 'modernise existing before building new gas' recommendation applies directly here
sections.climate_angle: The Uniper report crystallises a transition finance dilemma that is arriving in Indian capital markets faster than most boards have modelled: as coal cashflows decline - driven by carbon pricing, renewable cost deflation, and policy-mandated capacity retirement - the internal capital generation capacity of coal utilities to self-fund renewables capex deteriorates precisely when the capex requirement is largest. Germany's answer for Uniper was a government-backed balance sheet and explicit policy ownership of the transition mandate. India's answer, as currently structured, is far less clear. NTPC's stated goal of 60 GW of renewable capacity by 2032 requires capital expenditure of approximately Rs 2.5-3 lakh crore over eight years - against a balance sheet that still derives the majority of its regulated equity returns from coal-based generation. Without an explicit government-backed debt facility or policy-linked transition finance mechanism (analogous to what the German government enabled for Uniper), NTPC's renewables capex programme faces the same crowding-out risk that Carbon Tracker identifies as structurally unresolved at Uniper. For Indian institutional investors, the BRSR and ISSB S2 lens makes this a disclosure and portfolio risk question, not merely a policy one. IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose climate-related transition risks that are reasonably expected to affect cash flows, financing, and strategy over the short, medium, and long term. A PSU power utility that discloses coal asset retirement schedules without modelling the capex funding gap - or that reports Scope 1 targets without Scope 3 coverage of coal procurement - is producing a materially incomplete climate risk disclosure under any credible international standard. As India's BRSR Core framework converges toward ISSB S2 interoperability (a direction SEBI has publicly endorsed in its 2023 consultation paper), this gap will become an audit and assurance exposure, not merely a best-practice gap. The physical risk angle is equally live: India's coal-producing regions in Jharkhand, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh face compound climate risk - extreme heat stress, water scarcity affecting thermal plant cooling, and monsoon variability impacting coal logistics - that is not currently modelled or disclosed in PSU utility BRSR filings. TNFD's LEAP methodology, now referenced in SEBI's proposed nature-related disclosure guidance, would require exactly this site-level physical risk assessment for coal asset portfolios.
sections.what_to_do: CFOs of top-150 listed power and energy companies: commission an independent Scope 3 gap analysis against GHG Protocol Category 1 (purchased coal/fuel) and Category 11 (use of sold energy) before the FY2024-25 BRSR Core filing - Carbon Tracker's Uniper critique is a direct proxy for what SEBI assurance reviewers and FPI ESG teams will ask next CSOs and sustainability teams at NTPC, NLCIL, SJVN, and Adani Power: map your current emissions target architecture against SBTi Power Sector pathway requirements and ISSB S2 Appendix B scenario disclosure requirements; identify whether your 2030/2032 renewables targets are Paris-aligned or merely capacity-volume targets with no emissions intensity baseline Board risk committees of PSU utilities: table a formal agenda item on the 'coal cashflow-to-renewables capex funding gap' modelled under three carbon price scenarios (India CCTS baseline, EU CBAM shadow price, SBTi 1.5°C pathway) - this is the exact analytical frame Carbon Tracker applied to Uniper and it is the question your institutional investors will begin asking in AGM season 2025 Ministry of Power and MNRE policy teams: review Carbon Tracker's Uniper government-ownership model as a governance template for structuring a formal Just Transition Finance Facility for Indian coal utilities - including a policy-linked debt capacity enhancement mechanism that ring-fences renewables capex from coal asset depreciation cycles ESG analysts at domestic mutual funds and LIC: incorporate Carbon Tracker's three-flag framework (no interim targets, gas crowding out renewables, declining cashflow-capex mismatch) as a standard engagement checklist for power sector holdings in India; this directly maps to SEBI's stewardship code obligations for institutional investors Green bond and SLB structurers: ensure any new NTPC or PSU utility green bond framework explicitly addresses Scope 3 coal procurement emissions and includes a KPI for coal capacity retirement - without this, bonds face ICMA Green Bond Principles greenwashing scrutiny and potential FPI exclusion under SFDR Article 9 fund mandates
sections.our_take: Carbon Tracker's Uniper report is, at its core, a warning about the gap between stated transition ambition and structurally funded transition delivery - and that warning lands in India with considerably more force than in Germany. Germany's coal exit is a 2030 political commitment backed by a government that nationalised its largest gas and coal utility to control the transition timeline. India's coal exit is a 2070 net zero commitment backed by NDC targets that have not been disaggregated into sector-level carbon budgets, transition finance mechanisms, or utility-level interim milestones. That asymmetry is not sustainable as global capital markets - including the FPIs that hold approximately 20-25% of BSE-listed power sector equity - begin pricing transition risk with the granularity that ISSB S2, CSRD, and SBTi demand. In the next 24-36 months, I expect three specific regulatory and market shifts to force this issue into Indian boardrooms: first, SEBI will elevate Scope 3 disclosures from leadership to core assured indicators in the next BRSR revision, creating an assurance liability for utilities that have not built Scope 3 data infrastructure; second, India's CCTS compliance carbon market will make coal asset retirement financially material in ways the current regulatory accounting framework does not capture, triggering impairment review obligations under Ind AS 36 for coal plant balance sheet valuations; and third, EU CBAM's expansion beyond its current five sectors will eventually reach electricity imports and energy-intensive industrial goods, creating a de facto Scope 2 and Scope 3 carbon price signal in Indian export supply chains that no listed company can ignore. Boards and investors that treat the Uniper question - 'is government ownership the best mechanism for getting to net zero?' - as a European curiosity will miss the fact that India has already answered it implicitly: the government owns the largest coal utilities and the largest renewable targets simultaneously. The missing piece is the explicit policy mandate, the transition finance architecture, and the disclosure framework to make that ownership actually deliver net zero. Building those three elements, with urgency, is the most consequential ESG governance task in India today.

title: UK Mandates Climate Transition Plans for Financial Institutions and Large Corporates: What India's Listed Companies and Asset Managers Must Read Into This Now
summary: The UK Government's Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) is consulting on mandatory climate transition plan requirements for UK-regulated financial institutions and large corporates - and Carbon Tracker's formal submission, published 1 September 2025 (https://carbontracker.org/reports

sections.summary: The UK Government's Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) is consulting on mandatory climate transition plan requirements for UK-regulated financial institutions and large corporates - and Carbon Tracker's formal submission, published 1 September 2025 (https://carbontracker.org/reports/carbon-trackers-response-to-the-uk-governments-consultation-on-transition-plan-requirements/), argues these plans must be mandatory, 1.5°C-aligned, and cover both transition and physical climate risks including capex financing costs. For Indian listed companies, this is not a distant UK policy debate: Indian firms with UK institutional shareholders, London-listed GDRs, UK export relationships, or supply chain exposure to UK-regulated financial counterparties face direct upstream pressure as their UK creditors, investors, and trade finance providers will themselves be required to hold credible transition plans - and will demand the same from material counterparties. The regulatory gravity pulling India's BRSR Core disclosures and SEBI's transition plan expectations into alignment with this global trajectory is accelerating faster than most Indian boards have priced in.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Carbon Tracker (carbontracker.org), the London-based independent financial think tank, details their formal response to DESNZ's live consultation on introducing statutory transition plan requirements across UK-regulated financial institutions and large corporates. The submission marks a significant inflection point: it signals that the UK - already home to the Transition Plan Taskforce (TPT) disclosure framework, which itself influenced ISSB IFRS S2 Climate disclosures - is moving from voluntary best-practice guidance toward legally binding obligations. Carbon Tracker's key positions are unambiguous: transition plans must be mandatory (not comply-or-explain); they must include the full capex cost of the energy transition financed through both debt and equity; physical climate risks must be explicitly addressed alongside transition risks; fossil fuel producers require bespoke, distinct guidance given the structural incompatibility of their business models with a 1.5°C pathway; and pension funds, as universal asset owners holding systemic risk, carry a specific stewardship obligation. The submission also calls for a national UK transition plan to anchor a whole-of-government strategy - a concept directly analogous to India's Long-Term Low Emissions Development Strategy (LT-LEDS) submitted to the UNFCCC in 2022, and India's National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC), both of which currently lack the corporate-level transition plan architecture the UK is now legislating. For Indian companies, the immediate consequence runs through three channels: first, UK institutional investors - including pension funds such as LGIM, Aviva Investors, and Legal & General, all significant holders of Indian equities through MSCI EM mandates - will face mandatory transition plan requirements that will cascade disclosure and decarbonisation demands down to their Indian investee companies; second, Indian exporters to UK markets in sectors like steel, chemicals, textiles, and automotive components will encounter UK supply chain due diligence tightening as UK corporates building their own transition plans must assess Scope 3 emissions from suppliers, putting Indian Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers under direct scrutiny; third, Indian companies listed on UK markets or raising UK-regulated capital face direct in-scope obligations as the legislation is finalised.
sections.who_is_affected: Indian steel, cement, chemicals, and oil & gas producers with UK institutional shareholders or UK-listed instruments (GDRs, bonds) - these companies will face transition plan disclosure demands from UK-regulated investors who are themselves now moving toward mandatory plan requirements BSE 500 and NSE Nifty 500 companies already subject to SEBI BRSR Core disclosures, particularly the top 150 listed entities required to provide reasonable assurance on BRSR Core KPIs from FY2024-25 - the UK framework sets a higher bar that institutional investors will use as a benchmark Indian exporters in hard-to-abate sectors (steel, aluminium, refinery products, fertilisers, textiles) with UK supply chain relationships - UK corporates building 1.5°C-aligned transition plans must assess and disclose Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods) emissions, putting Indian suppliers directly in scope CFOs and Chief Sustainability Officers at Indian conglomerates and large-cap industrials who manage UK investor relations - they will need to respond to enhanced stewardship questionnaires and transition plan alignment requests from UK pension funds and asset managers Indian asset managers and insurance companies with UK regulatory touchpoints or IFRS S2 adoption plans - they face the clearest read-across as the UK framework for financial institutions directly shapes what ISSB IFRS S2 adoption will demand in India Risk managers and treasury heads at Indian infrastructure and energy companies funded through UK-regulated green bonds or sustainability-linked loans - transition plan credibility will become a covenant-linked disclosure requirement ESG analysts and stewardship teams at India-focused foreign institutional investors (FIIs) regulated in the UK - they will be required to integrate transition plan assessments of Indian holdings into their own mandatory plans
sections.key_obligations: UK consultation on mandatory transition plan requirements is live as of September 2025 - Indian companies with UK investor relations exposure should monitor DESNZ's final legislative framework, expected to inform UK statute within 12-18 months of consultation close SEBI BRSR Core - top 150 listed companies by market cap: reasonable assurance on KPIs including GHG emissions (Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3) mandatory from FY2024-25 annual reports; this is the Indian hook that connects to transition plan credibility requirements SEBI's BRSR does not yet mandate a formal climate transition plan, but the trajectory from the UK, EU (CSRD Article 15 requires transition plan disclosure aligned with Paris Agreement), and ISSB IFRS S2 (which requires disclosure of transition plan existence and progress) means SEBI will face pressure to upgrade BRSR to include transition plan requirements within 2-3 regulatory cycles ISSB IFRS S2 paragraph 14(b) already requires companies adopting the standard to disclose whether they have a climate-related transition plan and, if so, key assumptions and dependencies - Indian companies voluntarily aligning to IFRS S2 must address this now, not at adoption Carbon Tracker's call for distinct fossil fuel producer guidance directly affects ONGC, Coal India, Reliance Industries (E&P segment), and IOCL - these companies face the sharpest investor scrutiny under any 1.5°C-aligned transition plan framework, and their UK-regulated institutional shareholders will apply this lens in stewardship engagements from FY2025-26 onward India's PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme Cycle 3 and the proposed Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 - companies building internal transition plans should map decarbonisation pathways against PAT targets and CCTS obligations simultaneously to avoid double governance burden BEE's proposed trajectory toward mandatory net zero targets for energy-intensive industries - Indian companies in cement, steel, aluminium, and pulp & paper should treat UK-style transition plan architecture as a forward-planning tool for BEE compliance, not merely an investor disclosure exercise
sections.climate_angle: Carbon Tracker's insistence that transition plans include the full capex cost of transition - financed through both debt and equity - is the most commercially significant element of this submission for Indian boards and CFOs, and it is the angle most Indian companies are completely unprepared for. A credible 1.5°C-aligned transition plan is not a narrative document about ambitions; it is a capital allocation roadmap that must reconcile business-as-usual capex projections with the investment required to retire or transform carbon-intensive assets on a Paris-compatible timeline. For Indian listed companies in the Nifty50 - where capital expenditure in energy, materials, and industrials runs into lakhs of crores - this means boards must be able to answer, with financial specificity, how they will fund the delta between current capex plans and transition-compliant capex. UK-regulated pension funds and asset managers will ask exactly this question in stewardship dialogues. The physical climate risk dimension is equally critical for India: Carbon Tracker's submission stresses that financially material physical impacts - flooding, heat stress, water scarcity, extreme weather events - must be embedded in transition plans, not treated as a separate reporting track. India's own physical climate vulnerability is extreme and rising: the June 2024 heatwaves across Rajasthan, UP, and Bihar, the recurring Chennai and Mumbai flood events, and the Himalayan glacial retreat affecting hydropower generation are all financially material physical risks that Indian companies already carry on their balance sheets but rarely quantify. ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis requirements, the TCFD framework underlying both UK TPT and BRSR Core's ESG disclosures, and the TNFD's nature-related financial disclosure framework (relevant for Indian agrochemicals, FMCG, and infrastructure sectors) all demand that physical risk quantification be embedded in transition planning. The UK's move toward mandatory plans crystallises what Indian companies face: within 36 months, the largest pools of global capital will require a credible, independently verified transition plan as a precondition for competitive cost of capital. India's carbon market under CCTS, and the alignment of sectoral decarbonisation roadmaps with India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) - 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, 45% emissions intensity reduction - provides the sovereign-level anchor against which company-level transition plans should be calibrated. Companies that build this architecture now will have a measurable cost-of-capital advantage over those that wait for SEBI to mandate it.
sections.what_to_do: CFO + CSO (immediate, within 60 days): Commission a gap analysis mapping your current BRSR Core disclosures against the transition plan architecture required by ISSB IFRS S2 paragraph 14(b) and the UK TPT framework's five-pillar structure (governance, business strategy, financial planning, engagement, metrics & targets) - identify which pillars are absent or underdeveloped Investor Relations team (within 90 days): Proactively engage your top 10 UK and EU institutional shareholders to understand their transition plan information requirements for FY2025-26 stewardship cycles; UK-regulated asset managers will increasingly use Carbon Tracker's framework as a benchmark for engagement quality assessments of Indian investee companies Sustainability / Strategy team (within 6 months): Develop a quantified capex transition roadmap that explicitly bridges current capital allocation plans and 1.5°C-aligned asset transformation requirements - this should be stress-tested against IEA Net Zero by 2050 scenario and India's NDC trajectory, and reviewed by the board's risk committee Legal + Compliance (within 3 months): Map your UK regulatory exposure - identify any UK-listed instruments, UK-regulated lenders, or UK supply chain counterparties that will themselves face mandatory transition plan requirements; assess whether Scope 3 Category 1 emissions disclosure from Indian operations will be required by UK corporate clients Board Risk Committee (next board cycle): Table a dedicated agenda item on transition plan governance, aligned with SEBI's BRSR principle of board-level accountability for ESG disclosures; ensure the board can articulate the company's transition plan strategy and capex commitment in the FY2025-26 annual report and investor presentations Companies in fossil fuel-adjacent sectors - ONGC, Coal India, Reliance E&P, IOCL, GAIL, Adani Ports (coal logistics) (within 6 months): Begin scenario analysis using a credible 1.5°C pathway to assess stranded asset risk in your current capex pipeline; Carbon Tracker's submission explicitly flags that fossil fuel producers require distinct, more stringent guidance - UK and EU investors will apply this lens in 2026 stewardship engagements
sections.our_take: The UK's move toward mandatory climate transition plans is not an isolated policy event - it is the leading edge of a global regulatory convergence that will make voluntary, narrative-only ESG disclosures commercially untenable within three to five years. Carbon Tracker's submission is significant precisely because it refuses to accept the comfortable middle ground: transition plans must be mandatory, 1.5°C-aligned, financially quantified, and physically grounded - or they are simply greenwash infrastructure. This is the standard that the most sophisticated institutional capital pools globally are already internalising, and India's regulatory architecture is on a collision course with this expectation. SEBI has built a credible foundation with BRSR Core and the phased reasonable assurance requirements, but the framework stops short of requiring a transition plan - and that gap is becoming visible to global investors. I expect SEBI will be compelled to introduce transition plan disclosure requirements within the next two to three BRSR revision cycles, likely in the FY2026-27 or FY2027-28 reporting framework, drawing directly on ISSB IFRS S2, the UK TPT framework, and the EU CSRD's transition plan obligations under ESRS E1. Indian companies that wait for SEBI to mandate this will find themselves in a reactive, expensive compliance exercise - those that build the architecture now will have already embedded it into capital allocation decisions and will credibly differentiate in global investor roadshows. The carbon intensity angle deserves specific attention: Carbon Tracker's parallel work on automotive Scope 3 underreporting and fossil fuel producer disguise (see related reports on their site) signals that sector-level scrutiny is intensifying. Indian companies in steel, cement, coal, and oil & gas that present transition credentials without underlying capex commitment and verified emissions data will face growing public challenge from the same think tanks and investor coalitions shaping the UK consultation. The board-level implication is clear: transition planning is no longer a sustainability team deliverable - it is a capital structure decision that belongs on the CFO's agenda, the board's risk committee charter, and the audit committee's assurance scope. India's window to lead this transition rather than react to it is narrowing.

title: SASB Oil & Gas Standards Revision: What Carbon Tracker's ISSB Submission Means for India's Upstream, Midstream, and Integrated Energy Majors
summary: Carbon Tracker, the London-based climate finance think tank, has formally submitted its response to the ISSB's exposure draft on SASB Standards for Oil and Gas - a development that signals the imminent tightening of sector-specific disclosure requirements for upstream, integrated, and midstream oil

sections.summary: Carbon Tracker, the London-based climate finance think tank, has formally submitted its response to the ISSB's exposure draft on SASB Standards for Oil and Gas - a development that signals the imminent tightening of sector-specific disclosure requirements for upstream, integrated, and midstream oil and gas companies under the global IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards architecture. For Indian companies - ONGC, Oil India, Reliance Industries, BPCL, HPCL, and their listed subsidiaries - this is not a distant global standard: SEBI's BRSR Core framework already references ISSB alignment as its stated direction of travel, and any hardening of SASB oil and gas metrics will cascade directly into what Indian ESG auditors, institutional investors, and eventually SEBI will demand. The core implication is this: carbon intensity metrics, methane emissions disclosures, stranded asset risk quantification, and climate scenario analysis for oil and gas assets are moving from voluntary best practice to verifiable, investor-grade disclosure obligations - and Indian energy majors are structurally underprepared. Carbon Tracker's submission, published 26 November 2025 and available at https://carbontracker.org/reports/sasb-standards-on-oil-and-gas/, sets out the think tank's priority recommendations to the ISSB on strengthening the SASB oil and gas standards, with particular emphasis on closing reporting gaps that currently allow companies to obscure true carbon intensity of their portfolios.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Carbon Tracker (carbontracker.org) confirms that the ISSB has issued an exposure draft proposing revisions to the SASB Standards specifically for the oil and gas sector - covering Exploration & Production, Midstream, Refining & Marketing, and Oil & Gas Services sub-sectors. Carbon Tracker's formal submission to this consultation sets out detailed recommendations on what the revised standards must capture to give capital markets a true picture of transition risk embedded in oil and gas assets. The SASB standards, now under ISSB stewardship since the consolidation of SASB into the IFRS Foundation in 2022, function as the industry-specific 'topical' layer beneath the cross-cutting IFRS S1 (general sustainability disclosures) and IFRS S2 (climate disclosures) standards. For oil and gas specifically, SASB metrics include Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions intensity, methane emissions as a percentage of natural gas throughput, volume of produced water, hydrocarbon spills, and proven and probable reserves by resource type - metrics that directly feed into investors' assessment of stranded asset exposure. What Carbon Tracker is pushing for - based on their broader body of research - is a material strengthening of: (1) carbon intensity reporting across the full asset lifecycle, not just operational emissions; (2) disclosure of capex allocation toward high-cost, high-carbon reserves that are inconsistent with 1.5°C pathways; (3) explicit scenario analysis linking reserve portfolios to IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 and Announced Pledges Scenario outcomes; and (4) methane intensity disclosures aligned with the Global Methane Pledge thresholds. This matters enormously for India because SEBI's BRSR Core - mandatory for the top 150 listed companies by market capitalisation from FY2023-24, expanding to the top 250 from FY2024-25 - has been explicitly designed by SEBI to converge with ISSB standards over time. SEBI's Consultation Paper of July 2023 and the subsequent BRSR Core notification are unambiguous on this convergence intent. When ISSB tightens SASB oil and gas metrics, BRSR Core's 'Key Performance Indicators' for energy-intensive sectors will follow - and ONGC, Reliance, and BPCL will be first in the audit committee's line of fire. Beyond BRSR, Indian oil and gas companies with international bond issuances, GDR listings, or foreign institutional investor (FII) ownership above threshold levels are already subject to investor stewardship expectations aligned with ISSB S1/S2. Institutional investors including Norway's Government Pension Fund Global, BlackRock, and the major UK and European asset managers that hold significant positions in Indian energy sector equities are beginning to use SASB oil and gas metrics as a baseline for engagement letters and proxy voting decisions. A revision that raises the floor on what must be disclosed - particularly on methane, reserve carbon intensity, and capex alignment - will translate into direct investor pressure on Indian energy boards within 12-18 months of final standard publication.
sections.who_is_affected: Upstream oil and gas producers: ONGC and Oil India - both BRSR Core-mandated disclosers - whose reserve portfolios and methane emission intensities from ageing fields in Assam, Rajasthan, and offshore basins will face new scrutiny under tightened SASB E&P metrics Integrated energy majors: Reliance Industries (refining, petrochemicals, upstream JVs) and GAIL (midstream gas transmission) - subject to SASB Midstream and Refining & Marketing sub-sector standards simultaneously Public sector oil marketing companies: BPCL and HPCL, whose refining Scope 1 intensity and transition capex allocation between fossil and clean energy assets will be a focal point of revised SASB Refining standards Listed oil field services companies: companies such as Deep Industries, Hindustan Oil Exploration (HOEC), and Selan Exploration, which fall under SASB Oil & Gas Services and are increasingly in the scope of supply chain due diligence by larger operators CFOs and treasury teams at Indian oil and gas companies with outstanding green bonds, sustainability-linked bonds, or syndicated loans with ESG-linked covenants - where SASB metric deterioration triggers ratchet clauses or covenant breaches Chief Sustainability Officers and BRSR reporting teams at BSE 500 energy sector companies who must anticipate ISSB-aligned metric additions in the next SEBI BRSR revision cycle ESG analysts and portfolio managers at domestic mutual funds (SBI MF, HDFC MF, Nippon India MF) and FII desks with energy sector exposure, who use SASB metrics as inputs to proprietary ESG scoring models Audit committees and statutory auditors (Big Four and Grant Thornton India) engaged in BRSR Core 'reasonable assurance' engagements, who will need to update their audit procedures to reflect any expanded SASB oil and gas metric set
sections.key_obligations: ISSB Exposure Draft on SASB Oil & Gas Standards - consultation period open as of November 2025; final revised standards expected to be published within 12-18 months of consultation close, likely mid-to-late 2026; companies should track IFRS Foundation workplan at ifrs.org for precise timeline BRSR Core reasonable assurance - mandatory for top 150 NSE/BSE-listed companies by market cap from FY2023-24 reporting; expanding to top 250 companies from FY2024-25; energy sector companies in both cohorts must ensure their ESG KPIs are auditable and consistent with ISSB-aligned metrics as SEBI updates the BRSR framework SEBI BRSR framework update - SEBI has signalled intent to revise BRSR KPIs in line with ISSB S1/S2 adoption trajectory; Indian oil and gas companies should anticipate a SEBI circular or consultation paper on sector-specific BRSR enhancements within 18-24 months, likely referencing updated SASB oil and gas metrics directly Methane disclosure alignment - companies participating in India's upstream gas sector should note that the Global Methane Pledge (to which India is not a signatory but which influences investor expectations) sets a 30% methane reduction target by 2030 from 2020 levels; SASB revised standards are expected to sharpen methane intensity metric definitions to align with this benchmark Stranded asset scenario analysis - IFRS S2 paragraph 22 already requires climate-related scenario analysis; revised SASB oil and gas standards will likely mandate specific IEA scenario references (NZE 2050, APS) for reserve-level analysis; Indian companies with IND AS-compliant financial statements must assess whether impairment disclosures under IND AS 36 are consistent with disclosed climate scenarios - a gap that statutory auditors are beginning to probe EU CBAM and CSRD supply chain obligations - Indian oil and gas product exporters (particularly petroleum coke, LPG, naphtha) supplying EU-domiciled buyers face indirect exposure to CSRD value chain disclosure requirements and CBAM carbon pricing from 2026 full implementation; SASB metric alignment strengthens Indian exporters' ability to respond to EU customer data requests
sections.climate_angle: The revision of SASB oil and gas standards sits at the precise intersection of physical climate risk, energy transition capital allocation, and financial materiality - and for India, the stakes are asymmetric and underappreciated. India's oil and gas sector faces a dual transition pressure: domestically, the government's push for energy security through domestic upstream development (OALP bid rounds, deep-water exploration) sits in tension with the capital market's growing demand for credible transition plans; externally, India's refining exports - particularly from Reliance's Jamnagar complex, the world's largest refining hub - face increasing carbon intensity scrutiny from European and North American buyers operating under CSRD value chain disclosure obligations. Carbon Tracker's research trajectory - as evidenced by their concurrent work on 'Oil Companies in Disguise' (published June 2026, examining how automaker investments mask fossil fuel carbon intensity) - signals that the think tank is building an evidence base for systemic under-reporting of carbon intensity across fossil fuel value chains. Applied to the SASB oil and gas context, this means pressure for metrics that capture embedded carbon in products sold (Scope 3 Category 11 - use of sold products), not just operational emissions. For ONGC and Reliance, Scope 3 Category 11 dwarfs Scope 1 and 2 combined, and its inclusion in mandatory SASB disclosures would fundamentally alter how institutional investors price transition risk in these stocks. India's own carbon market - the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified by MoEFCC in June 2023, and the PAT Scheme under BEE for energy-intensive sectors - creates a domestic price signal for carbon that, while nascent, is beginning to interact with global disclosure frameworks. As SASB oil and gas metrics tighten under ISSB oversight, the carbon intensity numbers that Indian companies report under BRSR Core will increasingly be used as inputs to both domestic CCTS baseline-setting and international investor ESG scoring - making accurate, assured, and ISSB-consistent reporting a financial asset, not merely a compliance cost. Companies that get ahead of the curve on methane intensity measurement, carbon intensity per barrel of oil equivalent, and capex alignment with transition scenarios will find access to green finance, sustainability-linked loans, and international capital markets materially easier than peers who treat SASB as a box-ticking exercise.
sections.what_to_do: BRSR and sustainability reporting teams (immediate - within 30 days): Download and review Carbon Tracker's full submission to the ISSB SASB Oil & Gas exposure draft at https://carbontracker.org/reports/sasb-standards-on-oil-and-gas/ and map Carbon Tracker's priority recommendations against your current BRSR Core KPI disclosures to identify gaps - particularly on methane intensity, reserve carbon intensity, and transition capex ratios CFO and treasury (within 60 days): Commission a gap analysis comparing current BRSR Core energy and emissions disclosures against the full SASB Exploration & Production, Midstream, or Refining & Marketing standards (as applicable) - this analysis should also assess consistency between climate scenario disclosures and IND AS 36 impairment assumptions, a growing area of statutory audit scrutiny Chief Sustainability Officer (within 90 days): Initiate a Scope 3 Category 11 (use of sold products) quantification exercise if not already underway - given Carbon Tracker's clear direction of advocacy, ISSB will face pressure to include Scope 3 in revised SASB oil and gas standards; being prepared with a credible Scope 3 inventory positions the company ahead of the next BRSR revision cycle and strengthens responses to FII stewardship questionnaires Board Risk Committee (next scheduled meeting): Add 'SASB Oil & Gas Standards Revision and BRSR Convergence Risk' as a standing agenda item; request management to provide a 12-month regulatory watch brief covering ISSB exposure draft finalisation, anticipated SEBI BRSR update, and implications for the company's sustainability-linked financing covenants Investor Relations (within 45 days): Brief key institutional shareholders - particularly FIIs and ESG-mandated domestic funds - on the company's current SASB metric coverage and the roadmap for alignment with revised standards; proactive IR on SASB convergence reduces the risk of being caught off-guard by escalated engagement letters or negative ESG score revisions when the final standards are published Legal and compliance (within 60 days): Review all sustainability-linked bond documentation, ESG-linked loan covenants, and green finance frameworks for references to SASB metrics or ISSB standards as KPI anchors - identify any covenant language that could be triggered by metric redefinition under revised SASB standards and engage legal counsel on renegotiation options if necessary
sections.our_take: Carbon Tracker's engagement with the ISSB on SASB oil and gas standards is not an isolated advocacy exercise - it is part of a coordinated, multi-year effort by the climate finance community to close the credibility gap between what oil and gas companies report and what their actual transition risk exposure looks like to a sophisticated capital markets investor. The pattern is now well established: Carbon Tracker publishes the analytical case for tighter disclosure (as they have done with their 'Oil Companies in Disguise' series on automotive-fossil fuel linkages), submits formal technical input to standard-setters, and then watches as those recommendations migrate - with a 2-4 year lag - into mandatory disclosure frameworks. Indian boards that treat this as a European or North American problem are making a strategic error of the first order. The trajectory for India is clear and accelerating. SEBI has already signalled ISSB convergence as BRSR's long-term destination. The MoEFCC-led CCTS will require carbon intensity baselines that overlap substantially with SASB oil and gas metrics. India's largest institutional investors - LIC, SBI Life, the National Pension System - are under growing pressure from IRDAI and PFRDA to integrate climate risk into portfolio management, which means they will begin demanding SASB-aligned disclosures from portfolio companies in the energy sector within the next 24-36 months. And as EU CSRD value chain obligations bite, Reliance's refining customers in Europe will begin passing SASB-derived data requests down the supply chain as early as 2026-27. The companies that will navigate this environment successfully are those that treat the SASB oil and gas revision not as a compliance burden but as a strategic disclosure opportunity: an invitation to tell a credible, investor-grade story about how they are managing the energy transition. For ONGC, that story must include a credible methane abatement roadmap and a candid assessment of which fields remain economic under a 2°C scenario. For Reliance, it requires a clear capex narrative distinguishing between high-carbon refining expansion and the new energy transition investments the market already knows are underway. The standard is tightening. The question is whether Indian energy majors lead the disclosure curve or are dragged across it.

title: Carbon Tracker's 'Recalibrating Climate Risk': Why India's Boards and Institutional Investors Are Sitting on Mispriced Physical Climate Exposure
summary: A major new report from Carbon Tracker and the University of Exeter's Green Futures Solutions, published 5 February 2026 and drawing on structured expert judgement from climate scientists across 12 countries, establishes with rigour what many Indian risk officers have quietly suspected: the economic

sections.summary: A major new report from Carbon Tracker and the University of Exeter's Green Futures Solutions, published 5 February 2026 and drawing on structured expert judgement from climate scientists across 12 countries, establishes with rigour what many Indian risk officers have quietly suspected: the economic damage models underpinning climate scenario analysis used by governments, central banks, and institutional investors are systematically understating physical climate risk as warming approaches 2°C - and the gap is widening, not closing. For Indian listed companies, this is not an abstract modelling debate. India is one of the world's highest-exposure geographies to physical climate impacts - heatwaves, monsoon disruption, coastal flooding, and agricultural stress - and the SEBI BRSR Core framework, ISSB IFRS S2, and the Reserve Bank of India's emerging climate risk supervisory guidance all require scenario-based physical risk disclosure. If the scenarios being used are structurally flawed, so is the disclosure, the capital allocation, and the board's risk assurance. The central finding is that economic damage functions used in integrated assessment models (IAMs) - including those embedded in the NGFS climate scenarios widely used by Indian banks and SEBI-regulated entities for BRSR and IFRS S2 reporting - undercount losses from mortality, ecosystem degradation, inequality, and regional extremes, while GDP-reconstruction effects can mask welfare destruction after disasters. For Indian CFOs and audit committees, this means the climate scenario analysis they are filing or preparing is not just incomplete - it may be creating a false sense of quantified certainty that will expose them to regulatory and investor challenge as physical impacts materialise.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Carbon Tracker (https://carbontracker.org/reports/recalibrating-climate-risk/), the report 'Recalibrating Climate Risk' published 5 February 2026, represents a significant escalation in the credibility of the critique against standard climate economic modelling. This is not a fringe academic argument - it builds on Carbon Tracker's 'Loading the DICE Against Pensions' (2023) and the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries/University of Exeter 'Emperor's New Climate Scenarios' (2023), and now has structured expert input from climate scientists across 12 countries. The University of Exeter's Green Futures Solutions - whose researchers have directly shaped UK, EU, and UN climate risk thinking - leads the analysis. The report's four structural findings each have direct Indian disclosure and investment implications. First, physical climate damages are structural and compounding: at higher warming levels, cascade effects across sectors and geographies undermine stable economic growth. India's economic geography makes it acutely vulnerable to this dynamic - a severe monsoon failure simultaneously stresses agriculture (22% of employment), hydropower generation, inland logistics, and rural consumption. Single-variable IAM models miss this compounding entirely. Second, economic damage models focus on global mean temperature, but India's actual risk is driven by regional and local extremes - the wet bulb temperature thresholds that determine outdoor labour productivity loss in Rajasthan and Odisha, the specific flood return periods in Mumbai's coastal industrial zones, the drought frequency in Vidarbha's cotton belt. BRSR Core's principal adverse impact disclosures and IFRS S2's physical risk quantification requirements both implicitly demand regional-level granularity that current models do not provide. Third, GDP metrics mask welfare losses: reconstruction spending after cyclones in Odisha or Andhra Pradesh can show as GDP-positive while communities and ecosystems suffer multi-year productivity losses. Fourth, and most critically for risk governance: as warming approaches 2°C, tail risks and tipping points become dominant features of the risk landscape, yet models present smooth, precise-looking point estimates - exactly the kind of output that audit committees sign off on as 'robust' climate scenario analysis. Carbon Tracker's CEO Mark Campanale is direct: 'financial institutions will continue to chronically under-price climate risks - meaning that pension funds and taxpayers will remain dangerously exposed.'
sections.who_is_affected: BSE 500 and NSE-listed companies in the top 1,000 by market capitalisation subject to SEBI BRSR Core mandatory disclosure - particularly those with material physical climate exposure: agriculture-linked FMCG, cement, steel, textiles, chemicals, real estate, and infrastructure Indian scheduled commercial banks and NBFCs preparing for RBI's climate risk and scenario analysis supervisory guidelines, whose NGFS scenario-based stress tests embed the same flawed damage functions this report critiques Indian insurance and reinsurance companies - GIC Re, New India Assurance, private sector general insurers - whose catastrophe modelling and premium pricing face material mis-calibration if physical damage functions understate tail risks Domestic institutional investors: LIC, EPFO, NPS Trust, and mutual funds with long-duration equity and infrastructure exposure in climate-vulnerable sectors and geographies across India CFOs and Chief Risk Officers at Indian infrastructure developers - ports, highways, renewable energy, urban water utilities - where project finance covenants increasingly require ISSB S2-aligned physical risk disclosure ESG analysts and stewardship teams at foreign institutional investors (FIIs) with India equity allocations, who use scenario analysis to underpin engagement letters and proxy voting on climate risk management Company secretaries and audit committee chairs at BRSR Core-filing entities who are responsible for assuring the integrity of climate-related financial disclosures under SEBI's Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements (LODR)
sections.key_obligations: BRSR Core - SEBI mandatory disclosure for top 150 listed entities (FY2023-24 onwards) and top 250 (FY2024-25 onwards): Principle 6 and Leadership Indicators require disclosure of physical and transition risk exposure and scenario analysis. The Carbon Tracker findings directly challenge whether NGFS-based scenarios used in these filings meet the 'decision-useful' standard SEBI expects ISSB IFRS S2 (effective for Indian companies voluntarily adopting or required by lenders/investors): Paragraphs 10-12 require disclosure of physical risk scenario analysis using recognised scenarios - but IFRS S2 does not mandate specific scenarios, leaving companies exposed to challenge if chosen scenarios systematically understate damages RBI Draft Guidelines on Climate Risk and Scenario Analysis (expected to be finalised in 2025-26 cycle): Indian banks using NGFS scenarios for supervisory stress tests must assess whether damage functions in those scenarios are fit for India's regional climate exposure profile SEBI ESG Rating Providers (ERPs) Regulations (2023): ERPs rating Indian companies on physical risk are implicitly calibrated against the same damage models - a systemic mis-calibration of the underlying science flows directly into ESG ratings and index inclusion/exclusion decisions Companies Act 2013 / LODR - Board fiduciary duty: Directors have an ongoing duty to ensure material risk disclosures are accurate. If physical climate risk is structurally mispriced in scenario analysis, audit committees need to assess whether current disclosures satisfy the materiality standard under Schedule III amendments requiring climate-related financial information CDP 2025 disclosure cycle (questionnaire responses due mid-2025): Physical risk scoring in CDP's Climate Change questionnaire C2 requires scenario analysis; companies selecting flawed scenarios risk lower scores and investor confidence gaps
sections.climate_angle: The Carbon Tracker report lands at a moment when India's physical climate risk exposure is moving from theoretical to balance-sheet real. The 2024 monsoon saw deficits in key agricultural states, the 2023 Manipur floods disrupted supply chains across the Northeast, and summer 2024 heatwaves in Rajasthan and UP resulted in documented labour productivity losses in construction and agriculture - the sectors that structurally underpin Indian GDP growth. Yet when Indian companies file BRSR Core disclosures citing NGFS 'Orderly 2°C' or 'Hot House World' scenarios, the damage functions embedded in those scenarios - derived from DICE, PAGE, or FUND model variants - will, per this report's expert-consensus findings, systematically undercount exactly the regional extreme-weather losses that are already appearing in Indian P&L statements. The transition finance dimension is equally sharp. India's sovereign green bond programme, the green infrastructure pipeline under the National Infrastructure Pipeline, and the nascent domestic carbon market under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) all depend on investor confidence in climate risk pricing. If the physical risk anchor is miscalibrated, the risk-adjusted return calculations underpinning green bond pricing and infrastructure equity valuations are wrong. This matters for India's ability to mobilise the USD 2.5 trillion in climate finance it needs to 2030 under its Nationally Determined Contribution. The Paris Agreement's Article 2.1(c) goal of making financial flows consistent with a low-carbon, climate-resilient pathway requires not just transition finance but accurate physical risk pricing - and this report says the latter is broken. For Indian issuers of sustainability-linked bonds and green loans under SEBI's green bond framework, lenders are beginning to ask harder questions about physical risk scenario quality, and the Carbon Tracker findings give them specific grounds to do so.
sections.what_to_do: CFO / Chief Risk Officer - within 90 days: Commission a gap assessment of the climate scenarios currently used in BRSR Core and IFRS S2 disclosures against the specific model limitations identified in the Carbon Tracker report - particularly whether your scenario source (NGFS, IEA, IPCC AR6 RCPs) uses global mean temperature damage functions or regional extreme-event calibrations relevant to your Indian asset footprint Sustainability / ESG team - within 60 days: Engage your scenario analysis provider or internal modelling team to identify whether physical risk quantification covers the four specific gaps flagged: cascade effects, regional extremes, non-GDP welfare losses (mortality, morbidity, ecosystem), and tail risk/tipping point representation. Document this assessment for audit committee review Audit Committee - at next quarterly meeting: Add a standing agenda item: 'Integrity of Climate Scenario Assumptions.' Request management to confirm which economic damage models underpin physical risk disclosures, and seek external expert assurance on model fitness-for-purpose in line with SEBI LODR Regulation 17 (board risk oversight) and Schedule III climate disclosure requirements Investor Relations / Board - before next AGM: Prepare an enhanced narrative in the Annual Report and BRSR that explicitly acknowledges the limitations of current physical risk models and describes the precautionary and robustness measures the company is taking in its risk management - this positions ahead of investor questions and demonstrates the disclosure sophistication that FIIs and proxy advisors like Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) and Glass Lewis increasingly reward Treasury / Green Finance team - linked to next debt issuance: If issuing green bonds or sustainability-linked instruments under SEBI's green bond circular, proactively engage lead arrangers and rating agencies on the physical risk scenario methodology question - document your approach to model uncertainty, as international bond investors post this report will treat NGFS-only scenario reliance as a disclosure risk flag Board Risk Committee - 12-month horizon: Commission scenario planning that goes beyond NGFS standard scenarios to incorporate India-specific regional physical risk pathways, using IPCC AR6 Working Group II regional chapters on South Asia as the scientific anchor, and stress-test key assets (manufacturing sites, supply chain nodes, water-intensive operations) against 1.5°C, 2°C, and 3°C regional warming trajectories with cascade and tail risk overlays
sections.our_take: The Carbon Tracker 'Recalibrating Climate Risk' report is, in our reading, a watershed document - not because its critique is new, but because it has now reached the threshold of structured, multi-country expert consensus that regulators, central banks, and institutional investors can no longer dismiss as minority scientific opinion. The trajectory from here is clear: within 24-36 months, NGFS will be compelled to update its scenario damage functions, ISSB will face pressure to specify minimum standards for physical risk model quality in IFRS S2 implementation guidance, and the EU's European Supervisory Authorities will likely require banks and insurers under CSRD and Pillar 3 to disclose the specific damage model assumptions underlying their physical risk quantification. India will not be insulated from this wave. SEBI, whose BRSR Core framework explicitly references ISSB alignment, will need to respond - and we expect SEBI's sustainability disclosure advisory committee to take up the model integrity question in its 2026-27 review cycle. For Indian companies, the risk is a two-stage exposure: first, the scenario analysis they are currently filing will be retrospectively seen as having understated physical risk - creating reputational and potential legal exposure as climate impacts materialise; second, the recalibration of damage models will, when it comes, produce a step-change upward in disclosed physical risk that will re-price insurance premiums, infrastructure valuations, and credit spreads for Indian climate-exposed assets. Boards that have treated climate scenario analysis as a compliance checkbox - exactly the 'tick-box disclosure exercise' Campanale names - will find themselves managing a sudden credibility gap with investors, lenders, and regulators simultaneously. The companies that invest now in model quality, regional granularity, and honest uncertainty communication will be structurally better positioned for that reckoning. This is not a 2050 problem. The physical damages are accruing now, the model gap is documented, and the regulatory response clock is running.

title: CAT Bonn 2026 Briefing: Energy Crisis Responses Under the Microscope - What Indian Policymakers and Listed Companies Must Watch
summary: The Climate Action Tracker (CAT) team - led by Niklas Höhne, Bill Hare, and Sofia Gonzales-Zuñiga - will present a critical briefing at the Bonn Climate Change Conference on 11 June 2026, examining whether national responses to the global energy crisis are accelerating or derailing the clean energy

sections.summary: The Climate Action Tracker (CAT) team - led by Niklas Höhne, Bill Hare, and Sofia Gonzales-Zuñiga - will present a critical briefing at the Bonn Climate Change Conference on 11 June 2026, examining whether national responses to the global energy crisis are accelerating or derailing the clean energy transition. For India, this is not an abstract diplomatic moment: CAT's assessments directly feed into sovereign climate credibility scores used by ESG-oriented global investors, export market gatekeepers under the EU's CBAM and CSRD supply chain rules, and multilateral lenders calibrating concessional green finance allocations. Indian CFOs and CSOs at BSE 500 companies - particularly in energy-intensive sectors - need to understand that a CAT downgrade of India's climate trajectory, or a finding that India's energy security response has locked in fossil fuel capacity, will have measurable consequences for cost of capital and investor engagement cycles within 12 to 24 months.
sections.what_changed: Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (climateactiontracker.org) confirms a press conference scheduled for 11 June 2026, 15:00-15:30 CEST, at the Bonn Climate Change Conference (Nairobi 4 press room, with webcast available). The CAT briefing will interrogate a question that sits at the heart of the current NDC cycle: have governments used the post-2022 global energy crisis - triggered by the Russia-Ukraine war, supply chain fractures, and LNG market volatility - as a launchpad for faster renewable deployment, or have they used it as political cover to entrench new fossil fuel infrastructure? Source article: https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/press-conference-bonn-sessions-2026/ For India, the stakes are unusually high at this particular Bonn session. India submitted its updated NDC in 2022, committing to 500 GW of non-fossil electricity capacity by 2030 and a 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP versus 2005 levels. However, since 2022, India has also approved significant expansions of domestic coal production under the energy security rationale - including Coal India capacity additions, new mine openings under the commercial coal mining programme, and continued investment in coal-based thermal capacity in states where renewable intermittency remains a grid management challenge. CAT's briefing is expected to examine precisely this tension: whether India's energy crisis response is consistent with a credible 1.5°C-aligned pathway or whether it represents a structural detour. CAT has historically rated India's overall climate action as 'Highly Insufficient' at the global level, though it has acknowledged India's renewable energy progress. Any update to this rating - upward or downward - carries immediate signalling value for ESG investors running ISSB IFRS S2-aligned portfolio risk assessments. The Bonn sessions sit within the broader UNFCCC inter-sessional calendar and serve as the technical preparation ground for COP31, scheduled for November 2026 in Brazil. Countries are expected to submit enhanced NDCs ahead of COP31 under the Paris Agreement's ratchet mechanism.
